                            Figure 10: The grievance process for the project
6.3.      Project-Specific Construction-Related Procedures
94.     During construction, the GRM will also be managed by the contractors under supervision of
the CSC. The contractors will inform the affected communities and communes about the GRM
availability to handle complaints and concerns about the project. This will be done via the community
consultation and information disclosure process under which the contractors will communicate with
the affected communities and interested authorities on a regular basis. Meetings will be held at least
quarterly, monthly information brochures will be published, announcements will be placed in local
media, and notices of upcoming planned activities will be posted, etc. All contractors and CSCs will
also appoint grievance focal persons to catch and solve the problem timely.
95.     All complaints and corresponding actions undertaken by the contractors will be recorded in
project safeguard monitoring reports. Complaints and claims for damages could be lodged as follows:

                                                                                                          Page | 57
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

 (i)    Verbally: direct to the CSC and/ or the contractors’ safeguard staff or representatives at the
        site offices.
 (ii)   In writing: by hand, delivering or posting a written complaint to specified addresses.
 (iii) By telephone, fax, e-mails: to the CSC, the contractors’ safeguard staff or representatives
96.      Upon receipt of a complaint, the CSC, the contractors’ safeguard staff or representatives will
register the complaint in a complaint file and maintain a log of events pertaining to it thereafter, until
it is resolved. Immediately after receiving receipt, four copies of the complaint will be prepared. The
original will be kept in the file, one copy will be used by the contractor’s safeguard staff, one copy
will be forwarded to the CSC, and the fourth copy to the APMB within 24 hours since receipt of the
complaint. Information to be recorded in the complaint log will consist of:
    o The date and time of the complaint;
    o The name, address and contact details of the complainant;
    o A short description of the complaint;
    o Actions taken to address the complaint, including contact persons and findings at each step
      in the complaint redress process;
    o The dates and times for the purpose of contact during the redress process;
    o The final resolution of the complaint;
    o The date, time and manner in which the complainant was informed thereof;
    o The complainant’s signature when resolution has been obtained.
97.     Minor complaints will be dealt with within one week. Within two weeks (and weekly
thereafter), a written reply will be delivered to the complainant (by hand, post, fax, e-mails) indicating
the procedures taken and progress to date.
98.     The main objective will be to resolve an issue as quickly as possible by the simplest means,
involving as few people as possible, and at the lowest possible level. Only when an issue cannot be
resolved at the simplest level and/ or within 15 days, will other authorities be involved. Such a
situation may arise, for example, when damages are claimed, the to-be-paid amount cannot be
resolved, or damage causes are determined.
6.4.    Procedures in Cases of Gender-Based Violence (GBV):
99.     To avoid the risk of stigmatization, exacerbation of the mental/psychological harm and
potential reprisal, the GRM shall have a different and sensitive approach to GBV related cases. Where
such a case is reported to the GRM, it should immediately be referred to the appropriate service
providers, such as medical and psychological support, emergency accommodation, and any other
necessary services. It should also be reported to the safeguard staff of the APMB who can advise on
relevant service providers. Data on GBV cases should not be collected through the GRM unless
operators have been trained on the empathetic, non-judgmental and confidential collection of these
complaints. Only the nature of the complaint (what the complainant says in her/his own words) and
additional demographic data, such as age and gender, can be collected as usual. All grievance related
to Gender Based Violence will be filed in a separate document and will be kept secure and
confidential.
6.5.    Procedures in Cases of Labors’ Concerns
100. Project workers can submit their complaints (related to employment opportunities, wages,
payment, working conditions, health, safety, administrative acts and decisions, discipline decisions,
etc.) in writing, or face-to-face verbally – except for complaints against discipline decisions to their

                                                                                                 Page | 58
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

employer/contractor who are responsible for solving first-time complaint. Complainants can also
lodge grievance through the APMB using email, post, or telephone or APMB dedicated channels for
GRM. Complainant can bring their case to the court of law at any stage during the complaint
resolution process (See the project LMP for details).
6.6.   Procedures in Cases of Land Acquisition
101. Complaints concerning land acquisition, physical resettlement, and relevant issues are
expected to be lodged through CPCs. Submission of complaints can be done using emails, postal,
hand delivery, or verbally through direct meetings or telephone. Within 10 days from the date of
receiving the complaint, the person in charge of complaint resolution shall accept the complaint and
notify the complainant in writing. The time limit for complaint resolution – from the date of
acceptance, is 30 days for first-time complaints, and 45 days for the second time and third-time
complaints. The statute of limitation (for initiating a lawsuit) is 30 days from the date the time-limit
for complaint resolution expires, or from the date the complainant receives the complaint resolution
decision. In a remote area where travel is difficult, the statute of limitation can be extended but not
exceeding 45 days. The complainant can also initiate a lawsuit at any stage of the grievance resolution
process if they disagree with the complaint resolution decision, or if the case is not resolved following
the expiry of the time-limit for complaint resolution (see the project RP for details).

7.     MONITORING AND REPORTING
102. During the project implementation phase, the SEP will be periodically revised and updated
as necessary in the course of off-grid solutions project implementations in order to ensure that the
information presented herein is consistent and is the most recent, and that the identified methods of
engagement remain appropriate and effective in relation to the project context and specific phases of
the development. Any major changes to the project-related activities and to its schedule will be duly
reflected in the SEP.
103. Monthly summaries and internal reports on public grievances, enquiries and related incidents,
together with the status of implementation of associated corrective actions will be collated by
responsible staff and referred to the senior management of the project. The monthly summaries will
provide a mechanism for assessing both the number and the nature of complaints and requests for
information, along with the Project’s ability to address those in a timely and satisfactorily manner.
104. Monitoring Indicators. The APMB will monitor the project implementation to assess
progress on indicators to be defined in the results framework, such as roads/canal/sewage system
rehabilitated/built; beneficiary satisfaction on such as the invested items of the project; employment
creation through the civil work; and functional maintenance systems. More specific monitoring
indicators on environmental and social issues, such as noise levels, air quality, water quality, soil
erosion, traffic and other occupational or community accidents and social issues including gender
impact will be proposed in the ESMP. The functioning of the GRM will be also closely monitored.
The APMB will also need to monitor stakeholder engagement itself. This includes data on the number
of meetings (or focus groups), how many people participated, etc.
7.1.   Internal Monitoring
105. The APMB will prepare data on activities and output in regular monthly and quarterly reports
based on input from the supervision consultant and the contractor. To the extent possible, the
monitoring and evaluation process will be participatory, engaging community members benefiting
from the infrastructure investments. A baseline survey and end-line beneficiary survey will be carried
out to measure who and to what extent people benefit from the infrastructure as well as how it affects

                                                                                                Page | 59
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

their lives in both social and economic terms. The Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) system will
target the documentation of case studies capturing the impact of the project on communities, taking
into account gender, impacts on vulnerable or marginalized groups and host communities.
7.2.    Third Party Monitoring
106. An independent consultant will be hired by the APMB to monitor the implementation of E&S
instruments. The overall objective of independent monitoring is to provide a periodic independent
evaluation of the results of implementing the environmental and social risk/impact management
objectives, such as the changes in living standards and employment, income rehabilitation and the
social basis of those affected, effectiveness, impacts and sustainability of entitlements, the need for
additional measures to minimize the damage (if any). The agency will be responsible for carrying out
environmental sampling, monitoring and marking reports during subproject implementation. The
implementation of the independent monitoring mission should be based on the terms of reference
approved by World Bank. The independent monitoring agency will begin its work as soon as the
project implementation begins. Monitoring reports will be periodically submitted to the APMB and
the World Bank (respectively every 03 months for APMB and every 6 months for WB in construction
phase). The agency will also supply specialized assistance to APMB and E&S matters.
7.3.    Community Monitoring
107. As per Decree No. 29/2021/ND-CP dated 26 March 2021 regarding investment supervision
and assessment, local people can establish a community investment supervision board to conduct
environmental, social, and construction safety supervision voluntarily. During project
implementation, communities that benefit from the project will be encouraged to form a local team
by this Decree to monitor and provide feedback on construction-related issues.
7.4.    Reporting back to Stakeholder
108. The project performance assessed through monitoring activities will be reported back to
stakeholders during the operation and maintenance phase, such as through disclosure of monitoring
outcome and engagement local community. The lessons learned through monitoring will also
contribute to the design of future subprojects and be shared with their stakeholders.
109. Monitoring and evaluation of the stakeholder process is considered vital to ensure the project
will be able to respond to identified issues and alter the schedule and nature of engagement activities
to make them more effective. Adherence to the following characteristics/commitments/activities will
assist in achieving successful engagement:
        i. Sufficient resources to undertake the engagement;
       ii. Inclusivity (inclusion of key groups) of interactions with stakeholders;
       iii. Promotion of stakeholder involvement;
       iv. Sense of trust shown by all stakeholders;
       v. Clearly defined approaches; and
       vi. Transparency in all activities.
110. Monitoring of the stakeholder engagement process allows the efficacy of the process to be
evaluated. Specifically, by identifying key performance indicators that reflect the objectives of the
SEP and the specific actions and timings, it is possible to both monitor and evaluate the process
undertaken. Two distinct but related monitoring activities in terms of timing will be implemented:
  i.        During the engagement activities: short-term monitoring to allow for adjustments/
            improvements to be made during engagement; and


                                                                                              Page | 60
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

 ii.    Following completion of all engagement activities: review of outputs at the end of
        engagement to evaluate the effectiveness of the SEP as implemented.

STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)


I.    INTRODUCTION
1.1. Objectives of the project
1.    The overall objective of the project is to strengthen fisheries management and increase product
value through smart infrastructure upgrades.
2.    The project aims to the following specific objectives:
  •   To build and improve infrastructure, innovate technology, strengthen management capacity and
      organize production in order to promote the fisheries sector in the direction of value
      enhancement, deep international integration, environmental friendliness and adaptation to
      climate change.
  •   To encourage and attract all economic sectors to contribute to the sustainable development of
      the province's fishery sector, promote socio-economic development associated with
      environmental protection.

1.2. Components of the project
1.2.1. Structural components
3.    The following are the structural components of the project:
  •   Building new basic infrastructure and upgrading current infrastructure for shrimp farming area
      in the south of NH80, including:
      o   Building 23 sluice gates with a width of 8 to 10 m and its management houses to separate
          fresh and brackish water areas for agricultural restructuring, aquaculture, and rice
          cultivation;
      o   Building a delta grade-V road with a total length of about 7.03 km (running along the right
          bank of Binh Giang 1 canal connecting NH80 to the sea dyke);
      o   Installing 25.18km (including 21.54km medium-voltage power lines, 3.64km low-voltage
          power lines), and 30 transformer stations to operate sluices;
      o   Upgrading the gates of 5 sluices along the sea dike, and 13 in-field sluices along the Don
          Dong canal for the current aquaculture area;
      o   Other ancillary works.
  •   Technological innovation, increased management capacity, and improved production
      organization in brackish shrimp farming:
      o   Livelihood activities (shrimp - rice; giant freshwater shrimp; other aquatic species).
      o   Engagement of the private sector (companies, individuals, etc.) in investing in livelihood
          models.
4.    The layout of project items is shown in Figure 11.




                                                                                             1|Page
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)




                                     The project’proposed road
                                     The project’s proposed medium voltage lines
                                     The project’s proposed sluices
                                     Existing sluices


Figure 11: Layout of project items

                                                                                   2|Page
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

5.         Technical specifications of the project's work items are presented below:
Sluices
6.    In the frame of the project, 23 sluices will be built. In addition, 4 level-IV management houses
area built on the right bank of 4 sluices (K3-6, K3-7, K9-1 and K9-2) toward the field. It has a usable
area of 40m2, including an office, dining rooms and bedrooms.
Connecting road
7.    The project’s road has a length of 7.03km and 36 culverts with a diameter of D600 and a length
of 20m along the road. The culverts are rebuilt to compensate for local people on the road to take
water Binh Giang 1 canal into the rice fields.
8.    On the road, 36 new water intake culverts will be installed at the existing culverts. These
provide water from Binh Giang 1 canal to the fields. The culverts are made of centrifugal concrete
with a diameter D=0.6m and load-bearing of H30-XB80. The culverts are placed on abutments
reinforced with Melaleuca piles with a density of 16 piles/m2.
Medium and low voltage lines
9.         Specification of medium and low voltage lines is show in Table 17.

1.2.2.        Non-structural activities
13.          Non-structural solutions implemented in the frame of the project:
  •      Establish and strengthen production cooperation groups, including:
         o    Farmers’ groups to manage their members’ activities in the direction of green agro products.
         o    Cooperatives to negotiate the formation of production chains, and operate the chains
              effectively via the application of national and international standards into the production.
  •      Disseminate the activities of the project to build consensus and support from the people, reduce
         the rate of people utilizing groundwater in agricultural production, and enhance people's
         understanding of production linked with natural resource protection and regeneration,
         including:



                                                                                                           4|Page
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

     o   Workshops, seminars, and propaganda panels to share experiences and communication of
         the project in 02 project communes.
     o   Propaganda on mass media about the project.
 •   Develop demonstration livelihood models.
 •   Conduct training classes, transfer production techniques to improve capacity, and apply
     scientific and technical advances to meet market needs and reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
 •   Connect and replicate the project livelihood models, introduce and register product offtake
     businesses to help farmers apply sustainable and effective livelihood models that mee the needs
     of product offtake businesses and reduce production costs by the procurement of substantial
     quantities of goods from retailers who supply goods of reputable origin and quality, including:
     o   At least 2 value chains (1 chain for one commune), with signed product offtake contracts
         for rice output certified green standards such as VietGAP, SRP, Organic, ASC, BAP.
     o   At least 2 value chains (1 chain for one commune), with signed product offtake contracts
         for rice output certified green standards such as VietGAP, SRP, Organic, ASC, BAP.
 •   Support training, consultation, and certification of national and international standards
     VietGAP, SRP, Organic, ASC, BAP) requested by the businesses of the value chains.
 •   Support the application of smart technology to adapt to climate change in production and apply
     technology in production to minimize chemical usage and carbon emissions.
 •   Building electronic traceability - QR code for 04 product chains.
 •   Evaluate the effectiveness of project livelihood models.

1.3. Project Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
1.3.1.  Positive impacts
14. The project's benefits are long-term rather than short-term. If successful, the project should not
be limited to increasing the number of aquaculture farmers and service providers for shrimp farming
activities, but should also contribute to the national sustainable fishery development, specifically:
 •   The project items assist An Giang province in resolving its urgent problems, such as preventing
     saline intrusion in coastal areas and regulating water for stable cultivation, etc. This contributes
     to adapting to climate change, protecting and improving people's lives, and creating a
     sustainable development environment for the locality.
 •   The 23 sluices of the project, together with existing sluices, will improve the ability to control
     salinity, and tides, adapt to climate change, and sea level rise, and separate salty and freshwater
     boundaries. The project will also ensure flood drainage, acid sulfate washing for LXQ, provide
     salt/fresh water, delineate the freshwater-saline water interface in the restructuring area, and
     regulate water sources for agriculture, fisheries, daily life, and rural environmental sanitation
     during the dry season. Furthermore, the project promotes rural development and socioeconomic
     integration by regulating water use demand for shrimp farming and contributing to disease
     control for an area of 5,500 ha of aquaculture, effectively changing the structure of agricultural
     land to the south of NH 80.
 •   The project roads that connect NH 80 to the West Sea dike will make it easier for farmers to
     transport breeds, food, equipment, shrimp, and other seafood outputs. The production costs for
     the residents will decrease as they no longer need to rely on manual or semi-automatic vehicles.
     This will contribute to the enhancement of seafood quality by reducing transportation time.


                                                                                               5|Page
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

  •      The project's medium and low-voltage power system will contribute to addressing the
         longstanding issue of electricity shortages in the aquaculture area. Shrimp farmers will use
         electric-powered machines and equipment for shrimp farming, which is both efficient and
         economical, while also reducing environmental pollution from diesel use (a common practice
         when facing power shortages). The aquaculture production costs will decrease by not using
         diesel fuel, leading to increased income and improved livelihoods for the farmers.
  •      When the project is finished, the shrimp farming area is expected to shift to an industrial and
         semi-industrial farming model with better extended agricultural procedures. This change is
         projected to boost shrimp output, improving the livelihoods of the local community.
  •      In addition to the direct impacts on shrimp farming development, the project is expected to
         attract investments from private enterprises for upgrading and constructing new infrastructure
         for industrial shrimp farming facilities.
  •      Farmers will be able to access aquaculture facilities with enhanced sanitary and environmental
         conditions, which reduces occupational health risks, improves the quality and value of shrimp
         production, and reduces shrimp losses due to shorter transportation.
  •      Improved waste management in shrimp farms will allow for less wastewater and sediment
         discharge into water bodies. As a result, in the long run, the project should help to improve the
         water quality in the area.
  •      By implementing Integrated Pest Management (IPM) and Good Agriculture Practices (GAP),
         as well as introducing bio-security practices and disease-free seeds, ponds can be rendered less
         susceptible to pests and diseases. This would result in a decreased reliance on antibiotics and
         chemicals in aquaculture, safeguarding the environment, mitigating losses caused by disease
         outbreaks, improving food security, and enhancing occupational health and safety (OHS) for
         farming-based laborers.
  •      Improved quality of shrimp seed and increased productivity of shrimp farming areas will help
         farmers expand their shrimp farming by adapting to the challenges of rising sea levels.
  •      Enhanced productivity of shrimp farming areas and improved quality of shrimp seed will enable
         farmers to expand their shrimp farming operations in response to challenges of sea level rising
         (SLR).
  •      Bio-security, good nutrition, and a reasonable cost are the primary elements for exporting
         shrimps internationally with good international competition.
  •      The project would also generate environmental and social benefits through a variety of other
         mechanisms. These include (i) beneficiaries, local communities, and authorities becoming more
         aware of and concerned about environmental and social issues; and (ii) more PPMU staff and
         aquaculture extension staff of the Department of Agriculture and Environment being trained in
         environmental and social screening. This helps with bringing up environmental and social
         problems during the planning and execution of the project.
1.3.2.      Negative impacts and risks
15. During preparation phase, the negative potential impacts and risks include (i) impacts due to
land acquisition. About 15.47 ha of land will be used for the project, but only 2.72ha will be acquired
from 57 households, in which 12 households will be relocated; (ii) impacts on livelihood; (iii) land
use change; (iv) impacts due to UXOs. Most impacts are temporary, local and reversible due to
medium scale construction. These impacts and risks can be mitigated by applying the approved
resettlement plan (RP) of the project and the project owner will contract with relevant unit to clear


                                                                                                6|Page
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

UXOs before commencing construction activities, and must closely monitor and consult with local
people about the land acquisition and clearance and UXO clearance.
16. During construction phase, dust, exhaust gas, noise, vibration; wastewater; solid waste;
ecosystem; labor influx; improper access, hard-working conditions, health and safety of workers and
locals; local flooding; local traffic; land subsidence, soil erosion; raw material supply, etc. are generic
impacts and risks of the project. These impacts can be considered as low to moderate for each item
and can be minimized by adhering to Environmental and Social Code of Practices (ESCOPs). In
addition, the project implementation causes specific impacts and risks, including impacts on domestic
water supply, impacts on livelihoods, impacts on water traffic, impacts by the relocation of
infrastructure, impacts on ethnic minorities (EMs) and impacts on sensitive receptors, etc. These
impacts and risks can be mitigated by specific measures including consultation with local authorities
about the project activities and construction plan, the arrangement of a temporary road, construction
in the dry season and the offseason for water taking; informing people of construction time; not using
high-noise machinery and equipment that emit a lot of dust and noise through the sensitive areas
during major religious holidays/study time; spraying sufficient water to reduce dust during dry and
windy days; providing safety measures as installation of fences, barriers warning signs, lighting
system against traffic accidents; prohibiting gathering of construction materials within 100m in front
of the sensitive objects; cleaning up construction areas at the end of the day, especially construction
around the sensitive structures; immediately addressing any issue/problem caused by the construction
activities; reinstating and providing adequate compensation for any impacts which will be identified
as caused by the construction activities.
17. During operations phase, the generic impacts and risks are dust, air emmision; noise and
vibration; domestic wastewater and solid waste; increase of traffic accidents; land use change; and
damage to traffic roads on the bridges over the sluices. The generic impacts and risks can be mitigated
by training and raising the awareness of the road and bridge’s users, arranging suitable speed limit
signs in the crowed areas; planting and maintaining trees on both road sides to prevent noise from
spreading into local people’s houses along the road; regular maintenance of the project items,
preparing an Emergency Contingency Plan; etc. In addition, the project operation cause specific
impacts and risks, including impacts on water drainage; risks of the road damage; impacts due to
electromagnetic field; degradation of surface water quality; impacts of saline and/or brackish water
on construction works; damage to sluice gates; increase in water use conflict; impacts on aquatic life;
impacts on waterway where the sluices are built; impacts on flood drainage; canal bank erosion; risk
that failure of livelihood models adversely impacting welfare of beneficiary households; farmers’
resistance to adapting to livelihood models; and Risk of land salinization. The impacts and risks are
mitigated by specific measures including communicate, raise local awareness of environmental
protection, especially water quality in canals; consulting local people and authorities about the
operational procedure for the sluice gate; early notify local people and authorities regarding sluice
gate operation (opening and closing times); conduct water/ecology monitoring at the monitoring sites
to provide baseline data prior to sluice gate operations, no later than 1 month before completion of
construction; collect packages, excessive foods during the argicultural and aquacultural production;
prohibit littering in surrounding area or water sources; etc.




                                                                                                 7|Page
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

II. OBJECTIVES OF THE STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN
18. The objective of this stakeholder engagement plan (SEP) is to define a program for stakeholder
engagement, including public information disclosure, grievance redress mechanism (GRM), and
consultation, throughout the implementation and operation of the project. It specifies how the An
Giang PPMU and contractors will communicate with stakeholders, as well as a mechanism for people
to express concerns, provide feedback, or file complaints about the project, and contractors.
Consultation and engagement with stakeholders are critical to ensure smooth collaboration between
the project and local communities, as well as to minimize and mitigate environmental and social risks
and impacts of the project, thereby contributing to the project's success.
19. The project stakeholders will engage at different stages, including during the initial design,
detailed design, pre-construction, construction, and operation. Engagement will vary during the life
of the project and this SEP will be dynamic and flexible to these changes. The SEP should be read
together with other project documents such as ESIA/ESMP, LMP, RP, EMDP, and ESCP. This SEP
is a living document that will be updated as consultations take place, incorporating minutes of
meetings and relevant comments from project stakeholders.

III. STAKEHOLDER IDENTIFICATION AND ANALYSIS
20. Project stakeholders are people and entities who have a role in the project, or could be affected
by the project, or who are interested in the project. Project stakeholders are defined as individuals,
groups or other entities who:
 (i)    are impacted or likely to be impacted directly or indirectly, positively or adversely, by the
        project (also known as “affected parties”); and
 (ii)   may have an interest in the project (“interested parties”). They include individuals or groups
        whose interests may be affected by the project and who have the potential to influence the
        project outcomes in any way.
21. Cooperation and negotiation with the stakeholders throughout the project development often
also require the identification of people within the groups who act as legitimate representatives of
their respective stakeholder group, i.e., the individuals who have been entrusted by their fellow group
members with advocating the groups’ interests in the process of engagement with the project.
Community representatives may provide helpful insight into the local settings and act as main
conduits for dissemination of the project-related information and as a primary communication/liaison
link between the project and targeted communities and their established networks. Verification of
stakeholder representatives (i.e., the process of confirming that they are legitimate and genuine
advocates of the community they represent) remains an important task in establishing contact with
the community stakeholders. Legitimacy of the community representatives can be verified by talking
informally to a random sample of community members and heeding their views on who can be
representing their interests in the most effective way.

3.1. Methodology
22. To meet best practice approaches, the project will apply the following principles for stakeholder
engagement:




                                                                                             8|Page
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

  •    Openness and life-cycle approach: public consultations for the project will be arranged during
       the whole lifecycle, carried out openly, free of external manipulation, interference, coercion, or
       intimidation.
  •    Informed participation and feedback: information will be provided to and widely distributed
       among all stakeholders in an appropriate format; opportunities are provided for communicating
       stakeholders’ feedback, and for analyzing and addressing comments and concerns.
  •    Inclusiveness and sensitivity: stakeholder identification will be undertaken to support better
       communications and build effective relationships. The participation process for the project will
       be inclusive. All stakeholders at all times will be encouraged to get involved in the consultation
       process. Equal access to information will be provided to all stakeholders. Sensitivity to
       stakeholders’ needs is the key principle underlying the selection of engagement methods.
       Special attention will be given to vulnerable groups, in particular women, displaced persons,
       persons with disabilities, youth, elderly.

3.2.   Stakeholder Identification
23. The stakeholder mapping for the project demonstrates that focus is placed on the core project
stakeholders and beneficiaries as well as host communities of the project. Therefore, the project
coordinates endeavors via facilitator/consultant teams to prompt and comprehensive involvement of
these groups (

Figure 13. Stakeholders’ influence and interest analysis
26. Stakeholders of the project are people or groups who are directly or indirectly affected by the
project, as well as those who may have an interest in the project and/or the ability to influence project
outcomes, either positively or negatively. For effective and tailored engagement, the project’s
stakeholders can be divided into the following 3 core categories:

3.3. Affected Parties
27. One of the primary goals of the project is to identify stakeholders on which the project can have
negative impact such as households or businesses who may be impacted by land acquisition,
livelihoods and business activities. However, the project may influence the activities of various
stakeholders in positive or negative ways. Thus, a list of key stakeholder groups can be identified as
potentially affected parties. These should be engaged throughout the life of the project and impacts
on them should be monitored and mitigated. Project-affected parties include those likely to be
affected by the project because of actual impacts or potential risks to their physical environment,
health, security, cultural practices, well-being, or livelihoods. The following groups can be identified
as potential project-affected stakeholders:
28. People who are physically or economically displaced by the project: The main groups of
project-affected parties in the stakeholder mapping are the people physically or economically
displaced by the project, and persons who reside or have land and non-land assets in the Right of
Way (ROW) of the proposed investments. The following parties are identified as directly affected:
 •   People physically or economically displaced and people who reside or have land and non-land
     assets in the ROW of the proposed investments:

                                                                                              11 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

     o People and companies who will lose their land.
     o People and businesses with structures on land that will be relocated.
     o People and companies with trees and crops that will be affected.
     o People and companies with surface water (aquaculture) that will be affected.
 •   Fishermen/fishing vessel owners.
 •   Woman (including women-headed households with dependents, female members of the
     affected households and companies).
 •   Other vulnerable households such as poor/near-poor households; landless households; persons
     with physical or mental disabilities (loss of working ability); people living alone, elderly; and
     social policy category (families of martyrs, war invalids, and sick soldiers, etc.).
 •   People who will have access to water from dredged canals for aquaculture.
 •   People who will have access to the roads and bridges for their travelling and the movement of
     their commodities.
29. Communities in the vicinity of the project areas: Local community and companies who are
likely to be exposed to temporary air pollution, traffic difficulties, and other potential risks such as
traffic safety, traffic disturbance, wastewater discharge, gender-based violence (GBV), sexual
exploitation and abuse (SEA)/Sexual harassment (SH), health risks as a result of labour influx, etc.
30. Executing/implementing agencies, relevant province’s agencies, and administrative
authorities at all levels: These groups include An Giang Provincial People’s Committee (An Giang
PPC), An Giang PPMU, Department of Finance (DOF), Department of Construction (DOC),
Department of Agriculture and Environment (DAE), Department of Ethnic and Religious Affairs
(DOERA), Department of Home Affairs (DOHA), Department of Science and Technology (DOST),
An Giang Province Site Clearance and Compensation Committee (SCCC); An Giang Province Land
Fund Development Center (LFDC), 2 Commune People’s Committees (CPCs) in the project area,
and public service utilities (such as telecommunication, electric power, water supply, wastewater,
traffic management).
31. Project beneficiaries: Project beneficiaries are large in number (they are positively affected
by the project). These include individuals, households, groups, public and private sectors who benefit
from the construction of roads, bridges, dredged canals, storm shelter.
32. Table 20 indicates potential project-affected parties of the project.

3.4. Other interested parties
33. The project’s “other interested parties” refer to individuals, groups, or organization with an
interest in the project, due to either the project location, its characteristics, its impacts, or matters
related to public interest. The following stakeholders are listed as project interested parties:
 •    Central level: Ministries and government agencies include the Office of Government (OOG)
      and interest line-ministries, those are playing a key role in their functions and responsibilities
      and sectoral management, such as the Ministry of Agriculture and Environment (MAE),
      Ministry of Finance (MOF), Ministry of Construction (MOC), Ministry of Home Affairs
      (MOHA), Ministry of Ethnic and Religious Affairs (MOERA), Ministry of Health (MOH),
      Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST).
 •    Provincial level: An Giang PPC, An Giang PPMU, DOF, DOC, DAE, DOHA, DOERA, DOH,
      DOST, Provincial LFDC, Provincial Site Clearance and Compensation Committee (SCCC).
 •    Commune level: 2 project CPCs.
 •    Mass organizations include 6 organizations at all levels as Women’s Union, Youth Union,
      Fatherland Front, Farmers’ Union, Veterans Associations, and Trade Union.
 •    Mass media include local, regional and national news agencies (TV, broadcasting, online
      newspaper) who may be interested in project activities, project progress, and impact on local
      development.
 •    Commune citizen groups benefit from additional income thanks to providing services to
      construction workers (e.g., foods and other essential supplies, etc.). Those who may become
      involved in construction and operation as seasonal workers are also those who may be interested
      in the project, and those who are interested in supervision of the construction of the project
      components.
 •    Development agencies and/or NGOs: Currently, there are no NGOs working in Project
      communes. However, NGOs become interested in the project during its implementation, this
      SEP will be updated to reflect their concerns (if any).
 •    Public service utilities, including telecommunication, electric power, water supply,
      wastewater treatment, waste collection, waterways traffic management, commune health
      centers, etc.
 •    Experts, universities, institutes, including Research Institute for Aquaculture 2
 •    Private companies: Service providers for Gender-based violation (GBV)/ Sexual exploitation
      and abuse (SEA)/ Sexual harassment (SH), other service providers (tourist centers, hotels,
      motels, etc.) for contracted workers.
34.   Potential project-affected parties of the project are shown in Table 21.

3.5. Disadvantaged/Vulnerable Individuals and Groups
35. Disadvantaged/vulnerable individuals and groups may be affected disproportionately - either
adversely or positively, by the project. Due to low socioeconomic profile, their voices may not be
heard in the formal decision-making process or included in consultation process, but their feedback
are not solicited in a manner that offers sufficient understanding of how they are affected, how they
would respond, and what support they may need to avoid or mitigate such impact – from their own
perspective. Identification of vulnerable individuals and groups, including those who are stigmatized,
is an important step to effectively engaging them in the consultation throughout the project cycle.
This enables An Giang PPMU to work out measures to avoid adverse impact, or minimize or mitigate
such impact, if avoidance is not possible. Inclusion of disadvantaged/vulnerable individuals and
groups - at the early stage of project planning, can also assist in project design that provide them
opportunities to restore and improve their livelihoods.
36. The project needs to make special efforts to ensure vulnerable and disadvantaged stakeholders
can be engaged. In this project, these groups include:
 • Ethnic minorities (EMs).
 • Elderly people and/or social policy householders (invalid soldiers, martyrs, heroic mothers).
 • Poor/near-poor families.
 • People with disabilities and their caretakers.
 • Women, particularly in women-headed households or single mothers with underage/ dependent
   children.
 • Unemployed people.
37. During project preparation, identified disadvantaged/vulnerable groups have been invited to
consultation to solicit their feedbacks on project impact and risks, and their proposed mitigation
measures. During project implementation, based on the nature and the magnitude of impact,
consultations will be continued to solicit their opinion, particularly prior to construction when project
impact become known and the affected vulnerable have sufficient time to better understand the
project impact and base on that provide meaningful feedback. Table 22 indicates disadvantaged or
vulnerable groups encountered under the project.
Table 22: List of Disadvantaged/Vulnerable Groups of the Project

38. As mentioned above, there is a wide range of project stakeholders that are affected by and are
interested in project implementation. Different stakeholders have different levels of interest and
influence on project design and implementation. Therefore, it is important to understand: (i) the level
of interest of each stakeholder as to the project’s investment activities; (ii) the impact magnitude of
the project on them, particularly those adversely affected, and (iii) the extent to which the stakeholder
influences the project design, as well as implementation process and outcome.
39. Under this project, stakeholders highly relevant to the project preparation and implementation
include governmental agencies and the community at large. These stakeholders are affected by the
project and at the same time can influence project design and implementation - to various extents.
Their interest in project investments also varies at different stages of the project cycle, and as such
exert different levels of influence on the project design and implementation process.
40. Based on the roles, responsibilities, and the potential interest of the key stakeholders obtained
from initial consultations with them, Table 23 summarizes the estimated levels of interest, impact,
and influence of each stakeholder group, of which:
 (i)     “impact” means the project's impacts (both positive and negative impacts) on each key group
         of the stakeholder during phases of the project.
 (ii)    “influence” means people’s influence on the project implementation.
 (iii) Level of “impact” and “influence” will be assessed on 3 different levels: High; Moderate;
       and Low.
41. It is noted that these levels of interest, impact, and influence may change throughout the project.
However, the current dynamics of stakeholders’ interest, impact, and influence, as described in Table
23, are useful for informing how each stakeholder should be engaged - in a meaningful manner, to
ensure they contribute to the overall environmental and social performance process, and outcome of
the project.

STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)


IV. STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PROGRAM
42. In order to achieve the objectives of the SEP, the project will develop an engagement program
with the following purposes:
 • Provide meaningful information in a format and language that is readily understandable.
 • Provide information in advance of consultation activities when possible.
 • Disseminate information in a manner and location easy for stakeholders to access.
 • Establish a two-way dialogue that allows the project and stakeholders to exchange views and
   information, and have issues heard and addressed.
 • Ensure inclusiveness in the representation of views, including those of women, the elderly,
   people living with a disability, and other vulnerable people as necessary.
 • Ensure any obstacles to participation that are identified are removed so that views of different
   stakeholders can be captured.
 • Ensure there are clear mechanisms for responding to people’s concerns, suggestions, and/or
   grievances.
 • Incorporate feedback into the project, and report back to stakeholders.
 • Monitor stakeholder engagement activities and include project stakeholders in monitoring to the
   extent possible.
 • Incorporate stakeholder engagement as part of the project management responsibilities and
   ensure staff in the PPMU and its consultants are equipped with specific responsibilities and
   budget.

4.1. Summary of Stakeholder Engagement during Project Preparation
4.1.1. During the preparation of the project Stakeholder Engagement Framework
43. Numerous meetings were held by the Central Project Management Board (CPMU) at 12 project
provinces in May and June 2020, as part of the stakeholder engagement process for preparation of
the Sustainable Fisheries Development Project. The consultation meeting with Kien Giang (now An
Giang) province was held on June 09, 2020.
44. The purpose of the consultation was to familiarize stakeholders with the project's scope and to
determine acceptable communication and consultation procedures throughout the project lifetime.
Participants were given general information about the project (objectives, components, project
footprint, potential risks and impacts, financing source, implementation arrangement) during the
consultation. The new applicable World Bank ESF, as well as its E&S standards and criteria, were
also introduced to the participants. The outcomes of previous consultations have been incorporated
into the overall stakeholder engagement process. This stakeholder engagement plan reflects and takes
into account all relevant feedback from those consultations.
4.1.2. During the preparation of SEP
45. During the preparation of the SEP for the Kien Giang Sustainable Fisheries Development
Project, many consultation meetings have been conducted at community and provincial levels, see
detail in Table 9.

4.2. Summary of Key Project Stakeholders’ Needs
46. Various stakeholders require different methods of communication and consultation. For local
government officials, since a well-established communication protocol has been in place, the
communications with them to arrange consultation sessions are through means such as couriers,
electronic mails, telephone conversations, face-to-face meetings, etc. For community,
communication is typically through courier at village level or paying a home visit to those who are
better approached through direct contact, such as people with disabilities, the elderly, etc.
47. Table 26 summarizes the stakeholder's needs for consultation in the project and proposed
methods to accommodate the needs of different stakeholders.




                                                                                        33 | P a g e

4.3. Strategy for Consultation
48. The project will apply a participatory and consultative approach involving meaningful
engagement with different stakeholders. Project consultations will be a two-way process of dialogue
between the project and its stakeholders, including on issues such as project objectives; working
conditions for contractors; engagement of women; ways to mitigate potential project impacts, and
risks in particular to vulnerable groups. This will depend on the stakeholders as well as the project
timing. For this project, consultation strategies will be proposed for each stage of the project, details
are as follows:

4.4. Consultation strategies
49. Project preparation (before project appraisal). Since the details of some proposed project
investments are unknown at this stage, consultations during project preparation will focus on a
broader range of stakeholders to understand their general interests and concerns about the project and
its potential positive and negative impacts and inform project design. This is also the stage where
potential vulnerable/disadvantaged groups relevant to the project will be identified.
50. Project implementation. At this stage, the details of proposed project components and specific
project-affected stakeholders will be determined. The consultation will focus more specifically on
people who are impacted by the project, especially those adversely impacted, to address their
concerns through the implementation of project-specific E&S plans. In addition, the project will
ensure that the GRM is disseminated and active to promptly address any grievances. Special attention
will be paid to vulnerable/disadvantaged groups to ensure inclusion, non-discrimination, and access
to project benefits. Consultations will also focus on job opportunities, specific action plans, etc.
51. Project operation. At this stage, the consultation will focus on feedback from stakeholders,
particularly affected ones, on the performance of E&S impact and risk mitigations, results of
grievance resolution conducted during the implementation stage as well as general project operations,
including implementation of the storm shelter operation. The consultation on employment
opportunities for operation and maintenance (O&M) of the project works will be also conducted.
Also, areas for improvement and lessons learned should be identified for the project. Furthermore,
dissemination and reporting back to stakeholders on the outcome of the monitoring and evaluation
of the project will be also arranged.

4.5. Consultation methods
52. The methods used to conduct consultations will have a direct impact on the outcome of the
project consultation strategy. Therefore, these methods should be designed to account for the diverse
characteristics of various stakeholder groups. The following consultation methods will be used for
this project:
53. National consultations. National consultations are particularly useful to target government
representatives, NGOs, and other groups who have an interest in the project and may also have the
ability to influence it. These types of consultations are also useful at the project design stage, and
annually to inform stakeholders of a project’s progress. National consultations may be conducted
face-to-face or virtual in a meeting format and may involve break-out groups or other methods to
ensure there is ample time for group discussion and questions and answers.
54. Local consultations. Local consultations are the main way to reach project affected
stakeholders and will involve face-to-face group meetings. In some cases, one-on-one consultations

                                                                                              36 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

or FGD may also be carried out, especially to reach and capture the views of vulnerable or
disadvantaged groups. Emphasis should be placed on seeking out the voice of women and vulnerable
groups, such as through female only FGDs. In the case of women, the project will ensure consultation
activities are conducted at a time that is convenient to them and that activities such as FGDs are run
by women if possible (particularly for sensitive topics like health or sexual issues).
55. One important way to engage stakeholders will be through training on important topics related
to the project such as gender, the project’s GRM, labor rights (for both men and women), child labor,
HIV/AIDS and STDs, gender-based violence, sexual harassment, and sexual exploitation and abuse.
Since some of these topics are quite sensitive, particular measures may need to be in place, such as
ensuring some training courses are done in female-only or male-only groups, or that they are
facilitated by men or women, as appropriate. Training may be delivered by An Giang PPMU/its
consultants.
56. Accessibility issues will also be taken into account, in particular, to ensure people with a
disability can be part of consultations, as well as the specific needs of other groups such as the elderly,
and poor groups.
57. Consultations with project workers. The project will strive to ensure that unskilled workers
come from the community as much as possible, to minimize the influx of labor. Consultations will
encourage local workers to apply for available jobs, including EMs, women and vulnerable or
disadvantaged groups. Contractors and their staff will need to be well consulted and trained in
sensitive issues, including respectful behavior, Codes of Conduct, GBV (including SEA and SH),
HIV/AIDS and STDs, and worker’s GRM, LMP. They also need to be well aware of construction
needs and schedules. Training, on-site interviews, and regular feedback with An Giang PPMU and/or
CSC will be used as methods of consultation with them.
58.   Consultation activities proposed for the project are summarized in Table 27.

4.6. Stakeholder Engagement Methods to be Used in the Next Stages
60. Public/community meetings. The An Giang PPMU will organize the meetings in each project
commune with leaders, PAPs, and other interested parties. Meetings will be open house events where
the PPMU will present all project information and people will be invited to make comments and
express any concerns. A range of consultations with different stakeholders will be conducted and
documented, and community level meetings will also be conducted with men and women (including
vulnerable households) to better understand needs, expectations, and concerns of these population
groups in relation to the project. The feedback received at these meetings will be documented along
with measures that the project will take to address the feedback received. Meetings in each commune
will also be organized on a quarterly basis.
61. Communication materials. Written information will be disclosed to the public via a variety
of communication materials including leaflets, posters, etc. The PPMU will also update its website
regularly (at least on a quarterly basis) with key project updates and reports on the project’s
performance in Vietnamese. The website will also provide information about the grievance
mechanism for the project.
62. Training, workshops. Training in a variety of E&S issues will be provided to the PPMU, and
possibly other relevant government or non-government service providers. Issues covered will include
sensitization to inclusion/exclusion, labor issues, gender-based violence risks.
63. Grievance Redress Mechanism. In compliance with the World Bank’s ESS10, a project-
specific grievance mechanism will be maintained to handle complaints and issues. This will be
integrated into a country system on GRM that is available to citizens. Dedicated communication
materials (specifically, a GRM brochure) will be developed to help local residents become familiar
with the grievance redress channels and procedures. The PPMU will coordinate with administrative
authorities to maintain a grievance register in order to capture and track grievances from submission
to resolution and communication with complainants.
64. The initial effort to resolve grievances to the complainant’s satisfaction will be undertaken by
administrative levels. These will be details in Section 7 below. Grievances may be submitted
anonymously, but in that case, it cannot be known if the complainant is satisfied with the resolution.
The PPMU’s website, posters, and leaflets will include clear information on how feedback, questions,
comments, concerns, and grievances can be submitted by any stakeholder. It will also provide
information on the way grievances will be handled, both in terms of process and deadlines.

4.7. Strategy to Incorporate the Views of Vulnerable Groups
65. In the project, vulnerable and disadvantaged groups may include, but not limited to, the
following groups: (i) EMs, (ii) Elderly and/or social policy households (injured ex-soldier, heroic
mothers); (iii) Poor/near-poor households; (iv) People with disabilities and their careers; and (v)
Women, in particular female-headed households, or single mothers with young children. However,
these groups in the project-affected communities will be further identified and consulted throughout
the project cycle. They may have a limited understanding of project information or participation in
the consultation process.
66. The views of vulnerable/disadvantaged groups will be sought during consultation process.
Accommodation and specific measures may be taken to remove obstacles to their participation, such
as:

                                                                                           41 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

 •   Providing large print,
 •   Choosing accessible venues that are easy to access (such as with ramps),
 •   Providing transportation for people in remote areas to the nearest meeting,
 •   Having small, focused meetings where vulnerable stakeholders are more comfortable asking
     questions or raising concerns,
 •   Good facilitation to ensure meaningful participation in the consultation,
 •   Using appropriate translators.
 •   Respecting traditions and religious practices, such as Theravada Buddhism and local festivals
     like Chol Chnam Thmay and Ok Om Bok.
 •   Involving community leaders and Buddhist monks, as they hold significant influence and trust
     within Khmer communities.
 •   Using Khmer language or bilingual communication for clarity and inclusion.
 •   Leveraging pagodas as community hubs for meetings, information sharing, and building trust.
 •   Incorporating visual tools like diagrams and videos to account for varying literacy levels.
 •   Facilitating participatory activities, such as small-group discussions, storytelling, or hands-on
     demonstrations, in informal settings.
 •   Acknowledging cultural sensitivities, such as gender roles, by ensuring women and other
     groups are included in discussions.
 •   Celebrating milestones with local traditions, such as Khmer music, dance, or community
     events, to strengthen collaboration and trust.
67. Also, GRM will be disclosed to the identified vulnerable groups based on their own
characteristics. For instance, information on the project, project risks and effects, and grievance
procedures will be delivered at home to people with disabilities and verbally to those with impaired
vision. A leaflet containing project information and GRM, as well as contact details, will be
distributed to vulnerable groups, allowing them to contact directly or ask someone else (e.g., a
caregiver) to contact them on their behalf.
68. Since vulnerable groups have been identified and consulted during project preparation, they
will be consulted again during the next stage of the project based on their specific requirements. For
instance, people using wheelchair may ask for the road sideway to have a ramp for wheelchair access,
or request contractors to stockpile construction materials in a manner that allows access for people
having difficulties moving or children for safety.

5.2. Implementation Arrangements
73. The SEP will be the responsibility of the Director and ESO of An Giang PPMU. They will
guide the process of stakeholder engagement throughout the preparation and implementation of the
project. Certain parts of the SEP will also be the responsibility of contractors or consultants, and the
ESO of An Giang PPMU will oversee this work as appropriate. The ESO under the guidance of the
Director will be responsible for:
 •     Leading, or supervising consultations, as per the SEP.
 •     Leading, or supervising the disclosure of information, as per the SEP.
 •     Recording and reporting on grievance resolution, as per the SEP.
 •     Reviewing monthly monitoring reports provided by contractors and/or consultants.
 •     Regularly reporting to the Director.
74. For effective implementation, the PPMU needs to engage various stakeholders identified in
Section 3. However, the participation of governmental agencies, such as PCs and their functional
agencies at 2 levels (provincial, and commune), and the community in the project area, are key to
effective SEP implementation (Table 32).
