VI. GRIEVANCE REDRESS MECHANISM
75. The grievance mechanism seeks to resolve concerns promptly, using an understandable process
that is culturally appropriate and readily accessible at no cost. Grievances can be submitted if
someone believes the project is having a detrimental impact on the community, the environment, or
their quality of life. Stakeholders may also submit their comments and suggestions on the project.
76. The An Giang PPMU’s ESO will be responsible for overseeing those grievances received and
resolved in a fair, objective, and constructive manner all concerns or complaints raised by project
stakeholders. The key principles of the grievance mechanism are to ensure that:
 •    The basic rights and interests of affected people are protected.
 •    The concerns of affected people arising from the project implementation process are adequately
      addressed.
 •    Entitlements or livelihood support for affected people are provided on time and in accordance
      with project documents, such as ESMP, RP.
 •    Affected people are aware of their rights to access grievance procedures free of charge for the
      above purposes.
77. Based on the SEP, wherever possible, the project team shall seek to resolve complaints as soon
as possible and thus avoid the escalation of issues. GRM shall not impede access to the country’s
judicial or administrative remedies. Stakeholders can approach a court of law at any time and
independent of the project-level grievance redress process. Along with the World Bank requirements
based on ESS10, the GRM will be in line with the Vietnamese national legislation, in particular the
Law on Complaints (2011) and the Law on Denunciation (2018).
78. The An Giang PPMU shall establish the GRM. The PPMU shall appoint a GRM focal point to
collaborate with local authorities in handling project-related grievances. The design of the GRM shall
take into consideration the views of affected stakeholders and what is considered an access
mechanism. The PPMU shall be required to disclose and explain to all stakeholders, in particular,
affected stakeholders and vulnerable and disadvantaged ones, the procedure for complaints filing as
early as possible and regularly throughout the project cycle. The grievance mechanism shall be
available at no cost, and it is important that it be easily accessible, with special attention paid to
accessibility for disadvantaged and vulnerable individuals or groups.
79. The PPMU will ensure that grievances are registered, tracked, and resolved in line with
stipulated timeframes.
80.   The GRM officer will be responsible for:

                                                                                                         49 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

 •   Developing and publicizing grievance management procedures.
 •   Receiving, reviewing, and keeping track of grievances (as applicable).
 •   Ensuring grievances are settled within the agreed time periods.
81. Stakeholders will be consulted about the GRM during the stakeholder engagement process to
ensure its accessibility and adequacy. Separate GRMs are available for: (i) project workers and
detailed in the project’s LMP; and (ii) for PAPs due to land acquisition and detailed in the project’s
RP. All information about grievance procedures, grievance forms, and responses will be available in
Vietnamese and English, as follows:

6.1. All-functional Local Level Procedures
 •   The first level –CPC. An affected stakeholder is to take his/her complaint to any member of
     the W/CPC, through the village/cluster head, or directly to CPC, in written or oral form. The
     W/CPC will work directly with the complainant to resolve the complaint and will issue a
     resolution within 15 working days after receiving the complaint. The secretariat of the CPC is
     responsible for documenting and recording all the complaints that it is handling. Once the CPC
     issues its decision, the stakeholder can appeal to the PPC within 30 working days if they are
     not satisfied with the decision or after 15 working days if they have not heard back from the
     CPC.
 •   The second level – PPC. Upon receiving a complaint from a stakeholder, the PPC will have
     30 working days to resolve the case. The PPC is responsible for filing and storing documents
     for all complaints that it handles. After the PPC has issued a decision, the stakeholder can appeal
     to the people’s court within 45 working days if they are not satisfied with the decision or after
     30 working days if they have not heard back from the PPC.
 •   The final level - Court of Law Decides: In case a stakeholder brings his/her case to provincial
     people’s court and the court rules in favor of the complainant. The decision ruling the settlement
     of complaints will have to be sent to complainants and concerned parties and shall be publicly
     posted at the headquarters of the People's Committee of the relevant level. The complainant
     will receive such ruling three days after the result of complaint resolution at the commune level
     has been decided upon and 7 working days at the provincial level.
82. The GRM Officer will need to liaise with the above levels to keep a database of the project-
related complaints from stakeholders, including information such as the nature of the complaint, the
source, and date of receipt of the complaint, the name, and address of the complainant, action plan,
and current status (see Annex 1), including complaints both received orally and in writing. If needed,
the GRM Officer will be able to contact complainants directly and guide them through the official
GRM channels for the project.




                                                                                             50 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)




Figure 14: The grievance process for the Project

6.2. Project-Specific Construction-Related Procedures
83. During construction, the GRM will also be managed by the Contractors under the supervision
of the construction supervision consultant. Through consultations and disclosure activities, the
Contractors will inform stakeholders about the GRM and how to submit complaints and concerns
about the project. The Contractors will also appoint a ESO to be responsible for grievance redress.
All complaints and corresponding actions undertaken by the Contractor will be recorded in project
monitoring reports. Complaints and claims for damages could be lodged as follows:
 •   Verbally: directly to the Contractor’s ESO or representatives at the site offices.
 •   In writing: by hand-delivering or posting a written complaint to a designated address of the
     project.
 •   By telephone, fax, or e-mails: to the project’s GRM Focal Point or the contractor’s
     representatives.
84. Upon receipt of a complaint, the Contractor’s ESO will register the complaint in a complaint
file and maintain a log until the grievance is resolved (see Annex 1). Immediately upon receipt, four
copies of the complaint will be made: the original for the file, one copy for the ESO, one copy

                                                                                          51 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

forwarded to the PPMU, and one copy for the complainant. The information to be recorded in the
complaint log will consist of at least:
 •   The date and time of the complaint,
 •   The name, address, and contact details of the complainant,
 •   A short description of the complaint,
 •   Actions taken to address the complaint, including contact persons and findings at each step in
     the complaint redress process,
 •   The final resolution of the complaint.
85. The main objective will be to resolve issues on the spot as much as possible. When that is not
possible, complaints will be dealt with within one week. When a complaint cannot be resolved within
15 days, local authorities will be involved. Such a situation may arise, for example, when damages
are claimed, the to-be-paid amount cannot be resolved, or damage causes need to be determined.

6.3. Procedures in Cases of Gender-Based Violence/Sexual exploitation and abuse/Sexual
      harassment
86. To avoid the risk of stigmatization, exacerbation of the mental/psychological harm, or potential
reprisal, the GRM shall have a different and sensitive approach to GBV/SEA/SH-related cases.
Where such a case is reported to the GRM, it should immediately be referred to appropriate service
providers, such as medical and psychological support, emergency accommodation, and/or any other
necessary services. It should also be reported to the PPMU’s GRM Officer, who can advise on
relevant service providers if needed. Data on GBV/SEA/SH cases should not be collected through
the GRM unless operators have been trained on the empathetic, non-judgmental, and confidential
collection of these complaints. Only the nature of the complaint (what the complainant says in her/his
own words) and additional demographic data, such as age and gender, can be collected as usual.
87. In addition, capacity-building for GRM committees on handling GBV/SEA/SH-related
grievances and awareness campaigns about the GRM for communities will be conducted during the
project implementation.

6.4. Procedures in cases of conflicts among ethnic groups
88. In the Project, addressing potential conflicts between the Chinese and Khmer ethnic groups is
critical to fostering collaboration and ensuring project success. The project will establish a Grievance
Redress Committee (GRC) composed of equal representation from the Hoa and Khmer communities,
including community elders, local government representatives, and religious leaders from both
groups. This committee will meet biweekly to review grievances and ensure timely resolution.
89. When a conflict arises, the GRC will appoint a neutral mediator, agreed upon by both parties,
to facilitate a resolution. The mediation process will take place in a neutral and culturally significant
location, such as the community center, and will prioritize open dialogue to understand each group’s
concerns. Decisions will be documented and shared transparently with both groups to maintain trust.
90. To protect the privacy of complainants, the GRC will ensure confidentiality throughout the
process. Additionally, regular workshops will be held to educate both communities about the
grievance process, emphasizing its fairness and neutrality. Progress and outcomes will be monitored,
and lessons learned will be incorporated into the GRM to continually improve its effectiveness. This

                                                                                              52 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

mechanism will ensure conflicts are resolved respectfully, fostering harmony between the Chinese
and Khmer communities while supporting the project’s objectives.
91. Grievances can be submitted through multiple channels, including community meetings at the
local pagoda, verbal submissions to village leaders, or written complaints deposited in project
grievance boxes located in communal houses. To ensure inclusivity, project staff will provide
interpreters for both Chinese and Khmer languages, and all written materials will be bilingual.

6.5. Procedures in Cases of Labors’ Concerns
92. Project workers can submit their complaints (related to employment opportunities, wages,
payment, working conditions, health, safety, administrative acts and decisions, discipline decisions,
etc.) in writing, or face-to-face verbally – except for complaints against discipline decisions to their
employer/contractor who are responsible for solving first-time complaint as outlined in the GRM for
project workers in the Project LMP. Complainants can also lodge a grievance through the PPMU
using email, post, or telephone or PPMU dedicated channels for GRM. Complainant can bring their
case to the court of law at any stage during the complaint resolution process (See the project LMP
for details).

6.6. Procedures in Cases of Land Acquisition
93. Complaint concerning land acquisition, physical resettlement, and relevant issues are expected
to be lodged through CPCs. Submission of complaints can be done using emails, postal, hand
delivery, or verbally through direct meetings or telephone. Within 10 days from the date of receiving
the complaint, the person in charge of complaint resolution shall accept the complaint and notify the
complainant in writing. The time limit for complaint resolution – from the date of acceptance, is 30
days for first-time complaints, and 45 days for the second complaints. The statute of limitation (for
initiating a lawsuit) is 30 days from the date the time-limit for complaint resolution expires, or from
the date the complainant receives the complaint resolution decision. In a remote area where travel is
difficult, the statute of limitation can be extended but not exceed 45 days. The complainant can also
initiate a lawsuit at any stage of the grievance resolution process if they disagree with the complaint
resolution decision, or if the case is not resolved following the expiry of the time-limit for complaint
resolution (see the project RP for details).

VII. MONITORING AND REPORTING
94. The SEP will be periodically revised and updated as necessary in the course of project
implementation to ensure that the information presented is consistent, and up to date, and that the
identified methods of engagement remain appropriate and effective concerning the project context
and specific phases of project development. Any major changes to project-related activities and its
schedule will be duly reflected in the updated SEP.
95. Monthly summaries and internal reports on public grievances, together with the status of
implementation of associated corrective actions will be collated by responsible staff and referred to
the PPMU’s Director. The monthly summaries will provide a mechanism for assessing both the
number and the nature of complaints and requests for information, along with the project’s ability to
address those in a timely and satisfactory manner. The project will make public quarterly project
monitoring reports on its website [https://angiang.gov.vn], which will include reporting on the SEP
and grievance redress.

                                                                                             53 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

7.1. Internal monitoring
96. The objective of internal monitoring relating to the SEP is to oversee its implementation to
ensure targeted consultations and disclosure activities are taking place. Internal Monitoring will be
led by the PPMU’s ESO, under the overall guidance of the PPMU’s Director. The involvement of
local communities in monitoring activities should be encouraged whenever possible. Internal
Monitoring will consist of quarterly reports during the implementation of the project, part of the
ESMP or RP monitoring, including public grievances, inquiries, and relating incidents, together with
the status of implementation of associated corrective/preventative actions will be gathered by the
ESO and reported to the PPMU’s Director.
97.   Monitoring SEP implementation should focus on:
 •    Level of understanding of the project,
 •    Community feedback incorporated into project design and planning,
 •    Knowledge by stakeholders of project documents, including the GRM and impact mitigation
      plans,
 •    Implementation of disclosure and consultation activities as per the SEP,
 •    Main grievances and efficacy of GRM,
 •    Overall community satisfaction,
 •    Minutes of consultation meetings,
 •    Number of staff working on the stakeholder engagement, including at the PPMU and its
      consultants or contractors, and
 •    Plans for the next month and long-term plans.
98.   Monitoring specific to land acquisition activities will be conducted as per the RP.

7.2. Third Party Monitoring
99. The PPMU will engage an IESMC as a third party to assist the PPMU in monitoring and
evaluation of the implementation of E&S risk and impact management and mitigation measures,
including compensation and resettlement.
100. This consultant will undertake periodic monitoring every six months on the implementation of
E&S risk and impact management in compliance with regulations set forth in the project’s
ESIA/ESMP, RP, SEP, and LMP. They will also monitor closely the implementation of project’s
GRM.
101. Key tasks of the consultant related to SEP will be:
 •    Adequacy of stakeholder engagement, including activities, staffing, and budget.
 •    Review of grievances submitted, time to respond, resolution of grievances, and complainant’s
      satisfaction with the process.
 •    Interview of sample households to assess satisfaction with stakeholder engagement and
      knowledge of the project and related programs.




                                                                                            54 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

7.3. Community Monitoring
102. As per Decree 84/2015/ND-CP dated 30 September 2015 regarding investment supervision and
assessment, local people can establish a community investment supervision board to conduct
environmental, social, and construction safety supervision voluntarily. During project
implementation, communities that benefit from the project will be encouraged to form a local team
by this Decree to monitor and provide feedback on construction-related issues.

7.4. Reporting back to stakeholders
103. The method of reporting back to stakeholders will depend on the stakeholder itself, as follows:
 •   Regarding community concerns, the project will report back to the community and other
     relevant stakeholder groups, primarily through public meetings and/or public disclosure which
     people of the community can easily access such as the community’s common house or
     communal office. The feedback report will be shared or disclosed to the concerned community
     and other stakeholders within the timeframe set up in the stakeholder engagement program.
 •   Regarding individual concerns, including grievances related to E&S issues such as inaccurate
     measurement of affected land area, undervalued compensation rate, or request for additional
     support for livelihood restoration, the PPMU, and local implementing agencies will report back
     individually, via the same channel through which the grievances were received, for easy follow-
     up of the complainant. Feedback received through the GRM will be responded to in writing and
     sent directly to the complainant via postal mail.
 •   Regarding comments of stakeholders both affected and concerned parties on the project design,
     E&S documents, they will be addressed in relevant documents and posted on the websites of
     the PPMU and PPC and also posted locally on the public notice board at CPC’s office.
     Comments and questions submitted in association with specific documents disclosed on the
     above websites will be reviewed and responded to promptly as regulated in the ESIA, ESCP,
     SEP, ESMP, RP, EMDP, and LMP.
 •   Regarding comments of national-level stakeholders, an email and/or official letter will be sent
     after workshops on how comments/suggestions were taken into account and/or to inform them
     of documents that are publicly available.
104. In addition, project documents, including monthly, quarterly, or annual reports will be updated
and uploaded as needed, including this SEP, and available on the project website at:
https://angiang.gov.vn. During consultation activities, stakeholders will also be reminded that these
documents are publicly available, as well as the project’s GRM for lodging their concerns,
grievances, and complaints related to the project implementation, including the implementation of
E&S risk and impact management and mitigation measures via public meeting, consultation and
feedback reports.

I. INTRODUCTION
1.1.    Project objectives
1. The project seeks to create a sustainable fisheries sector in Ca Mau province that includes deep
international integration, climate change adaptation, and environmental protection through
infrastructure construction and upgrades, technological innovation, and improved management and
production capacity.
2.     The project aims to the following specific objectives:
 −     To increase aquaculture productivity in 03 aquaculture areas, including Tan Duc commune (now
       Tan Thuan commune), Ta An Khuong Dong commune (now Ta An Khuong commune), Dam
       Doi district (before the merger); Dong Thoi commune (now Cai Nuoc commune), Cai Nuoc
       district (before the merger); Phu Tan commune, Phu My commune, Phu Tan district (before the
       merger) by investing in embankments combined with rural roads; improving technical
       production methods, disease control, and environmental management towards sustainability and
       adaptation to climate change; and establishing value chain linkages and branding strategies.
 −     To accommodate more boats while ensuring compliance with environmental protection
       regulations by improving the capacity of Rach Goc fishing port
 −     To combat illegal, unreported, and unregulated (IUU) fishing; to manage and protect marine
       protected areas; and to protect fishery resources in Ca Mau province by investing in equipment
       and enforcement mechanisms.

1.2.   Project Components
3. The project consists of 4 components which provide practical solutions to optimize and improve
resource management for fisheries exploitation and aquaculture, as follows:
Component 1: Development of fisheries exploitation infrastructure
4.     There are 2 subcomponents in the component 1, including:
Sub-component 1.1: Upgrades to the Rach Goc fishing port
5. The subcomponent (SC) includes the construction of a roof sorting facility, the expansion of the
wharf by 150 meters, and the dredging of the area in front of the port (Table 33). The upgrade aims
to ensure the port operates at a capacity of 100 boat arrivals per day, handling over 18,000 tons of
fish per year, while integrating a storm shelter anchorage for 1,000 boats.
Table 33: Specifications on the upgrades of the Rach Goc fishing port
 No.         Descriptions        Work                                   Specifications
                               grade/level

 1       Docking area         Grade III      Number of wharves: 2 wharves with total length of 150m will be built,
                                             each wharf has the following specifications:
                                             − Length: 75m

                                             − Width: 14m

                                             − Top elevation: +2.50m

                                             Bottom elevation: -5.00m

             area in front of the                  − Dredging length: 270m
             piers
                                                   Dredging bottom elevation: -5.00m

             roof                                  the following:
                                                   − Length: 29m

                                                   − Total height: 7.2m

                                                   − Clearance height: 4.5m

                                                   Width: 9.00m

6.       Structure of the wharf:
 −       Foundation reinforcement:
         •      Reinforced concrete piles with dimensions 40x40 cm, estimated length 31m.
         •      Pile spacing: 4.25m across the wharf width, 3.5m along the wharf length.
 −       Structural components:
         •      Reinforced concrete beams: 60x85 cm (including slab thickness).
         •      Deck slab thickness: 25 cm, with an additional 7 cm asphalt concrete layer on top.
 −       Additional installations:
         •      Mooring bollards with a 15-ton capacity.
         •      Fender system: Cylindrical type D300, 3.0m in length.
Sub-component 1.2: Purchasing 2 patrol boats and 2 canoes to support fisheries management
       operations
7.       The specifications of the boats and canoes are as follows:
 −       Patrol boats: Hull dimensions: Maximum length (Lmax): 30m, Maximum width (Bmax):
         6m, Max depth (Dmax): 2.82m; and engine power: ≥ 1,000 HP
 −       Canoes: Hull dimensions: Maximum length (Lmax): 7m, Maximum width (Bmax):
         2.5m, Maximum depth (Dmax): 1.02m; and engine power: 200 HP.
Component 2: Investment in infrastructure for aquaculture
8. There are 3 sub-components under the component for aquacultural zones for the 5 communes of
Tan Thuan (formerly Tan Duc), Ta An Khuong (formerly Ta An Khuong Dong), Cai Nuoc (formerly
Dong Thoi), Phu Tan and Phu My, including:
Sub-component 2.1: Building 76.72km of embankments in combination with rural roads
9.       Specifications of the embankments invested in the project are illustrated in Table 34.

Sub-component 2.3: Installing 4 monitoring stations for water quality for aquaculture
11. The 4 monitoring stations are located as follows:
−       One station at Cay Met intersection, Tan Hiep hamlet.
−       One station at Ong Rum intersection of Thuan Hoa and Thuan Loi hamlets.
−       One station at the intersection of Quang Loi - Lung Mam canals.
−       One station at Cai Nuoc commune.
Component 3: Capacity building for management and enhancement of the value of aquatic
products
12. The component includes the following activities:
−       Enhance Illegal, Unreported, and Unregulated (IUU) fishing management capacity; apply and
        transfer efficient fisheries exploitation technologies, reduce post-harvest losses, monitor fishing
        vessel journeys, and develop a fisheries database.
−       Strengthen the capacity of management agencies; develop high-tech application models and
        systems for seafood traceability.
−       Provide technical support for the development of biosecure aquaculture areas that meet
        international standards, promote value chain links, and build and market product branding.
Component 4: Project management
13. Ca Mau PPMU is responsible for managing the project in accordance with the provisions of the
Law on Public Investment and other applicable regulations. This ensures that investment decisions
are consistent with the project’s objectives and strategies, so increasing the project's overall
effectiveness.

1.3.   Project Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
1.3.1. Positive impacts
14. The project’s benefits are long-term rather than short-term. If successful, the project will
contribute to the sustainable development of aquaculture in coastal areas, improve livelihoods for
local communities, and enhance disaster mitigation efforts. Also, it will help protect production
activities and strengthen climate change resilience, specifically:


                                                                                                10 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

 −   The multi-species ecological aquaculture model helps minimize negative environmental
     impacts, protect biodiversity and coastal ecosystems, conserve natural aquatic resources, and
     contribute to the sector’s long-term sustainability. Aquatic products from ecological models are
     typically of higher quality, meeting food safety standards and the requirements of demanding
     markets. Additionally, the multi-species ecological aquaculture model enables farmers to
     increase productivity while reducing production costs, thereby improving their income. As
     domestic and international markets increasingly demand clean, safe, and traceable seafood
     products, this model will meet these requirements, fostering local economic growth, creating
     jobs, and enhancing the livelihoods of coastal communities.
 −   The transportation infrastructure invested in through the project will connect to the main roads
     in the area and surrounding regions, serving as a driving force for the sustainable development
     of aquaculture. This is reflected in several key aspects: reducing transportation costs for input
     materials (such as feed, biological products, seedlings, and farming equipment) and harvested
     products, while also increasing the market value of seafood. With improved road access, larger
     purchasing vehicles, including refrigerated trucks, will be able to reach farming areas directly.
     Additionally, the upgraded transportation infrastructure will enhance regional connectivity,
     laying the foundation for the growth of other economic sectors such as industry, trade, and
     tourism. It will also increase the overall economic value of agriculture and aquaculture in the
     region, improve logistics and mobility for local communities, and particularly benefit ethnic
     minority populations by facilitating better access to markets and essential services.
1.3.2.    Negative impacts
15. During preparation phase, the negative potential impacts and risks include (i) impacts due to
land acquisition. About 49.1 ha of land will be used for the project, but only 39.1 ha will be acquired
from affected households, in which no household will be relocated; (ii) impacts on livelihood; (iii)
land use change; (iv) impacts due to UXOs. Most impacts are temporary, local and reversible due to
medium scale construction. These impacts and risks can be mitigated by applying the approved
resettlement plan (RP) of the project and the project owner will contract with relevant unit to clear
UXOs before commencing construction activities and must monitor and consult with local people
about the land acquisition and clearance and UXO clearance.
16. During construction phase, dust, exhaust gas, noise, vibration; wastewater; solid waste;
ecosystem; labor influx; improper access, hard-working conditions, health and safety of workers and
locals; local flooding; local traffic; land subsidence, soil erosion; raw material supply, etc. are generic
impacts and risks of the project. These impacts can be considered as low to moderate for each item
and can be minimized by Environmental and Social Code of Practices (ESCOPs). In addition, the
project implementation causes specific impacts and risks, including impacts on water taking, impacts
on livelihoods, impacts on water traffic, impacts by the relocation of infrastructure, impacts on ethnic
minorities (EMs) and impacts on sensitive receptors, etc. These impacts and risks can be mitigated
by specific measures including consultation with local authorities about the project activities and
construction plan, the arrangement of a temporary road, construction in the dry season and the
offseason for water taking; informing people of construction time; not using high-noise machinery
and equipment that emit a lot of dust and noise through the sensitive areas during major religious
holidays/study time; spraying sufficient water to reduce dust during dry and windy days; providing
safety measures as installation of fences, barriers warning signs, lighting system against traffic


                                                                                               11 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

accidents; prohibiting gathering of construction materials within 100m in front of the sensitive
objects; cleaning up construction areas at the end of the day, especially construction around the
sensitive structures; immediately addressing any issue/problem caused by the construction activities;
reinstating and providing adequate compensation for any impacts which will be identified causing
by the construction activities.
17. During the operation phase, the generic impacts and risks are dust, air emission; noise and
vibration; domestic wastewater and solid waste; increase of traffic accidents; land use change; and
damage to the embankments, the fishing port. The generic impacts and risks can be mitigated by
training and raising awareness of the embankment’s users, arranging suitable speed limit signs in the
crowed areas; planting and maintaining trees on both sides of the embankments to prevent noise from
spreading into local people’s houses along the embankments; regular maintenance of the project
items, preparing an Emergency Contingency Plan; etc. In addition, the project operation causes
specific impacts and risks, including impacts on water drainage; risks of damage of the embankments,
the fishing port; impacts of saline and/or brackish water on construction works; impacts on aquatic
life; canal bank erosion. The impacts and risks are mitigated by specific measures including
communication, raising the local awareness of environmental protection, especially water quality in
canals; collecting packages, excessive foods during the agricultural and aquacultural production;
prohibiting littering in surrounding area or water sources; etc.

II. OBJECTIVES OF THE STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN
18. Objectives. The objective of this stakeholder engagement plan (SEP) is to define a program for
stakeholder engagement, including public information disclosure, grievance redress mechanism
(GRM), and consultation, throughout the implementation and operation of the project. It specifies
how the PPMU and contractors will communicate with stakeholders, as well as a mechanism for
people to express concerns, provide feedback, or file complaints about the project, and contractors.
Consultation and engagement with stakeholders are critical to ensure smooth collaboration between
the project and local communities, as well as to minimize and mitigate environmental and social risks
and impacts of the project, thereby contributing to the project's success.
19. Principle of the SEP. This SEP’s principles are:
       o Transparency: Providing the Project with decision-making processes which are open to
         scrutiny by stakeholders to enhance accountability.
       o Inclusivity: Involving all stakeholders systematically and meaningfully whilst ensuring
         the participation of Vulnerable Groups (VGs).
       o Objectivity: Considering all opinions, feedback and positions from all stakeholders in
         without bias manner or prejudice.
       o Timeliness: Providing stakeholders with easily understandable, relevant and accurate
         information, and engaging them, within reasonable and consistent timeframes for their
         participation to be meaningful and informed.
       o Non-Coercive: Preventing and not using any forms of coercion, intimidation,
         misinformation, external influence/interference and retribution in all consultations.
       o Culturally Appropriate: Using consultation interfaces and modes that are adhere with
         stakeholders’ cultural norms and traditions (e.g. Khmer ethnic minority people)


                                                                                          12 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

20. Scope of the SEP. This SEP is to assess the level of stakeholder interest and support for the
project, enable stakeholder views to be taken into account in project design and environmental and
social performance, promote and provide means for inclusive engagement throughout the project
life-cycle, ensure that appropriate project information is disclosed to stakeholders in a timely,
understandable, accessible, and appropriate manner and format, provide citizens with accessible and
inclusive means to raise issues and grievances and enable the project implementing entity to respond
to and manage such grievances. Stakeholders were first identified, then mapped according to their
level of involvement and impact, then a consultation plan and methods were proposed based on
stakeholder prioritization.
21. The project stakeholders will engage at different stages, including during the initial design,
detailed design, pre-construction, construction, and operation. Engagement will vary during the life
of the project and this SEP will be dynamic and flexible to these changes. The SEP should be read
together with other project documents such as ESIA, ESMP, LMP, EMDP, RP, and ESCP. This SEP
is a living document that will be updated once consultations have taken place, incorporating minutes
of meetings and relevant comments from project stakeholders.

III. STAKEHOLDER IDENTIFICATION AND ANALYSIS
22. Project stakeholders are people and entities who have a role in the project, or could be affected
by the project, or who are interested in the project. Project stakeholders are defined as individuals,
groups or other entities who:
 (iii) are impacted or likely to be impacted directly or indirectly, positively or adversely, by the
        project (also known as “affected parties”); and
 (iv)   may have an interest in the project (“interested parties”). They include individuals or groups
        whose interests may be affected by the project and who have the potential to influence the
        project’s outcomes in any way.
23. Cooperation and negotiation with the stakeholders throughout the project development often also
requires the identification of people within the groups who act as legitimate representatives of their
respective stakeholder group, i.e., the individuals who have been entrusted by their fellow group
members with advocating the groups’ interests in the process of engagement with the project.
Community representatives may provide helpful insight into the local settings and act as main
conduits for dissemination of the project-related information and as a primary communication/liaison
link between the project and targeted communities and their established networks. Verification of
stakeholder representatives (i.e., the process of confirming that they are legitimate and genuine
advocates of the community they represent) remains an important task in establishing contact with
the community stakeholders. Legitimacy of the community representatives can be verified by talking
informally to a random sample of community members and heeding their views on who can be
representing their interests in the most effective way.

3.1. Methodology
24. To meet best practice approaches, the project will apply the following principles for stakeholder
engagement:




                                                                                           13 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

 −   Openness and life-cycle approach: public consultations for the project will be arranged during
     the whole lifecycle, carried out openly, free of external manipulation, interference, coercion, or
     intimidation.
 −   Informed participation and feedback: information will be provided to and widely distributed
     among all stakeholders in an appropriate format; opportunities are provided for communicating
     stakeholders’ feedback, and for analyzing and addressing comments and concerns.
 −   Inclusiveness and sensitivity: stakeholder identification will be undertaken to support better
     communications and build effective relationships. The participation process for the project will
     be inclusive. All stakeholders at all times will be encouraged to get involved in the consultation
     process. Equal access to information will be provided to all stakeholders. Sensitivity to
     stakeholders’ needs is the key principle underlying the selection of engagement methods. Special
     attention will be given to vulnerable groups, in particular women, displaced persons, persons
     with disabilities, youth, elderly.

3.2. Stakeholder Identification
25. The stakeholder mapping for the project demonstrates that focus is placed on the core project
stakeholders and beneficiaries as well as host communities of the project. Therefore, the project
coordinates endeavors via facilitator/consultant teams to prompt and comprehensive involvement of
these groups (

Figure 16: Stakeholders’ influence and interest analysis
29. Stakeholders of the project are people or groups who are directly or indirectly affected by the
project, as well as those who may have an interest in the project and/or the ability to influence project
outcomes, either positively or negatively. For effective and tailored engagement, the project’s
stakeholders can be divided into the following 3 core categories:

3.3. Affected Parties
30. One of the primary goals of the project is to identify stakeholders on which the project can have
negative impact such as households or businesses who may be impacted by land acquisition,
livelihoods and business activities. However, the project may influence the activities of various
stakeholders in positive or negative ways. Thus, a list of key stakeholder groups can be identified as
potentially affected parties. These should be engaged throughout the life of the project and impacts
on them should be monitored and mitigated. Project-affected parties include those likely to be
affected by the project because of actual impacts or potential risks to their physical environment,
health, security, cultural practices, well-being, or livelihoods. The following groups can be identified
as potential project-affected stakeholders:
31. People who are physically or economically displaced by the project: The main groups of
project-affected parties in the stakeholder mapping are the people physically or economically
displaced by the project, and persons who reside or have land and non-land assets in the Right of
Way (ROW) of the proposed investments. The following parties are identified as directly affected:


                                                                                              16 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

 −   People, physically or economically displaced and people who reside or have land and non-land
     assets in the ROW of the proposed investments:
       • People and companies who will lose their land.
       • People and businesses with structures on land that will be relocated.
       • People and companies with trees and crops that will be affected.
       • People and companies with surface water (aquaculture) that will be affected.
 −   Fishing communities.
 −   Woman (including women-headed households with dependents, female members of the affected
     households and companies).
 −   Ethnic Minorities (EMs).
 −   Other vulnerable households such as poor/near-poor households; landless households; persons
     with physical or mental disabilities (loss of working ability); people living alone elderly; and
     social policy category (families of martyrs, war invalids, and sick soldiers, etc.).
 −   People who will have access brackish water from canals and rives for production where
     embankments will be built.
 −   People who will have access to the embankments and the fish port for their travelling and the
     movement of their commodities.
32. Communities in the vicinity of the project areas: Local community and companies who are
likely to be exposed to temporary air pollution, traffic difficulties, and other potential risks such as
traffic safety, traffic disturbance, wastewater discharge, gender-based violence (GBV), sexual
exploitation and abuse (SEA)/Sexual harassment (SH), health risks as a result of labour influx, etc.
33. Executing/implementing agencies, relevant province’s agencies, and administrative
authorities at all levels: These groups include Ca Mau Provincial People’s Committee (Ca Mau
PPC), Ca Mau PPMU, Department of Finance (DOF), Department of Construction (DOC),
Department of Home Affairs (DOHA), Department of Agriculture and Environment (DAE),
Department of Ethnic and Religious Affairs (DOERA), Site Clearance and Compensation
Committee; Land Fund Development Center (LFDC), 6 Commune People’s Committee (CPC)s in
the project area, and public service utilities (such as telecommunication, electric power, water supply,
wastewater, traffic management).
34. Project beneficiaries: Project beneficiaries are large in number (they are positively affected by
the project). These include individuals, households, groups, public and private sectors who benefit
from the construction of embankments, fishing ports, and the dredging of canals.

3.4. Other interested parties
36. The project’s “other interested parties” refer to individuals, groups, or organization with an
interest in the project, due to either the project location, its characteristics, its impacts, or matters
related to public interest. The following stakeholders are listed as project interested parties:
 • Central level: Ministries and government agencies include the Office of Government (OOG)
   and interest line-ministries, those play a key role in their functions and responsibilities and
   sectoral management, such as the Ministry of Finance (MOF), Ministry of Agriculture and
   Environment (MAE), Ministry of Construction (MOC), Ministry of Home Affairs (MOHA),
   Ministry of Ethnic and Religious Affairs (MOERA).
 • Provincial level: Ca Mau PPC, Ca Mau PPMU, DOF, DOC, DAE, DOERA, DOHA, DOST.
 • Commune level: 6 project CPCs.
 • Mass organizations include 6 organizations at all levels as Women’s Union, Youth Union,
   Fatherland Front, Farmers’ Union, Veterans Associations, and Trade Union.
 • Mass media include local, regional and national news agencies (TV, broadcasting, online
   newspaper) who may be interested in project activities, project progress, and impact on local
   development.
 • Commune’s citizen groups benefit from additional income thanks to providing services to
   construction workers (e.g., foods and other essential supplies, etc.). Those who may become
   involved in construction and operation as seasonal workers are also those who may be interested
   in the project, and those who are interested in supervision of the construction of the project
   components.
 • Development agencies and/or NGOs: Currently, there are no NGOs working in the 6 project
   communes. However, NGOs become interested in the project during its implementation, this
   SEP will be updated to reflect their concerns (if any).
 • Public service utilities, including telecommunication, electric power, water supply, wastewater
   treatment, waste collection, waterways traffic management, commune health centers, etc.
 • Experts, universities, institutes, including Research Institute for Aquaculture 2
 • Private companies: Service providers for GBV/SEA/SH service provider, other service
   providers (tourist centers, hotels, motels, etc.) for contracted workers.

3.5. Disadvantaged/Vulnerable Individuals and Groups
38. Disadvantaged/vulnerable individuals and groups may be affected disproportionately - either
adversely or positively, by the project. Due to low socioeconomic profile, their voices may not be
heard in the formal decision-making process, or included in consultation process but their feedback
are not solicited in a manner that offers sufficient understanding of how they are affected, how they
would respond, and what support they may need to avoid or mitigate such impact – from their own
perspective. Identification of vulnerable individuals and groups, including those who are stigmatized,
is an important step to effectively engaging them in the consultation throughout the project cycle.
This enables Ca Mau PPMU to work out measures to avoid adverse impact, or minimize or mitigate
such impact, if avoidance is not possible. Inclusion of disadvantaged/vulnerable individuals and
groups - at the early stage of project planning, can also assist in project design that provide them
opportunities to restore and improve their livelihoods.
39. The project needs to make special efforts to ensure vulnerable and disadvantaged stakeholders
can be engaged. In this project, these groups include:


                                                                                                            24 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

 • Ethnic minorities (EMs).
 • Elderly people and/or social policy householders (invalid soldiers, martyrs, heroic mothers).
 • Poor/near-poor families.
 • People with disabilities and their caretakers.
 • Women, particularly in women-headed households or single mothers with underage/ dependent
   children.
 • Unemployed people.
40. During project preparation, identified disadvantaged/vulnerable groups have been invited to
consultation to solicit their feedbacks on project impact and risks, and their proposed mitigation
measures. During project implementation, based on the nature and the magnitude of impact,
consultations will be continued to solicit their opinion, particularly prior to construction when project
impact become known and the affected vulnerable have sufficient time to better understand the
project impact and base on that provide meaningful feedback. Table 22 indicates disadvantaged or
vulnerable groups encountered under the project.

41. As mentioned above, there is a wide range of project stakeholders that are affected by and are
interested in project implementation. Different stakeholders have different levels of interest and
influence on project design and implementation. Therefore, it is important to understand: (i) the level
of interest of each stakeholder as to the project’s investment activities; (ii) the impact magnitude of
the project on them, particularly those adversely affected, and (iii) the extent to which the stakeholder
influences the project design, as well as implementation process and outcome.
42. Under this project, stakeholders highly relevant to the project preparation and implementation
include governmental agencies and the community at large. These stakeholders are affected by the
project and at the same time can influence project design and implementation - to various extents.
Their interest in project investments also varies at different stages of the project cycle, and as such
exert different levels of influence on the project design and implementation process.
43. Based on the roles, responsibilities, and the potential interest of the key stakeholders obtained
from initial consultations with them, Table 23 summarizes the estimated levels of interest, impact,
and influence of each stakeholder group, of which:
 (iv) “impact” means the project's impacts (both positive and negative impacts) on each key group
      of the stakeholder during phases of the project.
 (v)   “influence” means people’s influence on the project implementation.
 (vi) Level of “impact” and “influence” will be assessed on 3 different levels: High; Moderate;
      and Low.
44. It is noted that these levels of interest, impact, and influence may change throughout the project.
However, the current dynamics of stakeholders’ interest, impact, and influence, as described in Table
23, are useful for informing how each stakeholder should be engaged - in a meaningful manner, to
ensure they contribute to the overall environmental and social performance process, and outcome of
the project.

STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)


IV. STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PROGRAM
45. In order to achieve the objectives of the SEP, the project will develop an engagement program
with the following purposes:
 • Provide meaningful information in a format and language that is readily understandable.
 • Provide information in advance of consultation activities when possible.
 • Disseminate information in a manner and location easy for stakeholders to access.
 • Establish a two-way dialogue that allows the project and stakeholders to exchange views and
   information, and have issues heard and addressed.
 • Ensure inclusiveness in the representation of views, including those of women, the elderly,
   people living with a disability, and other vulnerable people as necessary.
 • Ensure any obstacles to participation that are identified are removed so that views of different
   stakeholders can be captured.
 • Ensure there are clear mechanisms for responding to people’s concerns, suggestions, and/or
   grievances.
 • Incorporate feedback into the project and report back to stakeholders.
 • Monitor stakeholder engagement activities and include project stakeholders in monitoring to the
   extent possible.
 • Incorporate stakeholder engagement as part of the project management responsibilities and
   ensure staff in the PPMU and its consultants are equipped with specific responsibilities and
   budget.

4.1.   Summary of previous stakeholder engagement
4.1.1. During the preparation of the project Stakeholder Engagement Framework
46. Numerous meetings were held by the Central Project Management Board (CPMU) at 12 project
provinces in May and June 2020, as part of the Stakeholder Engagement Framework preparation for
the Sustainable Fisheries Development Project. The consultation meeting with Ca Mau province was
held on June 12, 2020.
47. The purpose of the consultation was to familiarize stakeholders with the project's scope and to
determine acceptable communication and consultation procedures throughout the project lifetime. In
addition, The E&S documents developed for the Sustainable Fisheries Development Project, such as
the ESMF, RPF, EMPF, SEF, LMP, and ESCP, were also consulted in order to obtain feedback from
participants for future improvements.
48. Participants were given general information about the project (objectives, components, project
footprint, potential risks and impacts, financing source, implementation arrangement) during the
consultation. The new applicable World Bank ESF, as well as its E&S standards and criteria, were
also introduced to the participants.
49. The list of participants attending the consultation is presented in Annex 4. The results of the
consultation are summarized in




                                                                                         2|Page
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

50. Table 24.
51. The frameworks are no longer applicable due to changes in project implementation arrangements
and design. However, the outcomes of previous consultations have been incorporated into the overall
stakeholder engagement process. This stakeholder engagement plan reflects and takes into account
all relevant feedback from those consultations.

4.2.    Summary of Key Project Stakeholders’ Needs
54. Various stakeholders require different methods of communication and consultation. For local
government officials, since a well-established communication protocol has been in place, the
communications with them to arrange consultation sessions are through means such as couriers,
electronic mail, telephone conversations, face-to-face meetings, etc. For the community,
communication is typically through courier at village level or paying a home visit to those who are
better approached through direct contact, such as people with disabilities, the elderly, etc.
55. Table 26 summarizes the stakeholder's needs for consultation in the project and proposed
methods to accommodate the needs of different stakeholders.




                                                                                         2|Page
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

4.3.    Strategy for Consultation
56. The project will apply a participatory and consultative approach involving meaningful
engagement with different stakeholders. Project consultations will be a two-way process of dialogue
between the project and its stakeholders, including on issues such as project objectives; working
conditions for contractors; engagement of women; ways to mitigate potential project impacts, and
risks in particular to vulnerable groups. This will depend on the stakeholders as well as the project
timing. For this project, consultation strategies will be proposed for each stage of the project, details
are as follows:

4.4.    Consultation strategies
57. Project preparation (before project appraisal). Since the details of some proposed project
investments are unknown at this stage, consultations during project preparation will focus on a
broader range of stakeholders to understand their general interests and concerns about the project and
its potential positive and negative impacts and inform project design. This is also the stage where
potential vulnerable/disadvantaged groups relevant to the project will be identified.
58. Project implementation. At this stage, the details of proposed project components and specific
project-affected stakeholders will be determined. The consultation will focus more specifically on
people who are impacted by the project, especially those adversely impacted, to address their
concerns through the implementation of project-specific E&S plans. In addition, the project will
ensure that the GRM is disseminated and active to promptly address any grievances. Special attention
will be paid to vulnerable/disadvantaged groups to ensure inclusion, non-discrimination, and access
to project benefits. Consultations will also focus on job opportunities, specific action plans, etc.
59. Project operation. At this stage, the consultation will focus on feedback from stakeholders,
particularly affected ones, on the performance of E&S impact and risk mitigations, results of
grievance resolution conducted during the implementation stage as well as general project operations.
The consultation on employment opportunities for operation and maintenance (O&M) of the project
works will be also conducted. Also, areas for improvement and lessons learned should be identified
for the project. Furthermore, dissemination and reporting back to stakeholders on the outcome of the
monitoring and evaluation of the project will be also arranged.
60. The SEP will be reviewed and updated during project implementation as necessary on adaptive
stakeholder engagement arrangements, particularly the approach, methods and forms of engagement
proposed, and assess the associated potential risks of virus transmission in conducting various
engagement activities.

4.5.    Consultation methods
61. The methods used to conduct consultations will have a direct impact on the outcome of the
project consultation strategy. Therefore, these methods should be designed to account for the diverse
characteristics of various stakeholder groups. The following consultation methods will be used for
this project:
62. National consultations. National consultations are particularly useful to target government
representatives, NGOs, and other groups who have an interest in the project and may also have the
ability to influence it. These types of consultations are also useful at the project design stage, and
annually to inform stakeholders of a project’s progress. National consultations may be conducted


                                                                                               2|Page
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

face-to-face or virtual in a meeting format and may involve break-out groups or other methods to
ensure there is ample time for group discussion and questions and answers.
63. Local consultations. Local consultations are the main way to reach project affected stakeholders
and will involve face-to-face group meetings. In some cases, one-on-one consultations or FGD may
also be carried out, especially to reach and capture the views of vulnerable or disadvantaged groups.
Particular emphasis should be placed on seeking out the voice of women and vulnerable groups, such
as through female only FGDs. In the case of women, the project will ensure consultation activities
are conducted at a time that is convenient to them and that activities such as FGDs are run by women
if possible (particularly for sensitive topics like health or sexual issues).
64. One important way to engage stakeholders will be through training on important topics related
to the project such as gender, the project’s GRM, labor rights (for both men and women), child labor,
HIV/AIDS and STDs, gender-based violence, sexual harassment, and sexual exploitation and abuse.
Since some of these topics are quite sensitive, particular measures may need to be in place, such as
ensuring some training courses are done in female-only or male-only groups, or that they are
facilitated by men or women, as appropriate. Training may be delivered by Ca Mau PPMU/its
consultants.
65. Accessibility issues will also be taken into account, in particular, to ensure people with a
disability can be part of consultations, as well as the specific needs of other groups such as the elderly,
and poor groups.
66. Consultations with project workers. The project will strive to ensure that unskilled workers
come from the community as much as possible, to minimize the influx of labor. Consultations will
encourage local workers to apply for available jobs, including EMs, women and vulnerable or
disadvantaged groups. Contractors and their staff will need to be well consulted and trained on
sensitive issues, including respectful behavior, Codes of Conduct, GBV (including SEA and SH),
HIV/AIDS and STDs, and worker’s GRM, LMP. They also need to be well aware of construction
needs and schedules. Training, on-site interviews, and regular feedback with Ca Mau PPMU and/or
CSC will be used as methods of consultation with them.
67. Consultation activities proposed for the project are summarized in Table 27.

4.6.    Stakeholder Engagement Methods to be Used in the Next Stages
68. Public/community meetings. The Ca Mau PPMU will organize meetings in each project
commune with leaders, PAPs, and other interested parties. Meetings will be open house events where
the PPMU will present all project information and people will be invited to make comments and
express any concerns. A range of consultations with different stakeholders will be conducted and
documented, and community level meetings will also be conducted with men and women (including
vulnerable households) to better understand needs, expectations, and concerns of these population
groups in relation to the project. The feedback received at these meetings will be documented along
with measures that the project will take to address the feedback received. Meetings in each commune
will also be organized on a quarterly basis.
69. Communication materials. Written information will be disclosed to the public via a variety of
communication materials including leaflets, posters, etc. The PPMU will also update its website
regularly (at least on a quarterly basis) with key project updates and reports on the project’s
performance in Vietnamese. The website will also provide information about the grievance
mechanism for the project.
70. Training, workshops. Training on a variety of E&S issues will be provided to the PPMU, and
possibly other relevant government or non-government service providers. Issues covered will include
sensitization to inclusion/exclusion, labor issues, gender-based violence risks.
71. Grievance Redress Mechanism. In compliance with the World Bank’s ESS10, a project-
specific grievance mechanism will be maintained to handle complaints and issues. This will be
integrated into a country system on GRM that are available to citizens. Dedicated communication
materials (specifically, a GRM brochure) will be developed to help local residents become familiar
with the grievance redress channels and procedures. The PPMU will coordinate with administrative
authorities to maintain a grievance register in order to capture and track grievances from submission
to resolution and communication with complainants.
72. The initial effort to resolve grievances to the complainant’s satisfaction will be undertaken by
administrative levels. This will be details in Section 7 below. Grievances may be submitted
anonymously, but in that case, it cannot be known if the complainant is satisfied with the resolution.
The PPMU’s website, posters, and leaflets will include clear information on how feedback, questions,
comments, concerns, and grievances can be submitted by any stakeholder. It will also provide
information on the way grievances will be handled, both in terms of process and deadlines.

4.7.    Strategy to Incorporate the Views of Vulnerable Groups
73. In the project, vulnerable and disadvantaged groups may include, but not limited to, the
following groups: (i) EMs, (ii) Elderly and/or social policy households (injured ex-soldier, heroic
mothers); (iii) Poor/near-poor households; (iv) People with disabilities and their carers; and (v)
Women, in particular female-headed households, or single mothers with young children. However,
these groups in the project-affected communities will be further identified and consulted throughout
the project cycle. They may have a limited understanding of project information or participation in
the consultation process.
74. The views of vulnerable/disadvantaged groups will be sought during the consultation process.
Accommodation and specific measures may be taken to remove obstacles to their participation:
 −   Providing large print,

  Ca Mau Sustainable Fisheries Development Project                                         49 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

 −   Choosing accessible venues that are easy to access (such as with ramps),
 −   Providing transportation for people in remote areas to the nearest meeting,
 −   Having small, focused meetings where vulnerable stakeholders are more comfortable asking
     questions or raising concerns,
 −   Good facilitation to ensure meaningful participation in the consultation,
 −   Using appropriate translators.
 −   Respecting traditions and religious practices, such as Theravada Buddhism and local festivals
     like Chol Chnam Thmay and Ok Om Bok.
 −   Involving community leaders and Buddhist monks, as they hold significant influence and trust
     within Khmer communities.
 −   Using Khmer language or bilingual communication for clarity and inclusion.
 −   Leveraging pagodas as community hubs for meetings, information sharing, and building trust.
 −   Incorporating visual tools like diagrams and videos to account for varying literacy levels.
 −   Facilitating participatory activities, such as small-group discussions, storytelling, or hands-on
     demonstrations, in informal settings.
 −   Acknowledging cultural sensitivities, such as gender roles, by ensuring women and other groups
     are included in discussions.
 −   Celebrating milestones with local traditions, such as Khmer music, dance, or community events,
     to strengthen collaboration and trust.
75. Also, GRM will be disclosed to the identified vulnerable groups based on their own
characteristics. For instance, information on the project, affected land of vulnerable, land
compensation and support policies, and grievance procedures will be delivered at home to people
with disabilities and verbally to those with impaired vision by CPCs or compensation and land
clearance committee. A leaflet containing project information and GRM, as well as contact details,
will be distributed to vulnerable groups, allowing them to contact directly or ask someone else (e.g.,
a caregiver) to contact them on their behalf.
76. Since vulnerable groups have been identified and consulted during project preparation, they will
be consulted again during the next stage of the project based on their specific requirements. For
instance, people using wheel chair may ask for the road sideway to have a ramp for wheelchair access,
or request contractors to stockpile construction materials in a manner that allows access for people
having difficulties moving or children for safety.

5.2.    Implementation Arrangements
81. The SEP will be the responsibility of the Director and ESO of Ca Mau PPMU. They will guide
the process of stakeholder engagement throughout the preparation and implementation of the project.
Certain parts of the SEP will also be the responsibility of contractors or consultants, and the ESO of
Ca Mau PPMU will oversee this work as appropriate. The ESO under the guidance of the Director
will be responsible for:
 −    Leading, or supervising consultations, as per the SEP.
 −    Leading, or supervising the disclosure of information, as per the SEP.
 −    Recording and reporting on grievance resolution, as per the SEP.
 −    Reviewing monthly monitoring reports provided by contractors and/or consultants.
 −    Regularly reporting to the Director.
82. For effective implementation, the PPMU needs to engage various stakeholders identified in
Section 4. However, the participation of governmental agencies, such as PCs and their functional
agencies at 2 levels (provincial, and commune), and the community in the project area, are key to
effective SEP implementation (Table 32).
