VI. GRIEVANCE REDRESS MECHANISM
83. The grievance mechanism seeks to resolve concerns promptly, using an understandable process
that is culturally appropriate and readily accessible at no cost. Grievances can be submitted if
someone believes the project is having a detrimental impact on the community, the environment, or
their quality of life. Stakeholders may also submit their comments and suggestions on the project.
84. The Ca Mau PPMU’s ESO will be responsible for overseeing those grievances are received and
resolved in a fair, objective, and constructive manner all concerns or complaints raised by project
stakeholders. The key principles of the grievance mechanism are to ensure that:
 • The basic rights and interests of affected people are protected.
 • The concerns of affected people, arising from the project implementation process are adequately
   addressed.
 • Entitlements or livelihood support for affected people are provided on time and in accordance
   with project documents, such as ESMP, EMDP, RP.
 • Affected people are aware of their rights to access grievance procedures free of charge for the
   above purposes.
85. Based on the SEP, wherever possible, the project team shall seek to resolve complaints as soon
as possible and thus avoid the escalation of issues. GRM shall not impede access to the country’s

                                                                                                 57 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

judicial or administrative remedies. Stakeholders can approach a court of law at any time and
independent of the project-level grievance redress process. Along with the World Bank requirements
based on ESS10, the GRM will be in line with the Vietnamese national legislation, in particular the
Law on Complaints (2011) and the Law on Denunciation (2018).
86. The Ca Mau PPMU shall establish the GRM. The PPMU shall appoint a GRM focal point to
collaborate with local authorities in handling project-related grievances. The design of the GRM shall
take into consideration the views of affected stakeholders and what is considered an access
mechanism. The PPMU shall be required to disclose and explain to all stakeholders, in particular,
affected stakeholders and vulnerable and disadvantaged ones, the procedure for complaints filing as
early as possible and regularly throughout the project cycle. The grievance mechanism shall be
available at no cost, and it is important that it be easily accessible, with special attention paid to
accessibility for disadvantaged and vulnerable individuals or groups.
87. The PPMU will ensure that grievances are registered, tracked, and resolved in line with
stipulated timeframes.
88.     The GRM officer will be responsible for:
 • Developing and publicizing the grievance management procedures.
 • Receiving, reviewing, and keeping track of grievances (as applicable).
 • Ensuring grievances are settled within agreed time periods.
89. Stakeholders will be consulted about the GRM during the stakeholder engagement process to
ensure its accessibility and adequacy. Separate GRMs are available for: (i) project workers and
detailed in the project’s LMP; and (ii) for PAPs due to land acquisition and detailed in the project’s
RP. All information about grievance procedures, grievance forms, and responses will be available in
Vietnamese and English, as follows:

6.1.     All-functional Local Level Procedures
 • The first level - CPC. An affected stakeholder is to take his/her complaint to any member of the
   CPC, through the village/cluster head, or directly to CPC, in written or oral form. The CPC will
   work directly with the complainant to resolve the complaint and will issue a resolution within
   15 working days after receiving the complaint. The secretariat of the CPC is responsible for
   documenting and recording all the complaints that it is handling. Once the CPC issues its
   decision, the stakeholder can appeal to the PPC within 30 working days if they are not satisfied
   with the decision or after 15 working days if they have not heard back from the CPC.
 • The second level – PPC. If after 30 days the aggrieved PAP does not hear from the CPC, or if
   the PAP is not satisfied with the decision taken on his/her complaint, the PAP may bring the
   case, either in writing or verbally, to any member of the PPC or lodge an administrative case to
   the Regional People’s Court for solution. The PPC has 45 days within which to resolve the
   complaint to the satisfaction of all concerned. The PPC secretariat is also responsible for
   documenting and keeping files of all complaints that it handles. The complainant may decide to
   initiate court proceedings at any stage if he/she no longer wishes to pursue administrative
       complaint procedures.
 • The third level - Regional People’s Court: In accordance with the two-tier local government
   structure effective from 01 July 2025, the Regional People’s Court (replacing the former district-

                                                                                           58 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

    level court) is the competent first-instance court for administrative, civil and criminal cases
    within its assigned area. If the complainant is not satisfied with the PPC’s decision or does not
    receive a response within the time limit, he/she may file a lawsuit with the Regional People’s
    court for adjudication.
 • The final level – Provincial People’s Court: After the Regional People’s Court issues a ruling,
   if the complainant or concerned parties disagree, he/she may appeal to the Provincial People’s
   Court, which acts as the appellate court in line with current legal procedures. Final court rulings
   will be sent to the complainant and publicly posted at the relevant CPC/PPC office within 03
   working days (if originating from CPC level) and 07 working days (if originating from PPC
   level).
90. The GRM Officer will need to liaise with the above levels to keep a database of the project-
related complaints from stakeholders, including information such as the nature of the complaint, the
source, and date of receipt of the complaint, the name, and address of the complainant, action plan,
and current status (see Annex 1), including complaints both received orally and in writing. If needed,
the GRM Officer will be able to contact complainants directly and guide them through the official
GRM channels for the project.




Figure 17: The grievance process for the project


                                                                                           59 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

6.2.   Project-Specific Construction-Related Procedures
91. During construction, the GRM will also be managed by the Contractors under the supervision
of the construction supervision consultant. Through consultations and disclosure activities, the
Contractors will inform stakeholders about the GRM and how to submit complaints and concerns
about the project. The Contractors will also appoint a ESO to be responsible for grievance redress.
All complaints and corresponding actions undertaken by the Contractor will be recorded in project
monitoring reports. Complaints and claims for damages could be lodged as follows:
 • Verbally: directly to the Contractor’s ESO or representatives at the site offices.
 • In writing: by hand-delivering or posting a written complaint to a designated address of the
   project.
 • By telephone, fax, or e-mails: to the project’s GRM Focal Point or the contractor’s
   representatives.
92. Upon receipt of a complaint, the Contractor’s ESO will register the complaint in a complaint
file and maintain a log until the grievance is resolved (see Annex 1). Immediately upon receipt, four
copies of the complaint will be made: the original for the file, one copy for the ESO, one copy
forwarded to the PPMU, and one copy for the complainant. Information to be recorded in the
complaint log will consist of at least:
 • The date and time of the complaint,
 • The name, address, and contact details of the complainant,
 • A short description of the complaint,
 • Actions taken to address the complaint, including contact persons and findings at each step in
   the complaint redress process,
 • The final resolution of the complaint.
93. The main objective will be to resolve issues on the spot as much as possible. When that is not
possible, complaints will be dealt with within one week. When a complaint cannot be resolved within
15 days, local authorities will be involved (Section 6.1). Such a situation may arise, for example,
when damages are claimed, the to-be-paid amount cannot be resolved, or damage causes need to be
determined.

6.3.    Procedures in Cases of Gender-Based Violence/Sexual exploitation and abuse/Sexual
        harassment
94. To avoid the risk of stigmatization, exacerbation of the mental/psychological harm, or potential
reprisal, the GRM shall have a different and sensitive approach to GBV/SEA/SH-related cases.
Where such a case is reported to the GRM, it should immediately be referred to appropriate service
providers, such as medical and psychological support, emergency accommodation, and/or any other
necessary services. It should also be reported to the PPMU’s GRM Officer, who can advise on
relevant service providers if needed. Data on GBV/SEA/SH cases should not be collected through
the GRM unless operators have been trained on the empathetic, non-judgmental, and confidential
collection of these complaints. Only the nature of the complaint (what the complainant says in her/his
own words) and additional demographic data, such as age and gender, can be collected as usual.




                                                                                           60 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

95. In addition, capacity-building for GRM committees on handling GBV/SEA/SH-related
grievances and awareness campaigns about the GRM for communities will be conducted during the
project implementation.

6.4.     Procedures in cases of conflicts among ethnic groups
96. In the Project, addressing potential conflicts between the Chinese and Khmer ethnic groups is
critical to fostering collaboration and ensuring project success. The project will establish a Grievance
Redress Committee (GRC) composed of equal representation from the Hoa and Khmer communities,
including community elders, local government representatives, and religious leaders from both
groups. This committee will meet biweekly to review grievances and ensure timely resolution.
97. When a conflict arises, the GRC will appoint a neutral mediator, agreed upon by both parties,
to facilitate a resolution. The mediation process will take place in a neutral and culturally significant
location, such as the community center, and will prioritize open dialogue to understand each group’s
concerns. Decisions will be documented and shared transparently with both groups to maintain trust.
98. To protect the privacy of complainants, the GRC will ensure confidentiality throughout the
process. Additionally, regular workshops will be held to educate both communities about the
grievance process, emphasizing its fairness and neutrality. Progress and outcomes will be monitored,
and lessons learned will be incorporated into the GRM to continually improve its effectiveness. This
mechanism will ensure conflicts are resolved respectfully, fostering harmony between the Chinese
and Khmer communities while supporting the project’s objectives.
99. Grievances can be submitted through multiple channels, including community meetings at the
local pagoda, verbal submissions to village leaders, or written complaints deposited in project
grievance boxes located in communal houses. To ensure inclusivity, project staff will provide
interpreters for both Chinese and Khmer languages, and all written materials will be bilingual.

6.5.    Procedures in Cases of Labors’ Concerns
100. The project establishes a workers’ grievance redress mechanism (GRM) in line with national
regulations (Decree 145/2020/ND-CP). Contractors and subcontractors must set up grievance
systems for their workforce before starting work, including multiple channels for submitting
complaints (writing, face-to-face verbally, hotline, etc.), clear response timelines, responsible units,
and grievance tracking. The system will be transparent, confidential, and non-discriminatory,
allowing anonymous submissions. All workers will be informed of the mechanism during induction
and have easy access to information at work sites. Workers can submit complaints verbally or in
writing (through grievance boxes, HR focal points, contractor E&S officers, worker representatives,
or hotlines) on issues such as working conditions, wages, safety, discrimination, or termination.
Complaints will be logged within 2 days, reviewed and classified within 3 days, discussed and
resolved within 7 days, and written responses provided within 10 days. Unresolved complaints can
be escalated to the PPMU, labor authorities, arbitration councils, or court. All grievances and
resolutions will be documented and reported quarterly, and GRM procedures and contacts will be
displayed at construction sites and worker camps. See the project LMP for details.

6.6.   Procedures in Cases of Land Acquisition
101. Complaint concerning land acquisition, physical resettlement, and relevant issues are expected
to be lodged through CPCs. Submission of complaints can be done using emails, postal, hand
delivery, or verbally through direct meetings or telephone. Within 10 days from the date of receiving

                                                                                              61 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

the complaint, the person in charge of complaint resolution shall accept the complaint and notify the
complainant in writing. The time limit for complaint resolution – from the date of acceptance, is 30
days for first-time complaints, and 45 days for the second time and third-time complaints. The statute
of limitation (for initiating a lawsuit) is 30 days from the date the time-limit for complaint resolution
expires, or from the date the complainant receives the complaint resolution decision. In a remote area
where travel is difficult, the statute of limitation can be extended but not exceed 45 days. The
complainant can also initiate a lawsuit at any stage of the grievance resolution process if they disagree
with the complaint resolution decision, or if the case is not resolved following the expiry of the time-
limit for complaint resolution (see the project RP for details).

VII. MONITORING AND REPORTING
102. The SEP will be periodically revised and updated as necessary in the course of the project
implementation to ensure that the information presented is consistent, and up to date, and that the
identified methods of engagement remain appropriate and effective concerning the project context
and specific phases of project development. Any major changes to project-related activities and its
schedule will be duly reflected in the updated SEP.
103. Monthly summaries and internal reports on public grievances, together with the status of
implementation of associated corrective actions will be collated by responsible staff and referred to
the PPMU’s Director. The monthly summaries will provide a mechanism for assessing both the
number and the nature of complaints and requests for information, along with the project’s ability to
address those in a timely and satisfactory manner. The project will make public quarterly project
monitoring reports on website of the province (https://camau.gov.vn), which will include reporting
on the SEP and grievance redress.

7.1.    Internal monitoring
104. The objective of the internal monitoring relating to the SEP is to oversee its implementation to
ensure targeted consultations and disclosure activities are taking place. Internal Monitoring will be
led by the PPMU’s ESO, under the overall guidance of the PPMU’s Director. The involvement of
local communities in monitoring activities should be encouraged whenever possible. Internal
Monitoring will consist of quarterly reports during the implementation of the project, part of the
ESMP or RP monitoring, including public grievances, inquiries, and relating incidents, together with
the status of implementation of associated corrective/preventative actions will be gathered by the
ESO and reported to the PPMU’s Director.
105. Monitoring SEP implementation should focus on:
 • Level of understanding of the project,
 • Community feedback incorporated into project design and planning,
 • Knowledge by stakeholders of project documents, including the GRM and impact mitigation
   plans,
 • Implementation of disclosure and consultation activities as per the SEP,
 • Main grievances and efficacy of GRM,
 • Overall community satisfaction,
 • Minutes of consultation meetings,

                                                                                              62 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

 • Number of staff working on the stakeholder engagement, including at the PMU and its
   consultants or contractors, and
 • Plans for the next month and long-term plans.
106. Monitoring specific to land acquisition activities will be conducted as per the RP.

7.2.   Third Party Monitoring
107. The PPMU will engage an IESMC as a third party to assist the PPMU in monitoring and
evaluation of the implementation of E&S risk and impact management and mitigation measures,
including compensation and resettlement.
108. This consultant will undertake periodic monitoring every six months on the implementation of
E&S risk and impact management in compliance with regulations set forth in the project’s
ESIA/ESMP, RP, SEP, EMDP and LMP. They will also monitor closely the implementation of
project’s GRM.
109. Key tasks of the consultant related to SEP will be:
 • Adequacy of stakeholder engagement, including activities, staffing, and budget.
 • Review of grievances submitted, time to respond, resolution of grievances, and complainant’s
   satisfaction with the process.
 • Interview of sample households to assess satisfaction with stakeholder engagement and
   knowledge of the project and related programs.

7.3.    Community Monitoring
110. As per Decree 84/2015/ND-CP dated 30 September 2015 regarding investment supervision
and assessment, local people can establish a community investment supervision board to conduct
environmental, social, and construction safety supervision voluntarily. During project
implementation, communities that benefit from the project will be encouraged to form a local team
by this Decree to monitor and provide feedback on construction-related issues (if needed).

7.4.  Reporting back to stakeholders
111. The method of reporting back to stakeholders will depend on the stakeholder itself, as follows:
 • Regarding community concerns, the project will report back to the community and other relevant
   stakeholder groups, primarily through public meetings and/or public disclosure which people of
   the community can easily access such as the community’s common house or communal office.
   The feedback report will be shared or disclosed to the concerned community and other
   stakeholders within the timeframe set up in the stakeholder engagement program.
 • Regarding individual concerns, including grievances related to E&S issues such as inaccurate
   measurement of affected land area, undervalued compensation rate, or request for additional
   support for livelihood restoration, the PPMU, and local implementing agencies will report back
   individually, via the same channel through which the grievances were received, for easy follow-
   up of the complainant. Feedback received through the GRM will be responded to in writing and
   sent directly to the complainant via postal mail.
 • Regarding comments of stakeholders both affected and concerned parties on the project design,
   E&S documents, they will be addressed in relevant documents and posted on the websites of the


                                                                                           63 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

    PPMU and PPC, and also posted locally on the public notice board at CPC’s office. Comments
    and questions submitted in association with specific documents disclosed on the above websites
    will be reviewed and responded to promptly as regulated in the EMDP, ESCP, SEP, ESMP, RP,
    and LMP.
 • Regarding comments of national-level stakeholders, an email and/or official letter will be sent
   after workshops on how comments/suggestions were taken into account and/or to inform them
   of documents that are publicly available.
112. In addition, project documents, including monthly, quarterly, or annual reports will be updated
and uploaded as needed, including this SEP, and available on the project website at:
www.bqldactxd.camau.gov.vn. During consultation activities, stakeholders will also be reminded
that these documents are publicly available, as well as the project’s GRM for lodging their concerns,
grievances, and complaints related to the project implementation, including the implementation of
E&S risk and impact management and mitigation measures via public meeting, consultation and
feedback reports.

I.        INTRODUCTION
1.1.   Objectives of the project
1.    The overall objective of the project is to invest in infrastructure, enhance the efficiency and
management capacity of exploitation and aquaculture, and increase the value of aquatic products;
thereby contributing to promoting sustainable growth, adapting to climate change, improving local
people's lives, and deeply integrating into international communities.
2.       Specific objectives of the project are:
     •   To invest in fishing infrastructure, including constructing a storm shelter for fishing vessels;
         creating momentum, enhancing efficiency, and reducing post-harvest losses of fishing
         activities.
     •   To upgrade and complete aquaculture infrastructure.
     •   To build infrastructure and provide equipment for ethnic minority communities involved in
         aquaculture and fishing; contribute to effectively and sustainably protecting fisheries resources,
         increased income, and livelihood stabilization.
     •   To improve management and production organization capacity for combatting illegal,
         unreported, and unregulated (IUU) fishing and sustainably managing fisheries resources.
     •   To reduce post-harvest losses, decrease disease outbreaks, improve shrimp productivity,
         quality, and value; and ensure food safety, environmental protection, and climate change
         adaptation.
     •   To improve fisheries management capacity.

1.2.   Project locations
3.   The project is implemented in the aquaculture areas of 14 communes/wards (24 communes and
wards before the merger) in Soc Trang province (now Can Tho city), including An Thanh Nam, An
Thanh 3 (now Cu Lao Dung Commune), Khanh Hoa Ward, Hoa Dong Commune, Vinh Hiep
Commune (now Khanh Hoa Ward), Vinh Tan Commune, Vinh Phuoc Ward (still named Vinh Phuoc
Ward), Lac Hoa Commune, Ward 1, Ward 2 (Vinh Chau Ward), Vinh Hai Commune (still named
Vinh Hai), Lai Hoa Commune (still named Lai Hoa), Lich Hoi Thuong Commune (still named Lich
Hoi Thuong), Lieu Tu Commune, Vien Binh Commune (now Lieu Tu Commune), Thanh Thoi Thuan
Commune, Thanh Thoi An Commune (now Thanh Thoi An), Trung Binh Commune (now Tran De
Commune), Ngoc To Commune (still named Ngoc To), Hoa Tu 1 Commune, Hoa Tu 2 Commune
(now Hoa Tu Commune), Gia Hoa 2 Commune (now Gia Hoa Commune), Gia Hoa 1 Commune,
and Thanh Phu Commune (now Nhu Gia Commune), see details in Table 49 and Figure 18.

1.3.   Components of the project
16. The project will consist of 3 components providing practical solutions to optimize and improve
the management of resource use for fishing and aquaculture and the construction of the storm shelter
to support local aquaculture areas, including:

Component 1: Investment in fisheries infrastructure improvement
17. Main structural sub-component (SC)s of component 1: (i) SC1.1: upgrading traffic routes; (ii)
SC1.2: Dredging water supply/drainage canals; and (iii) SC1.3: Constructing a storm shelter; (iv)
SC1.4: Building toilets; and (v) SC1.5: Upgrading the PMU’s headquarter and equipment. Details
are as follows:
Sub-component 1.1: Upgrading the current traffic routes and building new traffic routes with a
total length of 73,719.9m

18. The project will construct a new transportation system with a total length of 73,791.9 m in 14
wards and communes in Can Tho city. The components of the road include road surfaces, bridges,
and             culverts               along               the                route              (




19.   Figure 19):
 •    Grade V delta roads with a length of 42,419 meters, a road surface width of 5.5 meters, and a
      roadbed width of 7.5 meters;
 •    Grade VI delta roads with a length of 24,410.4 meters, a road surface width of 4.5 meters, and
      a roadbed width of 6.5 meters;
 •    Grade B rural roads with a length of 6,890.5 meters, a road surface width of 3.5 meters, and a
      roadbed width of 5.0 meters.
 •    Permanent reinforced concrete bridges: (i) bridges of HL93 load capacity and width of 9.0
      meters; (ii) bridges of 0.65HL93 load capacity and width of 7.8 meters; and (iii) bridges of load
      capacity of 3.5T and a width of 4.0 meters.



                                                                                             8|Page
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)




Figure 19: Location of the traffic routes built in the project (before the merger)
Sub-component 1.2: Dredging water supply and drainage canals in aquaculture areas
20. Dredging to clear flow paths and backfill the project roads for 305,504m of water supply and
drainage canals in 14 wards and communes in Can Tho City (belong to districts of Tran De, Cu Lao
Dung, My Xuyen and Vinh Chau town before the merger) with a total volume of dredged materials
of 2,921,865 m3.




Figure 20: Locations of canals to be dredged in the project (before the merger)
Sub-component 1.3: Building a storm shelter area

21. A storm shelter will be built in Vam Ho hamlet, Cu Lao Dung commune (An Thanh Nam
commune before the merger), to safely anchor boats and allow passengers to disembark on the Vam
Ho River. This area will accommodate 200 boats with engine capacities of 90 horsepower or less.

                                                                                       9|Page
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

The                    shelter                   will                   include                     (see




22.   Figure 21 and Figure 22):
 •    A reinforced concrete pile embankment with a length of 520m.
 •    A waiting area covering 400m2.
 •    Supporting facilities such as boat mooring piles spaced 20 meters apart, solar-powered lighting
      poles with a capacity of 70W, drainage culverts, pavement, sidewalks, planting beds, and trees
      along the embankment.




Figure 21: Layout of the storm shelter




Figure 22: Typical cross section of the storm shelter
Sub-component 1.4: Building toilets
23.   Building 1,450 toilets, including septic tanks and flush toilets for 1,450 households, as follows:




                                                                                             10 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

 •    Dimensions for flush toilets: 800x900mm, constructed with 100mm thick brick walls around;
      the base is plastered and finished with 20mm thick Mac 75 cement mortar and coated with three
      layers of pure cement; lined with 4x6 Mac 100 stone concrete.
 •    Dimensions for 3-compartment septic tanks: diameter Ø1,000mm, height 1,500mm, volume
      approximately 0.975m³; the tank consists of three precast concrete pipes, each 45mm thick,
      joined together, one of which has a 45mm thick base plate; the tank partition is made of 1.5m
      high, 45mm thick precast concrete; the cover plate is 1m x 1m, made of 1x2 M200 concrete
      stone, 80mm thick; lined with 4x6 Mac 100 stone concrete.
 •    Waste pipes and drainage pipes: Ø90mm; vent pipes: Ø42mm.
24. The project only provides each toilet with 5m² of 4-zem thick corrugated iron sheets.
Households (self-funding) and private corporations (donations) will both contribute to this sub-
component's private capital.
Sub-component 1.5: Upgrading the PMU’s headquarter and equipment
25. The PMU’s headquarter will be upgraded and equipment installed for its activities in the
project, including computers, laptops, air conditioners, servers, software, etc.

Component 2: Improving management capacity and techniques for sustainable fisheries
development
Sub-component 2.1: Application of cross-cutting technologies in aquaculture
26.   The following activities will be included in the sub-component of the project:
 •    Enhancing capacity for aquaculture officials, households, small and medium enterprises
      o Establishing/Strengthening Aquaculture Cooperatives following the Cooperative Law,
        enhancing the quantity and quality of cooperative activities in the project area.
      o Conducting training sessions on (i) Advanced aquaculture technologies (Biofloc,
        recirculating aquaculture systems, multi-stage farming); (ii) Energy-saving measures; (iii)
        Technologies of water recirculation, waste treatment, and wastewater treatment; (iv)
        Improving capacity in group management; (v) Evaluating and scaling up the successful
        activities.
      o Advanced technology transfer for aquaculture: Biofloc, and recirculating aquaculture
        systems (RAS), etc.
 •    Establishing demonstration models utilizing cutting-edge technologies and implementing best
      management practices in aquaculture:
      o In the communes of Ngoc To, Hoa Tu, Gia Hoa and Nhu Gia belongs to My Xuyen district
        before the merger (2,400ha): (i) Shrimp-rice farming techniques to reduce greenhouse gas
        (GHG) emissions, improve rice quality and shrimp yield, aiming for organic shrimp-rice
        certification; (ii) Supplementing alternative nutrition to reduce feed costs in farming tiger
        shrimp and whiteleg shrimp; and (iii) Pilot models related to plastic waste management and
        reduction.
      o In the communes of Tran De, Lich Hoi Thuong, Lieu Tu and Thanh Thoi An commune
        belongs to Tran De district before the merger (1,200ha): (i) Improving the shrimp farming
        process through multi-stage farming, intensive farming, etc., suitable for each area in the
        project area, combined with the development of herbal shrimp farming, Biofloc technology,

                                                                                          11 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

        application of energy-saving measures, renewable energy utilization (solar energy, biogas,
        etc.), water recirculation technology, wastewater, and solid waste treatment, application of
        Aquaculture Stewardship Council (ASC) and GlobalGAP standards; and (ii) Diversified
        farming models linked with value chains.
     o In the communes of Vinh Hai and Lai Hoa and wards of Khanh Hoa, Vinh Phuoc, and Vinh
       Chau belongs to Vinh Chau town before the merger (6,700ha): (i) Refining the farming
       process (multi-stage shrimp farming, intensive farming, etc.) suitable for each area in the
       project area, combined with the development of herbal shrimp farming, Biofloc technology,
       semi-biofloc; Application of energy-saving measures; Utilization of renewable energy
       (solar energy, wind power, biogas, etc.); Application of water recirculation technology and
       waste treatment; Application of ASC and Global GAP standards; (ii) Diversified farming
       techniques (crab, fish, artemia, clams, etc.) linked with value chains, supplemental nutrition
       techniques to reduce feed costs in farming tiger shrimp and whiteleg shrimp; and (iii) Pilot
       models related to plastic waste management and reduction.
Sub-component 2.2: Raising management capacity and advanced technologies for fisheries
exploitation
 •   Raising awareness among fishermen about protecting fisheries resources:
     o Conducting training/seminars: related to Fisheries Law, Illegal, unreported, and unregulated
       (IUU).
     o Information dissemination: developing fisheries websites, installing loud speaker systems
       at fishing ports, etc.; designing communications materials, implementing fisheries action
       programs.
     o Developing plastic waste management models for fishing vessels, fishing ports, and fish
       landing sites: (i) Conducting communication campaigns, forming voluntary fishermen
       teams to collect marine debris; (ii) Establishing fishermen teams to collect marine debris to
       fishing ports; (iii) Launching plastic waste collection movements, including “exchanging
       garbage to gifts” program; (iv) Supporting waste sorting bins at fishing ports, fish landing
       sites; and (v) Establishing “Materials Recovery Facility” (MRF) model and green house
       model at fishing ports and fish landing sites.
 •   Supporting livelihoods with a focus on job change for coastal fishing communities:
     o Surveying the demand for vocational changes among fishermen.
     o Supporting vocational change models: aquaculture under forest canopy linked with
       ecotourism; upgrading small boats to provide tourism services; vocational training
       (focusing on jobs supporting fisheries exploitation and aquaculture).
 •   Enhancing fisheries exploitation, monitoring and supervision capacity:
     o Training the use of electronic catch documentation and traceability (eCDT) software for
       sustainable and traceable fisheries.
  • Providing necessary equipment, such as computers and scanners, etc.
     o Improving sanitation conditions, ensuring food safety, and enhancing the efficiency of fish
       ports:



                                                                                          12 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

       o Training for purchasing and preprocessing facilities on food safety regulations aiming to
         ensure Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP), Sanitation Standard Operating Procedures
         (SSOP) standards, etc., and methods of product preservation for each fishing target.
       o Providing fish ports with the necessary equipment (for fishing storage, waste sorting, etc.):
         Applying a PU-based seafood preservation model for offshore fishing vessels.

Component 3: Project management
27. This component will facilitate efficient project management. It will encompass recurrent
expenditures (mostly from the counterpart fund) for the operations of the PMU, including workshops,
training, project implementation, monitoring and evaluation costs, and consultant costs.

1.4.     Project Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
1.4.1. Positive impacts
28. The benefits of the project are long-term rather than short-term. If the endeavor is successful,
it should not only benefit the current number of fishermen, aquaculture farmers, and service providers
involved in the fishing and shrimp farming in the project area, but also make a significant contribution
to the overall sustainable development of the national fishery industry as follow:
  •    By taking resilience to climate change and extreme weather events into the design process of
       the project items, especially the storm shelter, it is possible to increase its lifespan and decrease
       the operation and maintenance (O&M) cost.
  •    The storm shelter construction will help commercial fishing vessels in Can Tho city and
       neighboring areas anchor safely during storms, reducing fishing costs and increasing value-
       added products by shortening distances between ports and fishing grounds. This will increase
       income and improve the nutritional value of aquatic products.
  •    Fishermen will be able to use the fishing facilities that have better sanitation and weather
       conditions. This lowers the occupational health risks, improves the quality and value of fish
       production, and lowers physical losses by making sure fish are caught on time and stored
       properly.
  •    Improved waste management in the storm shelter and aquaculture farms reduces water pollution
       and sediment discharge into water bodies. As a result, in the long run, the project should help
       to improve the water quality of the project area.
  •    Implementing Good Agriculture Practices (GAP) and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
       principles, along with the introduction of bio-security practices and disease-free seeds, can
       foster an environment that is less conducive to pests and diseases in aquaculture ponds. This,
       in turn, can reduce the reliance on antibiotics and chemicals in aquaculture, safeguard the
       environment, mitigate losses caused by disease outbreaks, enhance food security, and improve
       occupational health and safety for farming-based laborers.
  •    Increased productivity of shrimp aquaculture areas will help farmers expand their shrimp
       farming adapting to the challenges of rising sea level.
  •    The project will also generate environmental and social benefits through a variety of other
       mechanisms, including (i) improved awareness and concern for environmental and social issues
       on the part of beneficiaries, local communities and districts; (ii) increasing the availability of


                                                                                                13 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

     staff conversant with environmental and social issues throughout the project area through
     training of Department of Argiculture and Environment (DAE)’s senior staff as environmental
     and social management specialists and aquaculture extension staff at the DEA in environmental
     and social screening; (iii) awareness raising among senior governmental staff on benefits of
     adopting environmental and social principles in sustainability of development projects, thus
     assisting in main streaming environmental and social issues in project planning and
     implementation.

1.4.2. Negative impacts and risks
29. The implementation of the project will cause potential negative environmental and social
impacts and risks during pre-construction, construction and operational phases, as follows:
30. During pre-construction phase: Impacts and risks during pre-construction phase are as
follows:
 •   Safety risk relating to UXO. Vietnam in general and the project area in particular undergone
     civil wars which were ended in 1975. Although the land has been used in the last 45 years since
     the war ended, some UXOs may remain underground. If disturbed by construction activities
     such as site clearance, ground leveling and excavation, the UXO may pose hazard to the
     workers, damage to construction works.
 •   Land acquisition. The implementation of the project will unavoidably involve land acquisition
     (both permanent and temporary) in the project area. This has a negative impact on the lives,
     incomes and livelihoods of affected people, especially those severely affected (relocation,
     significant loss of productive land and/or business activities/incomes, and vulnerable groups).
 •   Impacts on income/livelihood: Local residents and affected parties are often involved in small-
     business activities and/or engage in fisheries and aquaculture activities where they live,
     therefore, relocation of affected households may result in loss of customers if affected
     households are relocated or loss of their existing sources of income/livelihood too far from their
     current location and place of business.
31. These impacts and risks can be mitigated by applying the approved resettlement plan (RP) of
the project and the project owner will contract with relevant unit to clear UXOs before commencing
construction activities, and must closely monitor and consult with local people about the land
acquisition and clearance and UXO clearance.
32. During construction phase: Constructing infrastructure would involve activities such as site
clearance, ground leveling, demolishing the old structures, construction of worker camps and site
offices, gathering materials and machines at working sites, soil excavation, transportation the
construction materials to construction sites, preparation of construction materials such as pile casting
and concrete mixing, waste disposal, structure construction, equipment installation for finalizing
works, etc. These activities would take place in the land area. For the storm shelter, construction of
bridges, canal dredging, construction activities will also take place in water. These include dredging,
piling activities, construction of embankments, etc. Environmental and social impacts and risks of
these construction activities are summarized below.
 •   Common construction related impacts and risk: These include increased level of dust, gases
     emission, noise, vibration, loss of vegetation cover and trees, waste and wastewater generation,
     water quality reduction affecting aquatic lives, soil erosion sedimentation and localized

                                                                                             14 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

         flooding risks. On the social side, the main impact would include traffic disturbances, increased
         traffic safety risks, damage of existing infrastructure services and interruption of related public
         services such as power supply, damage of rural roads due to heavy transportation; occupational
         and community health and safety risks, gender-based violence (GBV), Sexual Exploitation or
         Abuse (SEA) and sexual harassment (SH) in relation to labor influx; exposure to cultural
         heritage and other social issues. It is anticipated that the project items mostly take place in
         modified habitats or in areas that are heavily disturbed by human activities.
     •   Other key risks and impacts during construction associated with the construction of storm
         shelter, bridges, canal dredging:
         o Water, soil pollution risks and impacts to aquatic life due to improper management and
           disposal of dredged materials. Dredging process may cause serious environmental risks and
           impacts if is not managed carefully.
         o Risks on soil erosion and subsidence during dredging and embankment process.
33. These impacts and risks are localized, short term during construction period, and avoidable if
geology and hydrology survey are carefully considered during the detailed design, and via the
application of good construction method and the ESCOPs.
34. During operation phase: impacts and risks during project operation phase are summarised as
follows:
 −   Operation of the storm shelter: In terms of environmental aspect, the impacts and risks associated
     with the storm shelter activities are air and water pollution due to gases emission, generation of
     solid waste and wastewater from fishing ships, landing services and administrative offices. For
     the social side, the impacts and risks during the operation of the storm shelter are associated with
     occupational and community health and safety. Beside the negative impact on water quality and
     aquatic life and riverbank erosion due to fishing ships’ movement and anchorage, potential E &S
     risks and impacts during operation of the storm shelter are associated with waste managements
     and social issues such as conflicts between fishermen and local people during the time of
     anchoring.
 −   Operation of roads and bridges cause an increase in traffic accidents and greenhouse gases
     emission.
35.      The impacts and risks can be mitigated measures during the operation phase.

2.       OBJECTIVES OF THE STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN
36. The objective of this stakeholder engagement plan (SEP) is to define a program for stakeholder
engagement, including public information disclosure, grievance redress mechanism (GRM), and
consultation, throughout the implementation and operation of the project. It specifies how the Can
Tho city Project Management Unit (PMU) and contractors will communicate with stakeholders, as
well as a mechanism for people to express concerns, provide feedback, or file complaints about the
project, and contractors. Consultation and engagement with stakeholders are critical to ensure smooth
collaboration between the project and local communities, as well as to minimize and mitigate
environmental and social risks and impacts of the project, thereby contributing to the project's
success.



                                                                                                 15 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

37. The project stakeholders will engage at different stages, including during the initial design,
detailed design, pre-construction, construction, and operation. Engagement will vary during the life
of the project and this SEP will be dynamic and flexible to these changes. The SEP should be read
together with other project documents such as ESIA/ESMP, LMP, EMDP, RP, and ESCP. This SEP
is a living document that will be updated once consultations have taken place, incorporating minutes
of meetings and relevant comments from project stakeholders.

3.          STAKEHOLDER IDENTIFICATION AND ANALYSIS
38. Project stakeholders are people and entities who have a role in the project, or could be affected
by the project, or who are interested in the project. Project stakeholders are defined as individuals,
groups or other entities who:
     (v)     are impacted or likely to be impacted directly or indirectly, positively or adversely, by the
             project (also known as “affected parties”); and
     (vi)    may have an interest in the project (“interested parties”). They include individuals or groups
             whose interests may be affected by the project and who have the potential to influence the
             project outcomes in any way.
39. Cooperation and negotiation with the stakeholders throughout the project development often
also requires the identification of people within the groups who act as legitimate representatives of
their respective stakeholder group, i.e., the individuals who have been entrusted by their fellow group
members by advocating the groups’ interests in the process of engagement with the project.
Community representatives may provide helpful insight into the local settings and act as main
conduits for dissemination of the project-related information and as a primary communication/liaison
link between the project and targeted communities and their established networks. Verification of
stakeholder representatives (i.e., the process of confirming that they are legitimate and genuine
advocates of the community they represent) remains an important task in establishing contact with
the community stakeholders. Legitimacy of the community representatives can be verified by talking
informally to a random sample of community members and heeding their views on who can be
representing their interests in the most effective way.

3.1. Methodology
40. To meet best practice approaches, the project will apply the following principles for stakeholder
engagement:
     •      Openness and life-cycle approach: public consultations for the project will be arranged during
            the whole lifecycle, carried out openly, free of external manipulation, interference, coercion, or
            intimidation.
     •      Informed participation and feedback: information will be provided to and widely distributed
            among all stakeholders in an appropriate format; opportunities are provided for communicating
            stakeholders’ feedback, and for analyzing and addressing comments and concerns.
     •      Inclusiveness and sensitivity: stakeholder identification will be undertaken to support better
            communications and build effective relationships. The participation process for the project will
            be inclusive. All stakeholders at all times will be encouraged to get involved in the consultation
            process. Equal access to information will be provided to all stakeholders. Sensitivity to
            stakeholders’ needs is the key principle underlying the selection of engagement methods.

                                                                                                   16 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

      Special attention will be given to vulnerable groups, in particular women, displaced persons,
      persons with disabilities, youth, elderly.

3.2. Stakeholder Identification
41. The stakeholder mapping for the project demonstrates that focus is placed on the core project
stakeholders and beneficiaries as well as host communities of the project. Therefore, the project
coordinates endeavors via facilitator/consultant teams to prompt and comprehensive involvement of
these groups (

Figure 24. Stakeholders’ influence and interest analysis
45. Stakeholders of the project are people or groups who are directly or indirectly affected by the
project, as well as those who may have an interest in the project and/or the ability to influence project
outcomes, either positively or negatively. For effective and tailored engagement, the project’s
stakeholders can be divided into the following 3 core categories:

3.3. Affected Parties
46. One of the primary goals of the project is to identify stakeholders on which the project can have
negative impact such as households or businesses who may be impacted by land acquisition,
livelihoods and business activities. However, the project may influence the activities of various
stakeholders in positive or negative ways. Thus, a list of key stakeholder groups can be identified as
potentially affected parties. These should be engaged throughout the life of the project and impacts
on them should be monitored and mitigated. Project-affected parties include those likely to be
affected by the project because of actual impacts or potential risks to their physical environment,
health, security, cultural practices, well-being, or livelihoods. The following groups can be identified
as potential project-affected stakeholders:
47. People who are physically or economically displaced by the project: The main groups of
project-affected parties in the stakeholder mapping are the people physically or economically
displaced by the project, and persons who reside or have land and non-land assets in the Right of
Way (ROW) of the proposed investments. The following parties are identified as directly affected:
 •   People physically or economically displaced and people who reside or have land and non-land
     assets in the ROW of the proposed investments:

                                                                                              19 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

     o People and companies who will lose their land.
     o People and businesses with structures on land tha will be relocated.
     o People and companies with trees and crops that will be affected.
     o People and companies with surface water (aquaculture) that will be affected.
 •   Fishermen/fishing vessel owners.
 •   Woman (including women-headed households with dependents, female members of the
     affected households and companies).
 •   Ethnic Minorities (EMs).
 •   Other vulnerable households such as poor/near-poor households; landless households; persons
     with physical or mental disabilities (loss of working ability); people living alone, elderly; and
     social policy category (families of martyrs, war invalids, and sick soldiers, etc.).
 •   People who will have access to water from dredged canals for aquaculture where the canals
     will be dredged.
 •   People who will have access to the roads and bridges for their travelling and the movement of
     their commodities.
48. Communities in the vicinity of the project areas: Local community and companies who are
likely to be exposed to temporary air pollution, traffic difficulties, and other potential risks such as
traffic safety, traffic disturbance, wastewater discharge, gender-based violence (GBV), sexual
exploitation and abuse (SEA)/Sexual harassment (SH), health risks as a result of labour influx, etc.
49. Executing/implementing agencies, relevant City’s agencies, and administrative
authorities at all levels: These groups include Can Tho City People’s Committee (Can Tho City
PC), Can Tho City Project Management Unit (PMU), Department of Finance (DOF), Department of
Construction (DOC), Department of Agriculture and Environment (DAE), Department of Ethnic and
Religious Affairs (DOERA), Department of Home Affairs (DOHA), Department of Science and
Technology (DOST), Can Tho City Land Clearance and Compensation Committee; Can Tho City
Land Fund Development Center (LFDC), 24 Ward/Commune People’s Committee (W/CPC)s in the
project area, and public service utilities (such as telecommunication, electric power, water supply,
wastewater, traffic management).
50. Project beneficiaries: Project beneficiaries are large in number (they are positively affected
by the project). These include individuals, households, groups, public and private sectors who benefit
from the construction of roads, bridges, dredged canals, storm shelter.
51. Table 20 indicates potential project-affected parties of the project.

3.4. Other interested parties
52. The project’s “other interested parties” refer to individuals, groups, or organization with an
interest in the project, due to either the project location, its characteristics, its impacts, or matters
related to public interest. The following stakeholders are listed as project interested parties:
 •    Central level: Ministries and government agencies include the Office of Government (OOG)
      and interest line-ministries, those are playing a key role in their functions and responsibilities
      and sectoral management, such as the Ministry of Agriculture and Environment (MAE),
      Ministry of Finance (MOF), Ministry of Construction (MOC), Ministry of Home Affairs
      (MOHA), Ministry of Ethnic and Religious Affairs (MOERA), Ministry of Health (MOH),
      Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST).
 •    Provincial level: Can Tho City PC, Can Tho City PMU, DOF, DOC, DAE, DOHA, DOERA,
      DOH, DOST, City LFDC, City Site Clearance and Compensation Committee (SCCC).
 •    Commune level: 24 project W/CPCs in the project area.
 •    Mass organizations include 6 organizations at all levels as Women’s Union, Youth Union,
      Fatherland Front, Farmers’ Union, Veterans Associations, and Trade Union.
 •    Mass media include local, regional and national news agencies (TV, broadcasting, online
      newspaper) who may be interested in project activities, project progress, and impact on local
      development.
 •    Commune’s citizen groups benefit from additional income thanks to providing services to
      construction workers (e.g., foods and other essential supplies, etc.). Those who may become
      involved in construction and operation as seasonal workers are also those who may be interested
      in the project, and those who are interested in supervision of the construction of the project
      components.
 •    Development agencies and/or NGOs: Currently, there are no NGOs working in Project
      City/communes. However, NGOs become interested in the project during its implementation,
      this SEP will be updated to reflect their concerns (if any).
 •    Public service utilities, including telecommunication, electric power, water supply,
      wastewater treatment, waste collection, waterways traffic management, commune health
      centers, etc.
 •    Experts, universities, institutes, including Research Institute for Aquaculture 2
 •    Private companies: Service providers for Gender-based violation (GBV)/Sexual exploitation
      and abuse (SEA)/ Sexual harassment (SH), other service providers (tourist centers, hotels,
      motels, etc.) for contracted workers.
53.   Table 21 indicates potential project-affected parties of the project.

3.5. Disadvantaged/Vulnerable Individuals and Groups
54. Disadvantaged/vulnerable individuals and groups may be affected disproportionately - either
adversely or positively, by the project. Due to low socioeconomic profile, their voices may not be
heard in the formal decision-making process or included in consultation process but their feedback
are not solicited in a manner that offers sufficient understanding of how they are affected, how they
would respond, and what support they may need to avoid or mitigate such impact – from their own
perspective. Identification of vulnerable individuals and groups, including those who are stigmatized,
is an important step to effectively engaging them in the consultation throughout the project cycle.
This enables Can Tho City PMU to work out measures to avoid adverse impact, or minimize or
mitigate such impact, if avoidance is not possible. Inclusion of disadvantaged/vulnerable individuals
and groups - at the early stage of project planning, can also assist in project design that provide them
opportunities to restore and improve their livelihoods.
55. The project needs to make special efforts to ensure vulnerable and disadvantaged stakeholders
can be engaged. In this project, these groups include:
 • Ethnic minorities (EMs).
 • Elderly people and/or social policy householders (invalid soldiers, martyrs, heroic mothers).
 • Poor/near-poor families.
 • People with disabilities and their caretakers.
 • Women, particularly in women-headed households or single mothers with underage/ dependent
   children.
 • Unemployed people.
56. During project preparation, identified disadvantaged/vulnerable groups have been invited to
consultation to solicit their feedbacks on project impact and risks, and their proposed mitigation
measures. During project implementation, based on the nature and the magnitude of impact,
consultations will be continued to solicit their opinion, particularly prior to construction when project
impact become known and the affected vulnerable have sufficient time to better understand the
project impact and base on that provide meaningful feedback. Table 22 indicates disadvantaged or
vulnerable groups encountered under the project.

57. As mentioned above, there is a wide range of project stakeholders that are affected by and are
interested in project implementation. Different stakeholders have different levels of interest and
influence on project design and implementation. Therefore, it is important to understand: (i) the level
of interest of each stakeholder as to the project’s investment activities; (ii) the impact magnitude of
the project on them, particularly those adversely affected, and (iii) the extent to which the stakeholder
influences the project design, as well as implementation process and outcome.
58. Under this project, stakeholders highly relevant to the project preparation and implementation
include governmental agencies and the community at large. These stakeholders are affected by the
project and at the same time can influence project design and implementation - to various extents.
Their interest in project investments also varies at different stages of the project cycle, and as such
exert different levels of influence on the project design and implementation process.
59. Based on the roles, responsibilities, and the potential interest of the key stakeholders obtained
from initial consultations with them, Table 23 summarizes the estimated levels of interest, impact,
and influence of each stakeholder group, of which:
 (vii) “impact” means the project's impacts (both positive and negative impacts) on each key group
       of the stakeholder during phases of the project.
 (viii) “influence” means people’s influence on the project implementation.
 (ix) Level of “impact” and “influence” will be assessed on 3 different levels: High; Moderate;
      and Low.
60. It is noted that these levels of interest, impact, and influence may change throughout the project.
However, the current dynamics of stakeholders’ interest, impact, and influence, as described in Table
23, are useful for informing how each stakeholder should be engaged - in a meaningful manner, to
ensure they contribute to the overall environmental and social performance process, and outcome of
the project.

STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)


4.      STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PROGRAM
61. In order to achieve the objectives of the SEP, the project will develop an engagement program
with the following purposes:
     • Provide meaningful information in a format and language that is readily understandable.
     • Provide information in advance of consultation activities when possible.
     • Disseminate information in a manner and location easy for stakeholders to access.
     • Establish a two-way dialogue that allows the project and stakeholders to exchange views and
       information, and have issues heard and addressed.
     • Ensure inclusiveness in the representation of views, including those of women, the elderly,
       people living with a disability, and other vulnerable people as necessary.
     • Ensure any obstacles to participation that are identified are removed so that views of different
       stakeholders can be captured.
     • Ensure there are clear mechanisms for responding to people’s concerns, suggestions, and/or
       grievances.
     • Incorporate feedback into the project, and report back to stakeholders.
     • Monitor stakeholder engagement activities and include project stakeholders in monitoring to the
       extent possible.
     • Incorporate stakeholder engagement as part of the project management responsibilities and
       ensure staff in the PMU and its consultants are equipped with specific responsibilities and
       budget.

4.1. Summary of Stakeholder Engagement during Project Preparation
During the preparation of the project Stakeholder Engagement Framework
62. Numerous meetings were held by the Central Project Management Board (CPMU) at 12 project
provinces in May and June 2020, as part of the Stakeholder Engagement Framework preparation for
the Sustainable Fisheries Development Project. The consultation meeting with Soc Trang province
(now Can Tho City) was held on June 09, 2020.
63. The purpose of the consultation was to familiarize stakeholders with the project's scope and to
determine acceptable communication and consultation procedures throughout the project lifetime. In
addition, The E&S documents developed for the project, such as the ESMF, RPF, EMPF, SEF, LMP,
and ESCP, were also consulted in order to obtain feedback from participants for future improvements.
64. Participants were given general information about the project (objectives, components, project
footprint, potential risks and impacts, financing source, implementation arrangement) during the
consultation. The new applicable World Bank ESF, as well as its E&S standards and criteria, were
also introduced to the participants.
65. The frameworks are no longer applicable due to changes in project implementation
arrangements and design. However, the outcomes of previous consultations have been incorporated
into the overall stakeholder engagement process. This stakeholder engagement plan reflects and takes
into account all relevant feedback from those consultations.


                                                                                            35 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

4.2. Summary of Key Project Stakeholders’ Needs
68. Various stakeholders require different methods of communication and consultation. For local
government officials, since a well-established communication protocol has been in place, the
communications with them to arrange consultation sessions are through means such as couriers,
electronic mails, telephone conversations, face-to-face meetings, etc. For the community,
communication is typically through courier at village level or paying a home visit to those who are
better approached through direct contact, such as people with disabilities, the elderly, etc.
69. Table 26 summarizes the stakeholders’ needs for consultation in the project and proposed
methods to accommodate the needs of different stakeholders.

4.3. Strategy for Consultation
70. The project will apply a participatory and consultative approach involving meaningful
engagement with different stakeholders. Project consultations will be a two-way process of dialogue
between the project and its stakeholders, including on issues such as project objectives; working
conditions for contractors; engagement of women; ways to mitigate potential project impacts, and
risks in particular to vulnerable groups. This will depend on the stakeholders as well as the project
timing. For this project, consultation strategies will be proposed for each stage of the project, details
are as follows:

4.4. Consultation strategies
71. Project preparation (before project appraisal). Since the details of some proposed project
investments are unknown at this stage, consultations during project preparation will focus on a
broader range of stakeholders to understand their general interests and concerns about the project and
its potential positive and negative impacts and inform project design. This is also the stage where
potential vulnerable/disadvantaged groups relevant to the project will be identified.
72. Project implementation. At this stage, the details of proposed project components and specific
project-affected stakeholders will be determined. The consultation will focus more specifically on
people who are impacted by the project, especially those adversely impacted, to address their
concerns through the implementation of project-specific E&S plans. In addition, the project will
ensure that the GRM is disseminated and active to promptly address any grievances. Special attention
will be paid to vulnerable/disadvantaged groups to ensure inclusion, non-discrimination, and access
to project benefits. Consultations will also focus on job opportunities, specific action plans, etc.
73. Project operation. At this stage, the consultation will focus on feedback from stakeholders,
particularly affected ones, on the performance of E&S impact and risk mitigations, results of
grievance resolution conducted during the implementation stage as well as general project operations,
including implementation of the storm shelter operation. The consultation on employment
opportunities for operation and maintenance (O&M) of the project works will be also conducted.
Also, areas for improvement and lessons learned should be identified for the project. Furthermore,
dissemination and reporting back to stakeholders on the outcome of the monitoring and evaluation
of the project will be also arranged.

4.5. Consultation methods
74. The methods used to conduct consultations will have a direct impact on the outcome of the
project consultation strategy. Therefore, these methods should be designed to account for the diverse
characteristics of various stakeholder groups. The following consultation methods will be used for
this project:
75. National consultations. National consultations are particularly useful to target government
representatives, NGOs, and other groups who have an interest in the project and may also have the
ability to influence it. These types of consultations are also useful at the project design stage, and
annually to inform stakeholders of a project’s progress. National consultations may be conducted
face-to-face or virtual in a meeting format and may involve break-out groups or other methods to
ensure there is ample time for group discussion and questions and answers.
76. Local consultations. Local consultations are the main way to reach project affected
stakeholders and will involve face-to-face group meetings. In some cases, one-on-one consultations

                                                                                              49 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

or FGD may also be carried out, especially to reach and capture the views of vulnerable or
disadvantaged groups. Particular emphasis should be placed on seeking out the voice of women and
vulnerable groups, such as through female only FGDs. In the case of women, the project will ensure
consultation activities are conducted at a time that is convenient to them and that activities such as
FGDs are run by women if possible (particularly for sensitive topics like health or sexual issues).
77. One important way to engage stakeholders will be through training on important topics related
to the project such as gender, the project’s GRM, labor rights (for both men and women), child labor,
HIV/AIDS and STDs, gender-based violence, sexual harassment, and sexual exploitation and abuse.
Since some of these topics are quite sensitive, particular measures may need to be in place, such as
ensuring some training courses are done in female-only or male-only groups, or that they are
facilitated by men or women, as appropriate. Training may be delivered by Can Tho City PMU/its
consultants.
78. Accessibility issues will also be taken into account, in particular, to ensure people with a
disability can be part of consultations, as well as the specific needs of other groups such as the elderly,
and poor groups.
79. Consultations with project workers. The project will strive to ensure that unskilled workers
come from the community as much as possible, to minimize the influx of labor. Consultations will
encourage local workers to apply for available jobs, including EMs, women and vulnerable or
disadvantaged groups. Contractors and their staff will need to be well consulted and trained in
sensitive issues, including respectful behavior, Codes of Conduct, GBV (including SEA and SH),
HIV/AIDS and STDs, and worker’s GRM, LMP. They also need to be well aware of construction
needs and schedules. Training, on-site interviews, and regular feedback with Can Tho City PMU
and/or CSC will be used as methods of consultation with them.
80.   Consultation activities proposed for the project are summarized in Table 27.

4.6. Stakeholder Engagement Methods to be Used in the Next Stages
82. Public/community meetings. The Can Tho City PMU will organize meetings in each project
commune with leaders, PAPs, and other interested parties. Meetings will be open-housing events
where the PMU will present all project information and people will be invited to make comments
and express any concerns. A range of consultations with different stakeholders will be conducted and
documented, and community level meetings will also be conducted with men and women (including
vulnerable households) to better understand needs, expectations, and concerns of these population
groups in relation to the project. The feedback received at these meetings will be documented along
with measures that the project will take to address the feedback received. Meetings in each commune
will also be organized on a quarterly basis.
83. Communication materials. Written information will be disclosed to the public via a variety
of communication materials including leaflets, posters, etc. The PMU will also update its website
regularly (at least on a quarterly basis) with key project updates and reports on the project’s
performance in Vietnamese. The website will also provide information about the grievance
mechanism for the project.
84. Training, workshops. Training in a variety of E&S issues will be provided to the PMU, and
possibly other relevant government or non-government service providers. Issues covered will include
sensitization to inclusion/exclusion, labor issues, gender-based violence risks.
85. Grievance Redress Mechanism. In compliance with the World Bank’s ESS10, a project-
specific grievance mechanism will be maintained to handle complaints and issues. This will be
integrated into a country system on GRM that is available to citizens. Dedicated communication
materials (specifically, a GRM brochure) will be developed to help local residents become familiar
with the grievance redress channels and procedures. The PMU will coordinate with administrative
authorities to maintain a grievance register in order to capture and track grievances from submission
to resolution and communication with complainants.
86. The initial effort to resolve grievances to the complainant’s satisfaction will be undertaken by
administrative levels. These will be details in Section 7 below. Grievances may be submitted
anonymously, but in that case, it cannot be known if the complainant is satisfied with the resolution.
The PMU’s website, posters, and leaflets will include clear information on how feedback, questions,
comments, concerns, and grievances can be submitted by any stakeholder. It will also provide
information on the way grievances will be handled, both in terms of process and deadlines.

4.7. Strategy to Incorporate the Views of Vulnerable Groups
87. In the project, vulnerable and disadvantaged groups may include, but not limited to, the
following groups: (i) EMs, (ii) Elderly and/or social policy households (injured ex-soldier, heroic
mothers); (iii) Poor/near-poor households; (iv) People with disabilities and their careers; and (v)
Women, in particular female-headed households, or single mothers with young children. However,
these groups in the project-affected communities will be further identified and consulted throughout
the project cycle. They may have a limited understanding of project information or participation in
the consultation process.
88. The views of vulnerable/disadvantaged groups will be sought during the consultation process.
Accommodation and specific measures may be taken to remove obstacles to their participation, such
as:

                                                                                           54 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

 •   Providing large print,
 •   Choosing accessible venues that are easy to access (such as with ramps),
 •   Providing transportation for people in remote areas to the nearest meeting,
 •   Having small, focused meetings where vulnerable stakeholders are more comfortable asking
     questions or raising concerns,
 •   Good facilitation to ensure meaningful participation in the consultation,
 •   Using appropriate translators.
 •   Respecting traditions and religious practices, such as Theravada Buddhism and local festivals
     like Chol Chnam Thmay and Ok Om Bok.
 •   Involving community leaders and Buddhist monks, as they hold significant influence and trust
     within Khmer communities.
 •   Using Khmer language or bilingual communication for clarity and inclusion.
 •   Leveraging pagodas as community hubs for meetings, information sharing, and building trust.
 •   Incorporating visual tools like diagrams and videos to account for varying literacy levels.
 •   Facilitating participatory activities, such as small-group discussions, storytelling, or hands-on
     demonstrations, in informal settings.
 •   Acknowledging cultural sensitivities, such as gender roles, by ensuring women and other
     groups are included in discussions.
 •   Celebrating milestones with local traditions, such as Khmer music, dance, or community
     events, to strengthen collaboration and trust.
89. Also, GRM will be disclosed to the identified vulnerable groups based on their own
characteristics. For instance, information on the project, project risks and effects, and grievance
procedures will be delivered at home to people with disabilities and verbally to those with impaired
vision. A leaflet containing project information and GRM, as well as contact details, will be
distributed to vulnerable groups, allowing them to contact directly or ask someone else (e.g., a
caregiver) to contact them on their behalf.
90. Since vulnerable groups have been identified and consulted during project preparation, they
will be consulted again during the next stage of the project based on their specific requirements. For
instance, people using wheelchair may ask for the road sideway to have a ramp for wheelchair access,
or request contractors to stockpile construction materials in a manner that allows access for people
having difficulties moving or children for safety.

5.2. Implementation Arrangements
96. The SEP will be the responsibility of the Director and ESO of Can Tho City PMU. They will
guide the process of stakeholder engagement throughout the preparation and implementation of the
project. Certain parts of the SEP will also be the responsibility of contractors or consultants, and the
ESO of Can Tho City PMU will oversee this work as appropriate. The ESO under the guidance of
the Director will be responsible for:
 •     Leading, or supervising consultations, as per the SEP.
 •     Leading, or supervising the disclosure of information, as per the SEP.
 •     Recording and reporting on grievance resolution, as per the SEP.
 •     Reviewing monthly monitoring reports provided by contractors and/or consultants.
 •     Regularly reporting to the Director.
97. For effective implementation, the PMU needs to engage various stakeholders identified in
Section 3. However, the participation of governmental agencies, such as PCs and their functional
agencies at three levels (provincial, and commune), and the community in the project area, are key
to effective SEP implementation (Table 32).
