6.       GRIEVANCE REDRESS MECHANISM
98. The grievance mechanism seeks to resolve concerns promptly, using an understandable process
that is culturally appropriate and readily accessible at no cost. Grievances can be submitted if
someone believes the project is having a detrimental impact on the community, the environment, or
their quality of life. Stakeholders may also submit their comments and suggestions on the project.
99. The Can Tho City PMU’s ESO will be responsible for overseeing those grievances that are
received and resolved in a fair, objective, and constructive manner all concerns or complaints raised
by project stakeholders. The key principles of the grievance mechanism are to ensure that:
     •   The basic rights and interests of affected people are protected.
     •   The concerns of affected people arising from the project implementation process are adequately
         addressed.
     •   Entitlements or livelihood support for affected people are provided on time and in accordance
         with project documents, such as ESMP, EMDP, RP.
     •   Affected people are aware of their rights to access grievance procedures free of charge for the
         above purposes.
100. Based on the SEP, wherever possible, the project team shall seek to resolve complaints as soon
as possible and thus avoid the escalation of issues. GRM shall not impede access to the country’s
judicial or administrative remedies. Stakeholders can approach a court of law at any time and
independent of the project-level grievance redress process. Along with the World Bank requirements
based on ESS10, the GRM will be in line with the Vietnamese national legislation, in particular the
Law on Complaints (2011) and the Law on Denunciation (2018).
101. The Can Tho City PMU shall establish the GRM. The PMU shall appoint a GRM focal point
to collaborate with local authorities in handling project-related grievances. The design of the GRM
shall take into consideration the views of affected stakeholders and what is considered an access
mechanism. The PMU shall be required to disclose and explain to all stakeholders, in particular,
affected stakeholders and vulnerable and disadvantaged ones, the procedure for complaints filing as
early as possible and regularly throughout the project cycle. The grievance mechanism shall be
available at no cost, and it is important that it be easily accessible, with special attention paid to
accessibility for disadvantaged and vulnerable individuals or groups.

                                                                                                           62 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

102. The PMU will ensure that grievances are registered, tracked, and resolved in line with
stipulated timeframes.
103. The GRM officer will be responsible for:
 •   Developing and publicizing grievance management procedures.
 •   Receiving, reviewing, and keeping track of grievances (as applicable).
 •   Ensuring grievances are settled within the agreed time periods.
104. Stakeholders will be consulted about the GRM during the stakeholder engagement process to
ensure its accessibility and adequacy. Separate GRMs are available for: (i) project workers and
detailed in the project’s LMP; and (ii) for PAPs due to land acquisition and detailed in the project’s
RP. All information about grievance procedures, grievance forms, and responses will be available in
Vietnamese and English, as follows:

6.1. All-functional Local Level Procedures
 •   The first level – W/CPC. An affected stakeholder is to take his/her complaint to any member
     of the W/CPC, through the village/cluster head, or directly to W/CPC, in written or oral form.
     The W/CPC will work directly with the complainant to resolve the complaint and will issue a
     resolution within 15 working days after receiving the complaint. The secretariat of the W/CPC
     is responsible for documenting and recording all the complaints that it is handling. Once the
     W/CPC issues its decision, the stakeholder can appeal to the City PC within 30 working days
     if they are not satisfied with the decision or after 15 working days if they have not heard back
     from the W/CPC
 •   The second level – City PC. Upon receiving a complaint from a stakeholder, the City PC will
     have 30 working days to resolve the case. The City PC is responsible for filing and storing
     documents for all complaints that it handles. After the City PC has issued a decision, the
     stakeholder can appeal to the people’s court within 45 working days if they are not satisfied
     with the decision or after 30 working days if they have not heard back from the City PC.
 •   The final level - Court of Law Decides: In case a stakeholder brings his/her case to the City
     people’s court and the court rules in favor of the complainant. The decision ruling the settlement
     of complaints will have to be sent to complainants and concerned parties and shall be publicly
     posted at the headquarters of the People's Committee of the relevant level. The complainant
     will receive such ruling three days after the result of complaint resolution at the commune level
     has been decided upon and 7 working days at the provincial level.
105. The GRM Officer will need to liaise with the above levels to keep a database of the project-
related complaints from stakeholders, including information such as the nature of the complaint, the
source, and date of receipt of the complaint, the name, and address of the complainant, action plan,
and current status (see Annex 1), including complaints both received orally and in writing. If needed,
the GRM Officer will be able to contact complainants directly and guide them through the official
GRM channels for the project.




                                                                                            63 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)




Figure 25: The grievance process for the Project

6.2. Project-Specific Construction-Related Procedures
106. During construction, the GRM will also be managed by the Contractors under the supervision
of the construction supervision consultant. Through consultations and disclosure activities, the
Contractors will inform stakeholders about the GRM and how to submit complaints and concerns
about the project. The Contractors will also appoint a ESO to be responsible for grievance redress.
All complaints and corresponding actions undertaken by the Contractor will be recorded in project
monitoring reports. Complaints and claims for damages could be lodged as follows:
 •   Verbally: directly to the Contractor’s ESO or representatives at the site offices.
 •   In writing: by hand-delivering or posting a written complaint to a designated address of the
     project.
 •   By telephone, fax, or e-mails: to the project’s GRM Focal Point or the contractor’s
     representatives.
107. Upon receipt of a complaint, the Contractor’s ESO will register the complaint in a complaint
file and maintain a log until the grievance is resolved (see Annex 1). Immediately upon receipt, four
copies of the complaint will be made: the original for the file, one copy for the ESO, one copy
forwarded to the PMU, and one copy for the complainant. The information to be recorded in the
complaint log will consist of at least:


                                                                                          64 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

 •   The date and time of the complaint,
 •   The name, address, and contact details of the complainant,
 •   A short description of the complaint,
 •   Actions taken to address the complaint, including contact persons and findings at each step in
     the complaint redress process,
 •   The final resolution of the complaint.
108. The main objective will be to resolve issues on the spot as much as possible. When that is not
possible, complaints will be dealt with within one week. When a complaint cannot be resolved within
15 days, local authorities will be involved. Such a situation may arise, for example, when damages
are claimed, the to-be-paid amount cannot be resolved, or damage causes need to be determined.

6.3. Procedures in Cases of Gender-Based Violence/Sexual exploitation and abuse/Sexual
      harassment
109. To avoid the risk of stigmatization, exacerbation of the mental/psychological harm, or potential
reprisal, the GRM shall have a different and sensitive approach to GBV/SEA/SH-related cases.
Where such a case is reported to the GRM, it should immediately be referred to appropriate service
providers, such as medical and psychological support, emergency accommodation, and/or any other
necessary services. It should also be reported to the PMU’s GRM Officer, who can advise on relevant
service providers if needed. Data on GBV/SEA/SH cases should not be collected through the GRM
unless operators have been trained on the empathetic, non-judgmental, and confidential collection of
these complaints. Only the nature of the complaint (what the complainant says in her/his own words)
and additional demographic data, such as age and gender, can be collected as usual.
110. In addition, capacity-building for GRM committees on handling GBV/SEA/SH-related
grievances and awareness campaigns about the GRM for communities will be conducted during the
project implementation.

6.4. Procedures in cases of conflicts among ethnic groups
111. In the Project, addressing potential conflicts between the Chinese and Khmer ethnic groups is
critical to fostering collaboration and ensuring project success. The project will establish a Grievance
Redress Committee (GRC) composed of equal representation from the Hoa and Khmer communities,
including community elders, local government representatives, and religious leaders from both
groups. This committee will meet biweekly to review grievances and ensure timely resolution.
112. When a conflict arises, the GRC will appoint a neutral mediator, agreed upon by both parties,
to facilitate a resolution. The mediation process will take place in a neutral and culturally significant
location, such as the community center, and will prioritize open dialogue to understand each group’s
concerns. Decisions will be documented and shared transparently with both groups to maintain trust.
113. To protect the privacy of complainants, the GRC will ensure confidentiality throughout the
process. Additionally, regular workshops will be held to educate both communities about the
grievance process, emphasizing its fairness and neutrality. Progress and outcomes will be monitored,
and lessons learned will be incorporated into the GRM to continually improve its effectiveness. This
mechanism will ensure conflicts are resolved respectfully, fostering harmony between the Chinese
and Khmer communities while supporting the project’s objectives.



                                                                                              65 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

114. Grievances can be submitted through multiple channels, including community meetings at the
local pagoda, verbal submissions to village leaders, or written complaints deposited in project
grievance boxes located in communal houses. To ensure inclusivity, project staff will provide
interpreters for both Chinese and Khmer languages, and all written materials will be bilingual.

6.5. Procedures in Cases of Labors’ Concerns
115. Project workers can submit their complaints (related to employment opportunities, wages,
payment, working conditions, health, safety, administrative acts and decisions, discipline decisions,
etc.) in writing, or face-to-face verbally – except for complaints against discipline decisions to their
employer/contractor who are responsible for solving first-time complaint as outlined in the GRM for
project workers in the Project LMP. Complainants can also lodge a grievance through the PMU using
email, post, or telephone or PMU dedicated channels for GRM. Complainant can bring their case to
the court of law at any stage during the complaint resolution process (See the project LMP for
details).

6.6. Procedures in Cases of Land Acquisition
116. Complaint concerning land acquisition, physical resettlement, and relevant issues are expected
to be lodged through CPCs. Submission of complaints can be done using emails, postal, hand
delivery, or verbally through direct meetings or telephone. Within 10 days from the date of receiving
the complaint, the person in charge of complaint resolution shall accept the complaint and notify the
complainant in writing. The time limit for complaint resolution – from the date of acceptance, is 30
days for first-time complaints, and 45 days for the second time and third-time complaints. The statute
of limitation (for initiating a lawsuit) is 30 days from the date the time-limit for complaint resolution
expires, or from the date the complainant receives the complaint resolution decision. In a remote area
where travel is difficult, the statute of limitation can be extended but not exceeding 45 days. The
complainant can also initiate a lawsuit at any stage of the grievance resolution process if they disagree
with the complaint resolution decision, or if the case is not resolved following the expiry of the time-
limit for complaint resolution (see the project RP for details).

7.   MONITORING AND REPORTING
117. The SEP will be periodically revised and updated as necessary in the course of project
implementation to ensure that the information presented is consistent, and up to date, and that the
identified methods of engagement remain appropriate and effective concerning the project context
and specific phases of project development. Any major changes to project-related activities and its
schedule will be duly reflected in the updated SEP.
118. Monthly summaries and internal reports on public grievances, together with the status of
implementation of associated corrective actions will be collated by responsible staff and referred to
the PMU’s Director. The monthly summaries will provide a mechanism for assessing both the
number and the nature of complaints and requests for information, along with the project’s ability to
address those in a timely and satisfactory manner. The project will make public quarterly project
monitoring reports on its website [https://sonnptnt.soctrang.gov.vn/], which will include reporting on
the SEP and grievance redress.




                                                                                              66 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

7.1. Internal monitoring
119. The objective of internal monitoring relating to the SEP is to oversee its implementation to
ensure targeted consultations and disclosure activities are taking place. Internal Monitoring will be
led by the PMU’s ESO, under the overall guidance of the PMU’s Director. The involvement of local
communities in monitoring activities should be encouraged whenever possible. Internal Monitoring
will consist of quarterly reports during the implementation of the project, part of the ESMP or RP
monitoring, including public grievances, inquiries, and related incidents, together with the status of
implementation of associated corrective/preventative actions will be gathered by the ESO and
reported to the PMU’s Director.
120. Monitoring SEP implementation should focus on:
 •   Level of understanding of the project,
 •   Community feedback incorporated into project design and planning,
 •   Knowledge by stakeholders of project documents, including the GRM and impact mitigation
     plans,
 •   Implementation of disclosure and consultation activities as per the SEP,
 •   Main grievances and efficacy of GRM,
 •   Overall community satisfaction,
 •   Minutes of consultation meetings,
 •   Number of staff working on the stakeholder engagement, including at the PMU and its
     consultants or contractors, and
 •   Plans for the next month and long-term plans.
121. Monitoring specific to land acquisition activities will be conducted as per the RP.

7.2. Third Party Monitoring
122. The PMU will engage an IESMC as a third party to assist the PMU in monitoring and
evaluation of the implementation of E&S risk and impact management and mitigation measures,
including compensation and resettlement.
123. This consultant will undertake periodic monitoring every six months on the implementation of
E&S risk and impact management in compliance with regulations set forth in the project’s
ESIA/ESMP, RP, SEP, EMDP and LMP. They will also monitor closely the implementation of
project’s GRM.
124. Key tasks of the consultant related to SEP will be:
 •   Adequacy of stakeholder engagement, including activities, staffing, and budget.
 •   Review of grievances submitted, time to respond, resolution of grievances, and complainant’s
     satisfaction with the process.
 •   Interview of sample households to assess satisfaction with stakeholder engagement and
     knowledge of the project and related programs.




                                                                                           67 | P a g e
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

7.3. Community Monitoring
125. As per Decree 84/2015/ND-CP dated 30 September 2015 regarding investment supervision and
assessment, local people can establish a community investment supervision board to conduct
environmental, social, and construction safety supervision voluntarily. During project
implementation, communities that benefit from the project will be encouraged to form a local team
by this Decree to monitor and provide feedback on construction-related issues.

7.4. Reporting back to stakeholders
126. The method of reporting back to stakeholders will depend on the stakeholder itself, as follows:
 •   Regarding community concerns, the project will report back to the community and other
     relevant stakeholder groups, primarily through public meetings and/or public disclosure which
     people of the community can easily access such as the community’s common house or
     communal office. The feedback report will be shared or disclosed to the concerned community
     and other stakeholders within the timeframe set up in the stakeholder engagement program.
 •   Regarding individual concerns, including grievances related to E&S issues such as inaccurate
     measurement of affected land area, undervalued compensation rate, or request for additional
     support for livelihood restoration, the PMU, and local implementing agencies will report back
     individually, via the same channel through which the grievances were received, for easy follow-
     up of the complainant. Feedback received through the GRM will be responded to in writing and
     sent directly to the complainant via postal mail.
 •   Regarding comments of stakeholders both affected and concerned parties on the project design,
     E&S documents, they will be addressed in relevant documents and posted on the websites of
     the PMU and PPC and also posted locally on the public notice board at CPC’s office. Comments
     and questions submitted in association with specific documents disclosed on the above websites
     will be reviewed and responded to promptly as regulated in the EMDP, ESCP, SEP, ESMP,
     RP, and LMP.
 •   Regarding comments of national-level stakeholders, an email and/or official letter will be sent
     after workshops on how comments/suggestions were taken into account and/or to inform them
     of documents that are publicly available.
127. In addition, project documents, including monthly, quarterly, or annual reports will be updated
and uploaded as needed, including this SEP, and available on the project website at:
https://cantho.gov.vn. During consultation activities, stakeholders will also be reminded that these
documents are publicly available, as well as the project’s GRM for lodging their concerns,
grievances, and complaints related to the project implementation, including the implementation of
E&S risk and impact management and mitigation measures via public meeting, consultation and
feedback reports.

 STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

1.       INTRODUCTION

1.1.     Project overview
1.      The Socialist Republic of Vietnam intends to apply for a loan from the World Bank to finance
the proposed Sustainable Fishery Development Project hereafter referred as “the Project”. This
project aims to enable improved management of and increased value from target fisheries by
investing in ‘smart’ infrastructures. The project will be implemented by the Ministry of Agricultural
and Rural Development (Now is MAE)/Central Project Office (CPO) for agricultural projects (called
the Central Project Management Unit (CPMU)) as well as the Provincial People’s Committees
(PPCs)/Provincial Project Management Unit (PPMUs) - the existing project management units under
the Department of Agriculture and Rural Development (Now is DAEs) of the participating provinces
including Hai Phong, Thanh Hoa, Gia Lai, Khanh Hoa, Can Tho, and An Giang (called the PPMUs).
This project addresses the environmental and social aspects through the World Bank’s Environmental
and Social Stands (ESS) approach/framework.
2.     Thanh Hoa province is one of the provinces participating in the project and the Thanh Hoa
province sub-project has been approved by the Provincial People's Council for investment policy.
Information on the investment items of the Thanh Hoa province sub-project is described below.
1.2.     Description of the Thanh Hoa sub-project
1.2.1. Subproject’s objectives
3.      The overall objective of the project is to build and upgrade infrastructure, reorganize
production, and develop the fisheries sector in Thanh Hoa province towards increasing its value,
integrating internationally, and adapting to climate change. The specific objectives of the project are
to establish a comprehensive fishery exploitation infrastructure system. This system will be
integrated with auxiliary industries and logistics services, aiming to improve the efficiency of fishery
exploitation and reduce post-harvest losses; to develop brackish water aquaculture infrastructure to
enhance the productivity and quality of farmed shrimp, while also protecting the ecological
environment.
4.       The specific objectives of the subproject includes:
     -   Forming a synchronous seafood exploitation infrastructure system, associated with
         supporting industries and logistics services to improve the efficiency of seafood exploitation,
         reducing post-harvest losses.
     -   Developing brackish water aquaculture infrastructure to improve productivity and quality of
         farmed shrimp, and protect the ecological environment.
5.      The People's Council of Thanh Hoa province decided the investment plan for the Sustainable
Fisheries Development Project in Thanh Hoa province, loan from the WB, including 09 sub-projects
in 07 communes: Hoang Tien, Hoa Loc, Nga Son, Tan Tien, Hoang Chau, Van Loc, Quang Chinh
(formerly, 11 communes belong to four districts, including Hoang Hoa, Hau Loc, Nga Son and Quang
Xuong) in accordance with Resolution No. 460/ND-HDND dated December 14, 2023. Thanh Hoa
Provincial People's Committee assigned the Project Management Unit for Investment and
Construction of Agricultural and Rural Development Works (PPMU) as the implementing unit in
Document No. 19231/UBND-THKH dated December 20, 2023.

Component 2: Project management
9.    This component does not relate to civil works; the main tasks of the component include: (i)
Managing project implementation in accordance with current legal regulations of Vietnam; (ii)
Implementing project monitoring and evaluation in accordance with regulations of the Donor (WB).

2.      OBJECTIVES AND SCOPE OF STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN

2.1.    Objectives of the SEP
10.     The overall objective of this SEP is to define a program for stakeholder engagement,
including public information disclosure, grievance redress mechanism and consultation, throughout
the implementation and operation of the proposed projects. The SEP outlines the ways in which
PPMU and contractors will communicate with stakeholders and includes a mechanism by which
people can raise concerns, provide feedback, or make complaints about the project, contractors, and
the Project itself. The consultation and engagement with the local population is essential to the
success of the Project in order to ensure smooth collaboration between project staff and local
communities and to minimize and mitigate environmental and social risks related to the proposed
Project.
11.     The specific objectives of the SEP are:
        o Establish a program for stakeholder engagement, including public information disclosure,
          grievance redressal mechanisms and consultation, throughout the implementation and
          operation of proposed subprojects;
        o Develop a mechanism through which stakeholders can raise concerns, provide feedback,
          or make positive and negative complaints about the project to minimize and limit
          environmental and social risks related to the project;



                                                                                                                 Page 7
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

         o Demonstrate the commitment of project implementing agencies with engagement of
           stakeholders in the project construction process, including identifying investment
           priorities and considering stakeholder’s concerns.
2.2.    Scope of the SEP and Project Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
12.     The scope of the SEP is to assess the level of stakeholder interest and support for the project,
enable stakeholder views to be taken into account in project design and environmental and social
performance, promote and provide means for inclusive engagement throughout the project life-cycle,
ensure that appropriate project information is disclosed to stakeholders in a timely, understandable,
accessible, and appropriate manner and format, provide citizens with accessible and inclusive means
to raise issues and grievances and enable the project implementing entity to respond to and manage
such grievances.
2.2.1. Positive impacts
13.     The benefits of the project are long-term rather than short-term. If the endeavor is successful,
it should not only benefit the current number of fishermen, aquaculture farmers, and service providers
involved in the fishing and shrimp farming in the project area, but also make a significant contribution
to the overall sustainable development of the national fishery industry as follow:
 •     By taking resilience to climate change and extreme weather events into the design process of
       the project items, especially the storm shelter, it is possible to increase its lifespan and decrease
       the operation and maintenance (O&M) cost.
 •     The storm shelter construction will help commercial fishing vessels in Thanh Hoa province and
       neighboring areas anchor safely during storms, reducing fishing costs and increasing value-
       added products by shortening distances between ports and fishing grounds. This will increase
       income and improve the nutritional value of aquatic products.
 •     Fishermen will be able to use the fishing facilities that have better sanitation and weather
       conditions. This lowers the occupational health risks, improves the quality and value of fish
       production, and lowers physical losses by making sure fish are caught on time and stored
       properly.
 •     Improved waste management in the storm shelter and aquaculture farms reduces water pollution
       and sediment discharge into water bodies. As a result, in the long run, the project should help
       to improve the water quality of the project area.
 •     Implementing Good Agriculture Practices (GAP) and Integrated Pest Management (IPM)
       principles, along with the introduction of bio-security practices and disease-free seeds, can
       foster an environment that is less conducive to pests and diseases in aquaculture ponds. This,
       in turn, can reduce the reliance on antibiotics and chemicals in aquaculture, safeguard the
       environment, mitigate losses caused by disease outbreaks, enhance food security, and improve
       occupational health and safety for farming-based laborers.
 •     Increased productivity of shrimp aquaculture areas will help farmers expand their shrimp
       farming adapting to the challenges of rising sea level.
 •     The project will also generate environmental and social benefits through a variety of other
       mechanisms, including (i) improved awareness and concern for environmental and social issues
       on the part of beneficiaries, local communities and districts; (ii) increasing the availability of
       staff conversant with environmental and social issues throughout the subproject area through
       training of DAEs’ senior staff as environmental and social management specialists and
       aquaculture extension staff at DAE in environmental and social screening; (iii) awareness
       raising among senior governmental staff on benefits of adopting environmental and social

                                                                                                     Page 8
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

     principles in sustainability of development projects, thus assisting in main streaming
     environmental and social issues in project planning and implementation.
2.2.2. Negative impacts and risks
14.    The implementation of the project will cause potential negative environmental and social
impacts and risks during pre-construction, construction and operational phases, as follows:
2.2.2.1. During pre-construction phase
 •   Safety risk relating to UXO. Vietnam in general and the project area in particular undergone
     civil wars which were ended in 1975. Although the land has been used in the last 45 years since
     the war ended, some UXOs may remain underground. If disturbed by construction activities
     such as site clearance, ground leveling and excavation, the UXO may pose hazard to the
     workers, damage to construction works.
 •   Land acquisition. The implementation of the project will unavoidably involve land acquisition
     (both permanent and temporary) in the project area. This has a negative impact on the lives,
     incomes and livelihoods of affected people, especially those severely affected (relocation,
     significant loss of productive land and/or business activities/incomes, and vulnerable groups).
 •   Impacts on income/livelihood: Local residents and affected parties are often involved in small-
     business activities and/or engage in fisheries and aquaculture activities where they live,
     therefore, relocation of affected households may result in loss of customers if affected
     households are relocated or loss of their existing sources of income/livelihood too far from their
     current location and place of business.
2.2.2.2. During construction phase
15.     Constructing infrastructure would involve activities such as site clearance, ground leveling,
demolishing the old structures, construction of worker camps and site offices, gathering materials
and machines at working sites, soil excavation, transportation to construction sites, preparation of
construction materials such as pile casting and concrete mixing, waste disposal, structure
construction, equipment installation for finalizing works, etc. These activities would take place in the
land area. For the storm shelter, construction of bridges, canal dredging, construction activities will
also take place in water. These include dredging, piling activities, construction of embankments, etc.
Environmental and social impacts and risks of these construction activities are summarized below.
 •   Common construction related impacts and risk: These include increased level of dust, gases
     emission, noise, vibration, loss of vegetation cover and trees, waste and wastewater generation,
     water quality reduction affecting aquatic lives, soil erosion sedimentation and localized
     flooding risks. On the social side, the main impact would include traffic disturbances, increased
     traffic safety risks, damage of existing infrastructure services and interruption of related public
     services such as power supply, damage of rural roads due to heavy transportation; occupational
     and community health and safety risks, gender-based violence (GBV) and Sexual Exploitation
     or Abuse (SEA) in relation to labor influx; exposure to cultural heritage and other social issues.
     It is anticipated that the project items mostly take place in modified habitats or in areas that are
     heavily disturbed by human activities.
 •   Other key risks and impacts during construction associated with the construction of storm
     shelter, bridges, canal dredging:
     o Water, soil pollution risks and impacts to aquatic life due to improper management and
       disposal of dredged materials. Dredging process may cause serious environmental risks and
       impacts if is not managed carefully.
     o Risks on soil erosion and subsidence during dredging and embankment process.

                                                                                                  Page 9
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

16.    These impacts and risks are localized, short term during construction period, and avoidable if
geology and hydrology survey are carefully considered during the detailed design, and via the
application of good construction method and the ESCOPs.
2.2.2.3. During operation phase
17.     Operation of the storm shelter: In terms of environmental aspect, the impacts and risks
associated with the storm shelter activities are air and water pollution due to gases emission,
generation of solid waste and wastewater from fishing ships, landing services and administrative
offices. For the social side, the impacts and risks during the operation of the storm shelter are
associated with occupational and community health and safety. Beside the negative impact on water
quality and aquatic life and riverbank erosion due to fishing ships’ movement and anchorage,
potential E&S risks and impacts during operation of the storm shelter are associated with waste
managements and social issues such as conflicts between fishermen and local people during the time
of anchoring.
18.    Operation of roads and bridges cause an increase in traffic accidents and greenhouse gases
emission.
19.      The impacts and risks can be mitigated measures during the operation phase.

3.       STAKEHOLDER IDENTIFICATION AND ANALYSIS

20.    The Thanh Hoa subproject stakeholders are people and entities who have a role in the Project,
or could be affected by the Project, or who are interested in the Project. Project stakeholders are
defined as individuals, groups or other entities who:
  (iii) are impacted or likely to be impacted directly or indirectly, positively or adversely, by the
        Project (also known as ‘affected parties’); and
  (iv) may have an interest in the Project (interested parties’). They include individuals or groups
       whose interests may be affected by the Project and who have the potential to influence the
       Project outcomes in any way
21.     Cooperation and negotiation with the stakeholders throughout the project development often
also requires the identification of people within the groups who act as legitimate representatives of
their respective stakeholder group, i.e., the individuals who have been entrusted by their fellow group
members by advocating the group’s interests in the process of engagement with the project.
Community representatives may provide helpful insight into the local settings and act as main
conduits for dissemination of the project-related information and as a primary communication/liaison
link between the project and targeted communities and their established networks. Verification of
stakeholder representatives (i.e., the process of confirming that they are legitimate and genuine
advocates of the community they represent) remains an important task in establishing contact with
the community stakeholders. Legitimacy of the community representatives can be verified by talking
informally to a random sample of community members and heeding their views on who can represent
their interests in the most effective way.
3.1.     Methodology
22.    To meet the best practice approaches, the project will apply the following principles for
stakeholder engagement:
       o Openness and life-cycle approach: public consultations for the project will be arranged
         during the whole lifecycle, carried out openly, free of external manipulation, interference,
         coercion, or intimidation.



                                                                                               Page 10
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

       o Informed participation and feedback: information will be provided to and widely distributed
         among all stakeholders in an appropriate format; opportunities are provided for
         communicating stakeholders’ feedback, and for analyzing and addressing comments and
         concerns.
       o Inclusiveness and sensitivity: stakeholder identification will be undertaken to support better
          communications and build effective relationships. The participation process for the project
          will be inclusive. All stakeholders at all times will be encouraged to get involved in the
          consultation process. Equal access to information will be provided to all stakeholders.
          Sensitivity to stakeholders’ needs is the key principle underlying the selection of engagement
          methods. Special attention will be given to vulnerable groups, in particular women, displaced
          persons, persons with disabilities, youth, elderly.
3.2.     Stakeholder Identification
23.    The stakeholder mapping for the project demonstrates that focus is placed on the core project
stakeholders and beneficiaries as well as host communities of the project. Therefore, the project
coordinates endeavors via facilitator/consultant teams to prompt and comprehensive involvement of
these groups (

                                 Figure 28: Stakeholders’ influence and interest analysis

26.     Stakeholders of the project are people or groups who are directly or indirectly affected by the
project, as well as those who may have an interest in the project and/or the ability to influence project
outcomes, either positively or negatively. For effective and tailored engagement, the project’s
stakeholders can be divided into the following 3 core categories
3.3.   Affected Parties
27.     One of the primary goals of the Project is to identify stakeholders on which the project can
have negative impact such as households or businesses who may be impacted by land acquisition,
livelihoods and business activities. However, the Project may influence the activities of various
stakeholders in positive or negative ways. Thus, a list of key stakeholder groups can be identified as
potentially affected parties. These should be engaged throughout the life of the Project and impacts
on them should be monitored and mitigated.
28.    The following groups can be identified as potential project-affected stakeholders:
       •               Fishermen/fishing vessel owners
       •               Seafood purchasing/processing facilities/fisheries services
       •               Management agencies of public fishery utilities
       •               Workers in the enterprise of aquaculture and processing seafood
       •               Women (including women workers/businesswomen in the fishing ports; female
                       workers in aquaculture farms/aquaculture households; female members of the affected
                       households)
       •               Aquaculture households


                                                                                                          Page 13
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

       •     Fishing communities
       •     Other vulnerable groups who are engaged in business activities at fishing ports, working
             on aquaculture farms and community member living nearby the project area (disabled,
             poor/near poor, landless households);
       •     Households affected by land acquisition.
29.     Project-affected parties include those likely to be affected by the Project because of actual
impacts or potential risks to their physical environment, health, security, cultural practices, well-
being, or livelihoods. The Table 65 below indicates potential project-affected parties of the SFDP –
Thanh Hoa subproject.

3.4.   Other Interested Parties
30.     The interested parties” include individuals, groups, or organization with an interest in the
Project, due to either the project location, its characteristics, its impacts, or matters related to public
interest. For example, these parties may include government officials, the private sector, the scientific
community, academics, mass organizations, other civil society organizations, and cultural groups.
The following stakeholders are listed as the subproject’s interested parties:
        •     Central level: Including relevant ministries and sectors such as the Ministry of Finance,
              the Ministry of Industry and Trade, the Ministry of Agriculture and Environment, the
              Ministry of Construction, and their affiliated agencies responsible for policies,
              planning, funding allocation, and overall supervision of the project.
        •     Provincial level: Including the Provincial People’s Committee and relevant provincial
              departments such as the Department of Finance, Department of Construction,
              Department of Home Affairs, Department of Agriculture and Environment, Department
              of Industry and Trade, the Provincial Project Management Unit, and the Provincial
              Inspectorate, which are responsible for coordinating and supervising the
              implementation of the project within the province.
        •     Commune level: Including the Commune People’s Committee, specialized departments
              such as the Division of Economic, the Commune Compensation, Assistance and
              Resettlement Committees (if applicable), and the commune authorities within the
              project-affected area.
        •     Mass organizations include 6 organizations at all levels as Women’s Union, Youth
              Union, Fatherland Front, Farmers’ Union, Veterans Associations, and Trade Union.
        •     Mass media include local, regional and national news agencies (TV, broadcasting,
              online newspaper) who may be interested in project activities, project progress, and
              impact on local development.
        •     Commune’s citizen groups benefit from additional income thanks to providing services
              to construction workers (e.g., foods and other essential supplies, etc.). Those who may
              become involved in construction and operation as seasonal workers are also those who
              may be interested in the project, and those who are interested in supervision of the
              construction of the project components.
        •     Development agencies and/or NGOs: Currently, there are no NGOs working in the
              communes. However, NGOs become interested in the project during its
              implementation, this SEP will be updated to reflect their concerns (if any).
        •     Public service utilities, including telecommunication, electric power, water supply,
              wastewater treatment, waste collection, waterways traffic management, commune
              health centers, etc.
        •     Experts, universities, institutes, including Research Institute….
        •     Private companies: Gender-based violation (GBV) service provider, other service
              providers (tourist centers, hotels, motels, etc.) for contracted workers.

3.5.        Disadvantaged/Vulnerable Individual and Groups
31.     “Disadvantaged or vulnerable” refers to those who may be more likely to be adversely
affected by the Project. Such groups may be less able to take advantage of a project’s benefits. Such
groups are also more likely to be excluded from the consultation process. Various types of barriers
may influence the capacity of such groups to articulate their concerns and priorities about project
impacts.
32.     Engagement with the vulnerable groups and individuals often requires the application of
specific measures and assistance aimed at the facilitation of their participation in the project-related
decision making so that their awareness of and input to the overall process are commensurate to those
of the other stakeholders. Within the Project Area of Influence, the vulnerable groups may include
and are not limited to the following:
  vii.         Elderly people and/or social policy householders (invalid soldiers, martyrs, heroic
               mothers);
  viii.        Poor/near poor households;
   ix.         Persons with disabilities and their caretakers;
       x.      Women, particularly women-headed                    households       or    single     mothers       with
               underage/dependent children;
   xi.         The unemployed persons.
  xii.         Ethnic Minority Community in the project area.
33.   Table 67 below indicates disadvantaged or vulnerable groups encountered under the SFDP
– Thanh Hoa subproject.
Table 67: List of disadvantaged/vulnerable groups

34.     Vulnerable groups within the communities affected by the project will be further confirmed
and consulted through dedicated means, as appropriate. Specific measures to address the needs of the
poor and women will be put in place through both stakeholder consultation and communication,
livelihood restoration and land acquisition. There will be separate consultation meetings conducted
with vulnerable group/individuals and the purpose will be to explore the critical issues for vulnerable
and what methods will be considered to support vulnerable people.


                                                                                                                Page 20
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

35.     Disadvantaged or vulnerable individuals or groups, who often do not have a voice to express
their concerns or understand the impacts of the sub- project. In the present context, this would include
women, who for cultural reasons have low mobility and are hard to reach, disabled and any displaced
(internally or externally) families among the PAPs. Special efforts will be made to disseminate
project information to these groups and to ensure their inclusion in the stakeholder engagement
process. To involve women, particularly among the PAPs, in the stakeholder engagement process,
the Project will work through female representatives in the affected communities, to identify suitable
venues and timing for consultations. Regarding disabled groups, all venues for consultations,
workshops and meetings should be selected with a view to facilitate physical access for disabled.
Displaced PAPs will be informed about meetings and consultations via other community members
in the area, and potentially through radio.
3.6.   Stakeholder Analysis
36.     As mentioned above, there is a wide range of project stakeholders that are affected by and are
interested in project implementation. Different stakeholders have different levels of interest and
influence on project design and implementation. Therefore, it is important to understand: (i) the level
of interest of each stakeholder as to the project’s investment activities; (ii) the impact magnitude of
the project on them, particularly those adversely affected, and (iii) the extent to which the stakeholder
influences the project design, as well as implementation process and outcome.
37.     Under this project, stakeholders highly relevant to the project preparation and implementation
include governmental agencies and the community at large. These stakeholders are affected by the
project and at the same time can influence project design and implementation - to various extents.
Their interest in project investments also varies at different stages of the project cycle, and as such
exert different levels of influence on the project design and implementation process.
38.     Based on the roles, responsibilities, and the potential interest of the key stakeholders obtained
from initial consultations with them Table 68, summarizes the estimated levels of interest, impact,
and influence of each stakeholder group, of which:
       o “impact” means the project's impacts (both positive and negative impacts) on each key
         group of the stakeholder during phases of the project.
       o “influence” means people’s influence on the project implementation.
       o Level of “impact” and “influence” will be assessed on 3 different levels: High;
         Moderate; and Low.
39.     It is noted that these levels of interest, impact, and influence may change throughout the
project. However, the current dynamics of stakeholders’ interest, impact, and influence, as described
in Table 68, are useful for informing how each stakeholder should be engaged - in a meaningful
manner, to ensure they contribute to the overall environmental and social performance process, and
outcome of the project.




                                                                                                 Page 21
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

                                                                                                                                                                     Page 26
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

4.       STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PROGRAM

40.    In order to achieve the objectives of the SEP, the subproject will develop an engagement
program with the following purposes:
     •   Provide meaningful information in a format and language that is readily understandable.
     •   Provide information in advance about consultation activities when possible.
     •   Disseminate information in a manner and location easy for stakeholders to access.
     •   Establish a two-way dialogue that allows the project and stakeholders to exchange views and
         information, and have issues heard and addressed.
     •   Ensure inclusiveness in the representation of views, including those of women, the elderly,
         people living with a disability, and other vulnerable people as necessary.
     •   Ensure any obstacles to participation that are identified are removed so that views of different
         stakeholders can be captured.
     •   Ensure there are clear mechanisms for responding to people’s concerns, suggestions, and/or
         grievances.
     •   Incorporate feedback into the project and report back to stakeholders.
     •   Monitor stakeholder engagement activities and include project stakeholders in monitoring to
         the extent possible.
     •   Incorporate stakeholder engagement as part of the project management responsibilities and
         ensure staff in the PPMU and its consultants are equipped with specific responsibilities and
         budget.
4.1.     Summary of Stakeholder Engagement during Project Preparation
4.1.1. During the preparation of the project Stakeholder Engagement Framework
41.     During SEF preparation, the Environmental and Social Consultants joined the meetings with
APMB, World Bank Task Team and conducted site visits to proposed components in 12
cities/provinces from 18 May 2020 to 15 June 2020. The social and environmental consultants held
meetings with relevant stakeholders in the modality of public consultations. There are 12 consultation
meetings organized in May and June 2020 in 12 cities/provinces under the Sustainable Fishery
Development Project (SFDP) with a total of 132 individuals attended. The consultation meeting with
Thanh Hoa province was held on May 21st, 2020. The participants included wide-ranging
representatives from provincial implementing agency (PMU staff), local governmental agencies
(PPCs, DPCs, C/WPCs), Provincial Departments (DAE, DOCT LFDC, DOHA), representatives of
local community (heads of villages, hamlets, residential groups), and local mass organizations
(Women's Union, Fatherland Front, Ethnic Minority Board, Veterans Association, Youth Union).
42.      However, due to limited timeframe and the impacts of COVID-19 pandemic, the consultation
activities with project direct affected households and beneficiaries have not been implemented.
During the project preparation, for development of the sub-project SEPs, various consultation
activities with the local community were carried out by various modalities (focus group discussions,
in-depth interview). Group discussion is an effective means of consultation because it encourages the
sharing and discussion of views and ideas related to the proposed project to get feedback and
comments that will be reflected in the specific sub-project SEP.
43.    During the stakeholder consultations for the SEF, the consultants provided overall
information about the project (objective, components, project footprint, potential risks/impacts,
funding source, implementation arrangement). The new applicable World Bank’s ESF and its E&S
standards and requirements were also oriented towards the participants. The aim of the consultations

                                                                                                 Page 27
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

was to familiarize the stakeholders with the scope of the project and identify appropriate mechanisms
for communication and consultation throughout the life of the project. In particular, the required E&S
documents prepared for the project such as ESIA/ESMP, RPF, SEP, LMP and ESCP were also
consulted in order to get feedback from the participants for further improvement. List of participants
attended in consultation meetings is presented in Annexes of the SEP.
44.        The main issues raised during the consultations are summarized as follows:
          (v)      The SFDP will play an important role and contribute to the development of the
                   city/provinces. It will bring benefits to local residents, economically, socially, and
                   environmentally.
          (vi)     The market price should be applied for compensation. Livelihood restoration and life
                   stabilization programs are necessary to ensure all the affected people are assisted to
                   restore their living conditions. The coordination with and support of local
                   authorities/agencies to ensure the smooth and timely site clearance are critical.
          (vii)    Sustainability and the functioning of the proposed items should be taken into
                   consideration.
          (viii)   Meaningful consultation and stakeholder engagement principles needs to be sustained
                   in all activities and phases of the project, especially for affected parties (women,
                   affected households, Ethnic Minority people and other vulnerable groups).
45.        The
46.

           LFDC);
      •    Representatives of the local community.
      •    Local mass organizations (Women's Union, Fatherland Front, Ethnic Minority Board,
           Veterans Association, Youth Union);
49.    Discussed contents: The following main contents were discussed in the consultation
meetings with the project stakeholders:
      •    Discussed and agreed on the list of the project’s stakeholders, including affected parties and
           interested parties.
      •    Discussed the current regulations on mobilizing stakeholders’ participation in the
           implementation of the investment projects.
      •    Discussed on the WB’s requirement in ESS10 - Stakeholder Engagement and Information
           Disclosure.
      •    Discussed the approaches and ways to mobilize the stakeholders’ participation.

50.     The frameworks are no longer applicable due to changes in project implementation
arrangements and design. However, the outcomes of previous consultations have been incorporated
into the overall stakeholder engagement process. This stakeholder engagement plan reflects and takes
into account all relevant feedback from those consultations.
4.1.2. Summary of consultation activities during the preparation of the SEP
51.     During the preparation of the SEP, environmental and social consultants worked closely with
PPMU, FS team, 07 project communes and conducted field surveys of proposed sub-projects
(formerly 11 communes in 04 districts, including Hoang Hoa, Hau Loc, Nga Son and Quang Xuong)
in November 2024. Environmental and social consultants also met with stakeholders in the form of
public consultation meetings (11 PMCs), focus group discussion (12 FGDs), in-depth interview (16
KIIs), questionnaire survey (214 HHs) and online public consultation, consultation with CPCs and
Commune Fatherland Front Committees in writing. There were 11 consultation meetings held in
November 2024 for 09 subprojects in 07 communes under the Sustainable Fisheries Development
Project (SFDP) of Thanh Hoa province. In addition, there are in-depth interviews that have been
conducted with representatives of some affected households including aquaculture households,
severe affected households and the local authorities of the agencies related to the project during the
RP/ESIA/ESMP/LMP/ESCP preparation. Participants were representatives from provincial
implementing agencies (PPMU’s staff), local government agencies (People's Committees of
communes), local community representatives (heads of community) and local organizations
(Women's Union, Fatherland Front, Farmer Association, War Veterans Association, Youth Union).
52.    During the preparation phase to develop the Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) for the
subprojects, local community consultation was carried out by various means (consultation meetings

                                                                                                 Page 29
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

to get direct opinion and written opinion, focus group discussions, in-depth interviews). Group
discussion is an effective means of consultation because it encourages sharing of opinions and
discussion of ideas related to the proposed project, thereby integrating the feedback into
the SEP of project.
53.     During the consultation with stakeholders to develop the SEP, the consultants provided
overall information on the project/subprojects (including objectives, proposed items, potential
risks/impacts, funding, implementation arrangements and expected implementation timeframe). The
newly adopted environmental and social framework (ESF) of the World Bank and its environmental
and social standards and regulations were also oriented to the participants in the consultation. The
purpose of the consultation is to let stakeholders understand the scope of the subprojects and identify
appropriate mechanisms for communication and consultation during the whole lifecycle of the
project. In particular, the required environmental and social documents were prepared such as
ESIA/ESMP, RP, SEP, LMP and ESCP that are also given for consultation and receive feedback
from participants on these documents for improvement.

                                                                     physiology of the shrimp – which is already very sensitive
                                                                     to external impacts such as noise, vibration, light (if there
                                                                     is light at night, the shrimp may jump out of the water or
                                                                     die)
                                                                   - In the rainy season, it is necessary to pay attention to
                                                                     drainage for the construction area and according to the
                                                                     section and zoning to prevent overflow into the pond,
                                                                     avoid damage to people, if affected, the contractor must
                                                                     compensate according to the actual damage recorded and
                                                                     confirmed by the local authorities.

4.2.   Summary of Key Project Stakeholders’ Needs
54.     Various stakeholders require different methods of communication and consultation. For local
government officials, since a well-established communication protocol has been in place, the
communications with them to arrange consultation sessions are through means such as couriers,
electronic mail, telephone conversations, face-to-face meetings, etc. For the local community,
communication is typically through courier at village level or paying a home visit to those who are
better approached through direct contact, such as people with disabilities, the elderly, etc.
55.   Table 71 summarizes the stakeholder's needs for consultation in the project and proposed
methods to accommodate the needs of different stakeholders:
Table 71: Summary of Stakeholder Needs

4.3.   Strategy for Consultation
56.    The sub-project implementing agencies will mostly employ interviews, focus group
discussions, open meetings, and workshops as a means of carrying out consultations. This will be
done right in the affected communes to enable easy participation of vulnerable and disadvantaged
groups of people. The strategy will also be different depending on the sub-project stage as presented
below.
4.3.1. Consultation Strategies
Subproject preparation stage (before project appraisal)
57.     While the details of some proposed investments are unknown at this stage, the consultation
would focus on a broader range of stakeholders to understand their general interests and concerns on
the project and its potential positive and negative impacts to inform the project’s environmental and
social frameworks. This is also the stage where potential vulnerable/disadvantaged groups relevant
to the project will be identified. Proposals methods are as follows by PPMU with the support of
relevant consultants. Information published in the consultation includes subproject content,
environmental, social requirements, relevant regulations and programs
         •     Community consultation (commune level);
         •     Focus group discussion;
         •     Listed at commune People's Committees;
         •     The website of the Provincial People's Committee;
         •     World Bank's website.
Subproject implementation
58.      During implementation stages, the subproject activities are likely to have public consultation
and stakeholder engagement activities planned and committed as part of project design. These
activities may be described in different project documents and will involve a variety of stakeholders.
Commonly planned avenues of such engagement are public hearings, community meetings, focus
group discussions, field surveys and individual interviews. With growing concern about the risk of
virus spread, there is an urgent need to adjust the approach and methodology for continuing
stakeholder consultation and engagement. The subproject implementation agencies should note some
of the following:
         •     Identify and review planned activities under the project requiring stakeholder
               engagement and public consultations.
         •     Assess the level of proposed direct engagement with stakeholders, including location
               and size of proposed gatherings, frequency of engagement, categories of stakeholders
               (international, national, local) etc.
         •     Identify project activities for which consultation/engagement is critical and cannot be
               postponed without having significant impact on project timelines. For example,
               selection of resettlement options by affected people during project implementation.
               Reflecting the specific activity, consider viable means of achieving the necessary input
               from stakeholders.
         •     Assess the level of Information and Communication Technology (ICT) penetration
               among key stakeholder groups, to identify the type of communication channels that
               can be effectively used in the project context.


                                                                                               Page 46
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

59.      Based on the above, the project implementing agencies should consider the specific channels
of communication that should be used while conducting stakeholder consultation and engagement
activities. The following are some considerations while selecting channels of communication:
        •     If smaller meetings are permitted, conduct consultations in small-group sessions, such
              as focus group meetings. If not permitted, make all reasonable efforts to conduct
              meetings through online channels, including Webex, zoom and skype;
        •     Diversify means of communication and rely more on social media and online
              channels. Where possible and appropriate, create dedicated online platforms and chat
              groups appropriate for the purpose, based on the type and category of stakeholders;
        •     Employ traditional channels of communications (Television, newspaper, radio,
              dedicated phone-lines, and mail) when stakeholders to do not have access to online
              channels or do not use them frequently. Traditional channels can also be highly
              effective in conveying relevant information to stakeholders, and allow them to provide
              their feedback and suggestions;
        •     Where direct engagement with project affected people or beneficiaries is necessary,
              such as would be the case for RP and implementation, identify channels for direct
              communication with each affected household via a context specific combination of
              email messages, mail, online platforms, dedicated phone lines with knowledgeable
              operators;
        •     Each of the proposed channels of engagement should clearly specify how feedback
              and suggestions can be provided by stakeholders;
        •     An appropriate approach to conducting stakeholder engagement can be developed in
              most contexts and situations. However, in situations where none of the above means
              of communication are considered adequate for required consultations with
              stakeholders to decide whether the project activity can be rescheduled to a later time,
              when meaningful stakeholder engagement is possible.
60.     At this stage, the details of proposed investments will be determined and specific project-
affected parties (PAPs) identified. The consultation will focus on PAPs to address their concerns
through the implementation of subproject-specific environmental and social plans. The subprojects
will ensure that the GRM will remain effective during the civil works to promptly address any
grievances from the PAPs. The PPMU and local authotities will pay particular attention to vulnerable
groups to ensure inclusion and non-discrimination of such groups. Proposed consultation methods
are as follows by PPMU with the support of relevant consultants (including supervision consultancy)
and coordination of local authorities and community groups. Consulting objects are PAPs,
beneficiaries from job opportunities, project workers, stakeholders. The information in the
consultation includes specific environmental and social impacts and minimizing measures in
ESIA/ESMP, RP, LMP and SEP, raising awareness of GRM.
        •     Community consultation (quarterly).
        •     Focus Group Discussion (for vulnerable groups).
        •     Survey of the construction scene.
        •     The method of GRM implementation will be notified at the construction site and in
              the commune/ ward (on the notice table).
        •     Complaint procedures for employees;
        •     Selection criteria and Tor for recruiting labor for construction.
                                                                                              Page 47
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

Subproject operation
61.    At this stage, the consultation will focus on the feedback from PAPs on the performance of
environmental and social mitigations conducted during the construction stage. The consultation on
employment opportunities for operation and maintenance works (O&M) will be also conducted.
Areas for improvement and lessons learned should be identified for the following subprojects.
Dissemination and reporting back to general stakeholders on the outcome of the monitoring and
evaluation of the sub, include GRM. Project will be also arranged. How to do it through quarterly
community consultation, newsletter.
62.    The SEP will be reviewed and updated during project implementation as necessary on
adaptive stakeholder engagement arrangements, particularly the approach, methods and forms of
engagement proposed, and assess the associated potential risks of virus transmission in conducting
various engagement activities.
4.3.2. Consultation Methods
63.     The methods used to conduct consultations will have a direct impact on the outcome of the
project consultation strategy. Therefore, these methods should be designed to account for the diverse
characteristics of various stakeholder groups. The following consultation methods will be used for
this project:
64.     National consultations. National consultations are particularly useful to target government
representatives, NGOs, and other groups who have an interest in the project and may also have the
ability to influence it. These types of consultations are also useful at the project design stage, and
annually to inform stakeholders of a project’s progress. National consultations may be conducted
face-to-face or virtual in a meeting format and may involve break-out groups or other methods to
ensure there is ample time for group discussion and questions and answers.
65.    Local consultations. Local consultations are the main way to reach project affected
stakeholders and will involve face-to-face group meetings. In some cases, one-on-one consultations
or FGD may also be carried out, especially to reach and capture the views of vulnerable or
disadvantaged groups. Particular emphasis should be placed on seeking out the voice of women and
vulnerable groups, such as through female only FGDs. In the case of women, the project will ensure
consultation activities are conducted at a time that is convenient to them and that activities such as
FGDs are run by women if possible (particularly for sensitive topics like health or sexual issues).
66.      One important way to engage stakeholders will be through training on important topics related
to the project such as gender, the project’s GRM, labor rights (for both men and women), child labor,
HIV/AIDS and STDs, gender-based violence, sexual harassment, and sexual exploitation and abuse.
Since some of these topics are quite sensitive, particular measures may need to be in place, such as
ensuring some training courses are done in female-only or male-only groups, or that they are
facilitated by men or women, as appropriate. Training may be delivered by Thanh Hoa PPMU/its
consultants.
67.     Accessibility issues will also be taken into account, in particular, to ensure people with a
disability can be part of consultations, as well as the specific needs of other groups such as the elderly,
and poor groups.
68.     Consultations with project workers. The project will strive to ensure that unskilled workers
come from the community as much as possible, to minimize the influx of labor. Consultations will
encourage local workers to apply for available jobs, including EMs, women and vulnerable or
disadvantaged groups. Contractors and their staff will need to be well consulted and trained in
sensitive issues, including respectful behavior, Codes of Conduct, GBV (including SEA and SH),

                                                                                                   Page 48
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

HIV/AIDS and STDs, and worker’s GRM, LMP. They also need to be well aware of construction
needs and schedules. Training, on-site interviews, and regular feedback with Thanh Hoa PPMU
and/or CSC will be used as methods of consultation with them.
69.    Table 72 below summarizes the proposed strategy for consultation for all stages of the
subprojects.

4.4.   Stakeholder Engagement Methods to Be Used in the next Stages
Public/community meetings
70.    The Thanh Hoa PPMU will organize project launch meetings on a district level as well as
organize meetings in each project commune level with leaders, PAPs, and other interested parties.
Meetings will be open-housed events where PPMU will present information, and people will be
invited to make comments and express any concerns. A range of consultations with different
stakeholders will be conducted and documented, and community level meetings will also be
conducted with men and women (including vulnerable households) to better understand needs,
expectations, and concerns of these population groups in relation to the project. The feedback
received at these meetings will be documented along with measures that the project will take to
address the feedback received. Meetings in each district will also be organized on a quarterly basis.
Communication materials
71.     Written information will be disclosed to the public via a variety of communication materials
including leaflets, posters, etc. The PPMU will also update its website regularly (at least on a
quarterly basis) with key project updates and reports on the project’s performance in Vietnamese.
The website will also provide information about the grievance mechanism for the subprojects.
Training, workshops
72.     Training on a variety of social issues will be provided to PPMU, and possibly other relevant
government or non-government service providers. Issues covered will include sensitization to
inclusion/exclusion, labor issues, gender-based violence risks.
Grievance Redress Mechanism
73.     In compliance with the World Bank’s ESS10, a project-specific grievance mechanism will be
set up to handle complaints and issues, and this will be integrated into a country system on GRM that
are available to citizens. Dedicated communication materials (specifically, a GRM brochure or
pamphlet) will be developed to help local residents become familiar with the grievance redress
channels and procedures. The PPMU will coordinate with administrative authorities to maintain a
grievance register in order to capture and track grievances from submission to resolution and
communication with complainants.
74.     The initial effort to resolve grievances to the complainant’s satisfaction will be undertaken
by administrative levels. The mechanism is described in more detail in Section VI below. Grievances
may be submitted anonymously, but in that case, it cannot be known if the complainant is satisfied
with the resolution. PPMU' website, posters, and leaflets will include clear information on how
feedback, questions, comments, concerns, and grievances can be submitted by any stakeholder. It
will also provide information on the way grievances will be handled, both in terms of process and
deadlines.
4.5.   Strategy to Incorporate the Views of Vulnerable Groups
75.     All views expressed by stakeholders will be carefully noted, documented in the consultation
summary and considered, including those of disadvantaged or vulnerable groups. The following
strategy will be used for consultation with vulnerable groups:
 iv.       Issuance of invitations specific to the relevant groups: a) female PAPs; b) displaced PAPs;
           and iii) poor and other vulnerable groups;
 v.        Conduct specific consultations with each of these groups separately in a location and
           venue easily accessible to them. Women’s union, Youth Union, Fatherland Front or
           elderly group will be used as mediators and will be involved reaching these groups;

                                                                                              Page 53
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

 vi.        Consultation with vulnerable people will be conducted throughout the project life. There
            will be separate FGDs arranged with vulnerable people during project design/ preparation
            stage on a quarterly basis. The key focus will be to explore the problems and needs of
            these groups and how to reach them. There will also be quarterly meetings conducted with
            these groups during project implementation.

changes to stakeholder classification or engagement are required. If so, the plan will be updated, and
a new revision distributed, and the budget will be revised accordingly in order to ensure the objectives
of the SEP are successful. This expense for engagement activities is calculated on the financial plan
of the project. The expected budget is allocated annually based on the specific activities proposed in
each SEP. The funding will be provided before commencement of construction once the ESIA/ESMP
is available, and the contractors are selected.

5.2.      Implementation Arrangements
83.     The SEP will be the responsibility of the Director and ESO of Thanh Hoa PPMU. They will
guide the process of stakeholder engagement throughout the preparation and implementation of the
project. Certain parts of the SEP will also be the responsibility of contractors or consultants, and the
ESO of Thanh Hoa PPMU will oversee this work as appropriate. The ESO under the guidance of the
Director will be responsible for:
                                                                                                     Page 58
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

   o   Leading, or supervising consultations, as per the SEP.
   o   Leading, or supervising the disclosure of information, as per the SEP.
   o   Recording and reporting on grievance resolution, as per the SEP.
   o   Reviewing monthly monitoring reports provided by contractors and/or consultants.
   o   Regularly reporting to the PPMU Director.
84.    For effective implementation, the PPMU needs to engage various stakeholders identified in
Section 3. However, the participation of governmental agencies, such as PCs and their functional
agencies and the community in the project area, are key to effective SEP implementation (Table 77)

                                        the land acquisition process.
