6.      GRIEVANCE REDRESS MECHANISM (GRM)

85.     The grievance mechanism seeks to resolve concerns promptly, using an understandable
process that is culturally appropriate and readily accessible at no cost. Grievances can be submitted
if someone believes the project is having a detrimental impact on the community, the environment,
or their quality of life. Stakeholders may also submit their comments and suggestions on the project.
86.    The Thanh Hoa PPMU’s ESO will be responsible for overseeing those grievances are
received and resolved in a fair, objective, and constructive manner all concerns or complaints raised
by project stakeholders. The key principles of the grievance mechanism are to ensure that:
     o The basic rights and interests of affected people are protected.


                                                                                                             Page 60
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

      o The concerns of affected people arising from the project implementation process are
        adequately addressed.
      o Entitlements or livelihood support for affected people are provided on time and in accordance
        with project documents, such as ESMP, RP.
      o Affected people are aware of their rights to access grievance procedures free of charge for the
        above purposes.
87.     Based on the SEP, wherever possible, the project team shall seek to resolve complaints as
soon as possible and thus avoid the escalation of issues. GRM shall not impede access to the country’s
judicial or administrative remedies. Stakeholders can approach a court of law at any time and
independent of the project-level grievance redress process. Along with the World Bank requirements
based on ESS10, the GRM will be in line with the Vietnamese national legislation, in particular the
Law on Complaints (2011) and the Law on Denunciation (2018).
88.     The Thanh Hoa PPMU shall establish the GRM. The PPMU shall appoint a GRM focal point
to collaborate with local authorities in handling project-related grievances. The design of the GRM
shall take into consideration the views of affected stakeholders and what is considered an access
mechanism. The PPMU shall be required to disclose and explain to all stakeholders, in particular,
affected stakeholders and vulnerable and disadvantaged ones, the procedure for complaints filing as
early as possible and regularly throughout the project cycle. The grievance mechanism shall be
available at no cost, and it is important that it be easily accessible, with special attention paid to
accessibility for disadvantaged and vulnerable individuals or groups.
89.     The PPMU will ensure that grievances are registered, tracked, and resolved in line with
stipulated timeframes.
90.      The GRM officer will be responsible for:
      o Developing and publicizing grievance management procedures.
      o Receiving, reviewing, and keeping track of grievances (as applicable).
      o Ensuring grievances are settled within the agreed time periods.
91.     Stakeholders will be consulted about the GRM during the stakeholder engagement process to
ensure its accessibility and adequacy. Separate GRMs are available for: (i) project workers and
detailed in the project’s LMP; and (ii) for PAPs due to land acquisition and detailed in the project’s
RP. All information about grievance procedures, grievance forms, and responses will be available in
Vietnamese and English, as follows:
6.1.     All-functional Local Level Procedures
92.     The first level - CPC. An affected stakeholder is to take his/her complaint to any member of
the CPC, through the village/cluster head, or directly to CPC, in written or oral form. The CPC will
work directly with the complainant to resolve the complaint and will issue a resolution within 15
working days after receiving the complaint. The secretariat of the CPC is responsible for
documenting and recording all the complaints that it is handling. Once the CPC issues its decision,
the stakeholder can appeal to the CPC within 30 working days if they are not satisfied with the
decision or after 15 working days if they have not heard back from the CPC.
93.     The second level – PPC. Upon receiving a complaint from a stakeholder, the PPC will have
30 working days to resolve the case. The PPC is responsible for filing and storing documents for all
complaints that it handles. After the PPC has issued a decision, the stakeholder can appeal to the
people’s court within 45 working days if they are not satisfied with the decision or after 30 working
days if they have not heard back from the PPC.


                                                                                                Page 61
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

94.     The final level - Court of Law Decides: In case a stakeholder brings his/her case to
provincial people’s court and the court rules in favor of the complainant. The decision to ensure the
settlement of complaints will have to be sent to complainants and concerned parties and shall be
publicly posted at the headquarters of the People's Committee of the relevant level. The complainant
will receive such a rule three days after the result of complaint resolution at the commune level has
been decided upon and 7 working days at the commune or provincial level.
95.     The GRM Officer will need to liaise with the above levels to keep a database of the project-
related complaints from stakeholders, including information such as the nature of the complaint, the
source, and date of receipt of the complaint, the name, and address of the complainant, action plan,
and current status (see Annex 1), including complaints both received orally and in writing. If needed,
the GRM Officer will be able to contact complainants directly and guide them through the official
GRM channels for the project.




                                                                                              Page 62
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

6.2.      Project-Specific Construction-Related Procedures
96.    During construction, the GRM will also be managed by the Contractors under the supervision
of the construction supervision consultant. Through consultations and disclosure activities, the
Contractors will inform stakeholders about the GRM and how to submit complaints and concerns
about the project. The Contractors will also appoint a ESO to be responsible for grievance redress.
All complaints and corresponding actions undertaken by the Contractor will be recorded in project
monitoring reports. Complaints and claims for damages could be lodged as follows:
    -     Verbally: directly to the Contractor’s ESO or representatives at the site offices.
    -     In writing: by hand delivering or posting a written complaint to a designated address of the
          project.
    -     By telephone, fax, or e-mails: to the project’s GRM Focal Point or the contractor’s
          representatives.
97.     Upon receipt of a complaint, the Contractor’s ESO will register the complaint in a complaint
file and maintain a log until the grievance is resolved (see Annex 1). Immediately upon receipt, four
copies of the complaint will be made: the original for the file, one copy for the ESO, one copy

                                                                                                                                    Page 63
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

forwarded to the PPMU, and one copy for the complainant. The information to be recorded in the
complaint log will consist of at least:
    -   The date and time of the complaint,
    -   The name, address, and contact details of the complainant,
    -   A short description of the complaint,
    -   Actions taken to address the complaint, including contacting people and findings at each step
        in the complaint redress process,
    -   The final resolution of the complaint.
98.    The main objective will be to resolve issues on the spot as much as possible. If that is not
possible, complaints will be dealt with within one week. When a complaint cannot be resolved within
15 days, local authorities will be involved (Section 6.1). Such a situation may arise, for example,
when damages are claimed, the to-be-paid amount cannot be resolved, or damage causes need to be
determined.
6.3.    Procedures in Cases of Gender-Based Violence
99.     To avoid the risk of stigmatization, exacerbation of the mental/psychological harm, or
potential reprisal, the GRM shall have a different and sensitive approach to GBV-related cases.
Where such a case is reported to the GRM, it should immediately be referred to appropriate service
providers, such as medical and psychological support, emergency accommodation, and/or any other
necessary services. It should also be reported to PPMU’s GRM Officer, who can advise relevant
service providers if needed. Data on GBV cases should not be collected through the GRM unless
operators have been trained in the empathetic, non-judgmental, and confidential collection of these
complaints. Only the nature of the complaint (what the complainant says in her/his own words) and
additional demographic data, such as age and gender, can be collected as usual.
6.4.    Procedures in Cases of Labors’ Concerns
100. Project workers can submit their complaints (related to employment opportunities, wages,
payment, working conditions, health, safety, administrative acts and decisions, discipline decisions,
etc.) in writing, or face-to-face verbally – except for complaints against discipline decisions to their
employer/contractor who are responsible for solving first-time complaint. Complainants can also
lodge a grievance through the PPMU using email, post, or telephone or PPMU dedicated channels
for GRM. Complainants can bring their case to the court of law at any stage during the complaint
resolution process (See the subproject LMP for details).
6.5.    Procedures in Cases of Land Acquisition
101. Complaints concerning land acquisition, physical resettlement, and relevant issues are
expected to be lodged through CPCs. Submission of complaints can be done using emails, postal,
hand delivery, or verbally through direct meetings or telephone. Within 10 days from the date of
receiving the complaint, the person in charge of complaint resolution shall accept the complaint and
notify the complainant in writing. The time limit for complaint resolution – from the date of
acceptance, is 30 days for first-time complaints, and 45 days for the second time and third-time
complaints. The statute of limitation (for initiating a lawsuit) is 30 days from the date the time-limit
for complaint resolution expires, or from the date the complainant receives the complaint resolution
decision. In a remote area where travel is difficult, the statute of limitation can be extended but not
exceeding 45 days. The complainant can also initiate a lawsuit at any stage of the grievance resolution
process if they disagree with the complaint resolution decision, or if the case is not resolved following
the expiry of the time-limit for complaint resolution (see the subproject RP for details).


                                                                                                 Page 64
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

7.      MONITORING AND REPORTING

102. The SEP will be periodically revised and updated as necessary in the course of the project to
ensure that the information presented is consistent, and up to date, and that the identified methods of
engagement remain appropriate and effective concerning the project context and specific phases of
project development. Any major changes to project-related activities and its schedule will be duly
reflected in the SEP.
103. Monthly summaries and internal reports on public grievances, together with the status of
implementation of associated corrective actions will be collated by responsible staff and referred to
the PPMU’s Director. The monthly summaries will provide a mechanism for assessing both the
number and the nature of complaints and requests for information, along with the project’s ability to
address those in a timely and satisfactory manner. The project will make public quarterly project
monitoring reports on its website [tbc.], which will include reporting on the SEP and grievance
redress (see Section 6)
7.1.    Internal Monitoring
104. The objective of internal monitoring relating to the SEP is to oversee its implementation to
ensure targeted consultations and disclosure activities are taking place. Internal Monitoring will be
led by the PPMU’s ESO, under the overall guidance of the PPMU’s Director. The involvement of
local communities in monitoring activities should be encouraged whenever possible. Internal
Monitoring will consist of quarterly reports during the implementation of the project, part of the
ESMP or RP monitoring, including public grievances, inquiries, and relating incidents, together with
the status of implementation of associated corrective/preventative actions will be gathered by the
ESO and reported to the PPMU’s Director.
105.    Monitoring SEP implementation should focus on:
     o Level of understanding of the project,
     o Community feedback incorporated into project design and planning,
     o Knowledge by stakeholders of project documents, including the GRM and impact mitigation
       plans,
     o Implementation of disclosure and consultation activities as per the SEP,
     o Main grievances and efficacy of GRM,
     o Overall community satisfaction,
     o Minutes of consultation meetings,
     o Number of staff working on stakeholder engagement, including at the PPMU and its
       consultants or contractors, and
     o Plans for the next month and long-term plans.
106.    Monitoring specific land acquisition activities will be conducted as per the RP.
7.2.    Third Party Monitoring (independent)
107. The PPMU will engage an Independent Environmental and Social Monitoring Consultant
(IESMC) as a third party to assist the PPMU in monitoring and evaluating the implementation of
E&S risk and impact management and mitigation measures, including compensation and
resettlement.
108. This consultant will undertake periodic monitoring every six months on the implementation
of E&S risk and impact management in compliance with regulations set forth in the project’s
ESIA/ESMP, RP, SEP, and LMP. They will also monitor closely the implementation of the
subproject’s GRM.

                                                                                               Page 65
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

109.   Key tasks of the IESMC related to SEP will be:
    o Adequacy of stakeholder engagement, including activities, staffing, and budget.
    o Review of grievances submitted, time to respond, resolution of grievances, and complainant’s
      satisfaction with the process.
    o Interview of sample households to assess satisfaction with stakeholder engagement and
      knowledge of the project and related programs.
7.3.   Community Monitoring
110. As per Decree No. 29/2021/ND-CP dated 26 March 2021 regarding investment supervision
and assessment, local people can establish a community investment supervision board to conduct
environmental, social, and construction safety supervision voluntarily. During project
implementation, communities that benefit from the project will be encouraged to form a local team
by this Decree to monitor and provide feedback on construction-related issues.
7.4.   Reporting back to stakeholders
111.   The method of reporting back to stakeholders will depend on the stakeholder itself, as follows:
    o Regarding community concerns, the project will report back to the community and other
      relevant stakeholder groups, primarily through public meetings and/or public disclosure
      which people of the community can easily access such as the community’s common house or
      communal office. The feedback report will be shared or disclosed to the concerned
      community and other stakeholders within the timeframe set up in the stakeholder engagement
      program.
    o Regarding individual concerns, including grievances related to E&S issues such as inaccurate
      measurement of affected land area, undervalued compensation rate, or request for additional
      support for livelihood restoration, the PPMU, and local implementing agencies will report
      back individually, via the same channel through which the grievances were received, for easy
      follow-up of the complainant. Feedback received through the GRM will be responded to in
      writing and sent directly to the complainant via postal mail.
    o Regarding comments of stakeholders both affected and concerned parties, on the project
      design, E&S documents, they will be addressed in relevant documents and posted on the
      websites of the PPMU and PPC and also posted locally on the public notice board at CPC’s
      office. Comments and questions submitted in association with specific documents disclosed
      on the above websites will be reviewed and responded to promptly as regulated in the ESCP,
      SEP, ESMP, RP, and LMP.
    o Regarding comments from national-level stakeholders, an email and/or official letter will be
      sent after workshops on how comments/suggestions were taken into account and/or to inform
      them of documents that are publicly available.
112. In addition, project documents, including monthly, quarterly, or annual reports will be
updated and uploaded as needed, including this SEP, and available on the subproject website at: [tbc].
During consultation activities, stakeholders will also be reminded that these documents are publicly
available, as well as the project’s GRM for lodging their concerns, grievances, and complaints related
to the project implementation, including the implementation of E&S risk and impact management
and mitigation measures via public meeting, consultation and feedback reports.

 STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

Translated Titles
                                    Page 83
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

                        THE SOCIALIST REPUBLIC OF VIETNAM
                              Independence – Freedom – Happiness


                                     MEETING MINUTES
      Community Consultation Meeting with Directly Affected Households on Environmental
                              and Social Impacts of the Project
     Subproject: “Sustainable Fisheries Development Project in Thanh Hoa Province, financed by
the World Bank (WB)”.
      Date: November 13, 2024
     Location: Meeting Hall of Da Loc Commune People's Committee, Hau Loc District, Thanh
Hoa Province.
      1. Participants
     1.1. Representatives of the Commune People's Committee (CPC) where the project is
implemented.
      Mr. Bui Van Thai – Vice Chairman of the Commune People's Committee
      Mr. Bui Van Duan – Vice Chairman of the Commune Fatherland Front Committee
      Ms. Tran Thi Tuyen – Land Administration Officer
      1.2. Representative of the Project Owner
      Mr. Nguyen Dang Sinh – Technical Officer
      1.3. Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) Consulting Team
      Ms. Nguyen Thi Thuy – Lead Consultant
      Mr. Le Duc Huy – Social Specialist
      Ms. Nguyen Thi Mai Hoa – Environmental Specialist
      1.4. Community Participants: Representatives of local residents and individuals directly
affected by the project (List of participants is attached to this meeting minutes).
      2. Consultation Content and Summary of Community Feedback
      2.1. Content:
      (i) Introduction and dissemination of information about the project, and presentation of a
summary of the environmental and social impacts on households affected by the “Sustainable
Fisheries Development Project in Thanh Hoa Province, funded by the World Bank.”
     (ii) Consultation with affected households regarding their opinions and expectations on
compensation, support, resettlement, gender issues, other vulnerable groups, and discussion of
income restoration support measures for affected people.
      (iii) Consultation with affected households on the project’s environmental impacts, including
both positive and negative effects on the environment and community health, and discussion of
environmental mitigation measures.
     (iv) Discussion and exchange between communities directly affected by the project and
commune-level People's Committees regarding the environmental and social impacts of the
“Sustainable Fisheries Development Project in Thanh Hoa Province, funded by the World Bank.
2.2    Summary of Consultation Results and Feedback from Participants
• Mr. Bui Van Thai, Vice chairman of Commune People's Committee
                                                                                            Page 84
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

- The local authority and people highly support the investment policy and construction of the project.
  When the project is completed, it will improve the environment, increase crop productivity and
  ensure water resources for aquaculture.
- The project design location is close to the residential area and the pond area of the household, when
  implementing construction, it will affect the aquaculture process of the households. Therefore,
  when implementing construction, the contractor needs to take construction measures to minimize
  the use of soil, rock, and wastewater into aquaculture ponds of people.
- The regulation of water in and out of aquaculture and agricultural production areas is very
  important, so it is necessary to pay attention, calculate and coordinate with the locality in the
  regulation of water for construction of the contractor.
- The construction area is located in the aquaculture area, so it is very sensitive to the environment,
  construction waste, machinery, grease, etc. It is recommended that the contractor must regularly
  collect waste and limit the amount of grease and oil spilled into the pond.
- During construction, there will be dust and noise directly affecting households living along the
  canal route, especially the route for transporting dredged soil from riverbeds. The Contractor needs
  to regularly clean the roads and spray water to reduce dust regularly.
• Mr. To Xuan Thach_Yen Loc Village
- We fully support and agree with the project. The project helps localities sustainably develop
  aquaculture areas as well as increase the value of agricultural production.
- In the area of the canal from Quay Co culvert to Ong Hong culvert, there are 7 aquaculture areas
  that have been planned, currently there are 7 water inles. When constructing the operation
  management road, it will affect these 7 culverts. It is recommended that the project will restore to
  the people.
- It is recommended to compensate for affected crops on the corridor.
- It is recommended to construct from November to February because this is the time when people
  does not do aquaculture.
- If during the construction process, there is a status of livestock in the pond died due to the
  contractor's construction activities, it is requested the project to compensate for people.
• Mr. Vu Xuan Trung, Yen Loc Village
- Currently, in the aquaculture area, it does not have a route for people to travel to their ponds,
  travelling is difficult, people have to make temporary bridge to travel. So when the project invests
  in constructing management and operation road, people are very excited.
- Currently, there are many works in the village area; however, the construction quality is not good,
  leading to the status that new works are degraded for a short time. It is recommended that when
  investing in construction, it should closely monitor the construction process to ensure the most
  effective quality of the works.
3. Conclusion
- The government and people agree to support the policy of investment and construction of the
  project
- Calculate and coordinate with localities in regulating water for construction of the contractor when
  dredging the canal.
- The Contractor must regularly collect waste and limit the amount of grease and oil spilled into the
  pond. It must ensure environmental sanitation in the construction site.
                                                                                               Page 85
STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT PLAN (SEP)

The meeting minutes were concluded on the same day and unanimously agreed upon by all
participants.
